title: Pre-flight creditworthiness proof before x402 service negotiation
description: Before an autonomous Theseus agent negotiates an x402 service contract, the counterparty needs proof the agent has spending authority — without the agent revealing its wallet balance, full delegation chain, or operator identity. Bolyra's AgentPolicy circuit already encodes FINANCIAL_SMALL/MEDIUM/UNLIMITED permission bits (2-4) in its 8-bit cumulative model; a ZKP can attest 'this agent is authorized to spend up to $10K' without leaking anything else. This is economically critical because x402 is now processing $600M annualized volume on Base and Solana, and Theseus agents have no human fallback to approve individual transactions — spending authority must be cryptographically self-certifying. Turnkey and Privy offer wallets but have no authorization proof layer; Bolyra's circuit is the only ready primitive.

rationale.agent_need: Theseus's no-human-key-path architecture makes self-certifying spending authority structurally necessary, and x402's $600M annualized volume on Base provides concrete demand evidence, though Theseus-specific documentation on their identity approach remains thin.
rationale.zkp_edge: The non-ZKP alternatives (Turnkey MPC, Privy session keys, Lit PKPs, W3C VCs) are correctly identified and their failure mode is structural — they reveal operator identity or scope to the counterparty or require trusting a third-party network — but the privacy sensitivity of revealing financial tier (SMALL/MEDIUM/UNLIMITED) versus full balance is a meaningful but not overwhelming gap.
rationale.primitive_readiness: AgentPolicy circuit maps directly to requiredScopeMask selective disclosure with no circuit changes, Delegation circuit covers sub-agent chains, and the integration spec credibly scopes deliverables to SDK call-path narrowing plus a stub promotion — timeline is aggressive but plausible given no new circuit work.
rationale.partnership_leverage: Theseus could plausibly adopt Lit Protocol or build a simple ACL-based spending attestation without ZKP in under 3 months, and their crypto team background is unassessed; the value proposition is real but not clearly a capability gap that only Bolyra can fill.

evidence.summary: Theseus is an agent-native L1 with no human-controlled key path, meaning agents hold their own keys and need self-certifying spending authority — exactly the gap Bolyra's AgentPolicy circuit fills.

evidence.source: x402.org + AWS blog on x402 and agentic commerce (provided signal URLs)
evidence.summary: x402 is processing ~$600M annualized volume on Base and Solana with no native spending-authority proof layer, creating a hard dependency on a pre-flight credential primitive before autonomous agent negotiation.
Turnkey provides programmable MPC wallets for agents but has no ZKP authorization-proof layer; it can sign transactions but cannot attest spending scope without revealing the full policy.
Privy targets consumer embedded wallets and offers session keys for agents, but session key scope is checked server-side with no cryptographic attestation available to a third-party counterparty.

evidence.source: Lit Protocol — decentralized key management + PKP
evidence.summary: Lit Protocol's Programmable Key Pairs (PKPs) can gate signing on arbitrary conditions, but conditions execute in Lit nodes and produce an ECDSA signature — no ZKP proof-of-scope that the counterparty can verify without trusting Lit's network.

evidence.source: W3C Verifiable Credentials + DID-based authorization (standards track)
evidence.summary: W3C VCs provide a delegation/credential format but are bearer tokens — they reveal all claims (operator identity, scope) to the verifier and offer no zero-knowledge selective disclosure for financial limits.
IETF discussions on AI agent authentication converge on OAuth 2.0-style delegation with no cryptographic spending-limit attestation, leaving a clear gap for ZKP-based pre-flight proofs.

evidence.source: Fetch.ai / ASI Alliance — autonomous economic agents on Cosmos
evidence.summary: Fetch.ai agents use registration-based trust (AgentAddress + capability manifests) rather than cryptographic spending-authority proofs; no ZKP primitive for financial authorization scope.
SingularityNET uses AGIX payment escrow for service negotiation, not cryptographic pre-flight authorization; scope of agent spending is opaque to counterparties.

evidence.source: Semaphore v4 (Ethereum Foundation) — ZKP group membership
evidence.summary: Semaphore proves group membership anonymously but has no financial-scope encoding; Bolyra's AgentPolicy circuit extends this primitive with 8-bit cumulative permission bits, a concrete differentiation.

evidence.source: Polygon ID / Iden3 — ZKP credential framework
evidence.summary: Polygon ID offers ZKP-based credential attestation for humans but is not purpose-built for agent spending authority or delegation chaining; no x402 integration or financial-bit encoding.

evidence.source: Base chain x402 adoption signals (Coinbase/AWS partnerships, 2026)
evidence.summary: AWS published a blog endorsing x402 for agentic commerce on Base, signaling institutional momentum and making pre-flight spending-proof infrastructure commercially urgent in H2 2026.

mapped_primitives.usage: Core primitive. The selective-disclosure capability — 'proves requiredScopeMask bits are set without revealing full bitmask' — maps directly to the x402 preflight requirement: prove FINANCIAL_MEDIUM (bit 3, implying bit 2) is set without revealing operator identity, wallet balance, or other permission bits. The identity-bound scope commitment and expiry enforcement give the counterparty a time-bounded, tamper-evident authorization attestation. No circuit changes required.

Theseus sub-agents receiving delegated spending authority from a human or parent agent use the Delegation circuit to prove the delegated scope includes FINANCIAL_MEDIUM/UNLIMITED without revealing the full delegation chain. The scope-narrowing guarantee (delegateeScope ⊆ delegatorScope) is exactly the property x402 counterparties need — they know the agent cannot self-escalate beyond what was granted. The existing max-3-hop enforcement bounds chain exposure.

On-chain verification anchor on Base — the x402 counterparty (or a relayer) calls the PlonkVerifier directly, or the atomic handshake endpoint, to verify the AgentPolicy proof against the current agentTree root. Root history buffers (30 entries) provide liveness tolerance so proofs generated seconds before the x402 negotiation window remain valid even if a new agent is enrolled mid-flight.

The existing integrations/payment-protocols/ stub is the direct home for the x402 adapter layer. It needs to be promoted from stub to a thin SDK wrapper that: (1) translates a spending threshold (e.g. $10K) into the requiredScopeMask bitmask, (2) calls proveHandshake or a standalone AgentPolicy prover, and (3) serializes the proof + vkey fingerprint into the x402 Authorization header format.

createAgentCredential already accepts permissions (→ bitmask) and expiry. The Theseus agent calls this at credential issuance time. At x402 negotiation time, it calls a new proveSpendingAuthority() thin wrapper (internally an AgentPolicy proof with requiredScopeMask set to the financial bits) instead of the full mutual-auth proveHandshake. No new circuit invocation — just a narrowed call path.

new_components_needed.description: proveSpendingAuthority(credential, spendingCapUSD) → {proof, vkeyFingerprint, scopeCommitment, expiry} — thin wrapper over AgentPolicy prover that translates a USD threshold to the correct financial bitmask (FINANCIAL_SMALL < $100, FINANCIAL_MEDIUM < $10K, FINANCIAL_UNLIMITED) and generates a standalone AgentPolicy proof. Does not require a paired HumanUniqueness proof, so it is lighter than proveHandshake. Exposed from @bolyra/sdk v0.3.

x402 preflight adapter — promotion of integrations/payment-protocols/ stub. Serializes the SpendingAuthority proof into the x402 Authorization header (base64url proof blob + on-chain verifier address + chainId). Includes a counterparty-side verify function that resolves the PlonkVerifier address from chainId and calls verifyProof. Ships as @bolyra/x402 package.

X402SpendingAuthorityVerifier — a thin Solidity wrapper (deployable on Base mainnet and any x402-active chain) that exposes verifySpendingAuthority(proof, requiredScopeMask, maxExpiry) as a pure view function. Internally delegates to the existing PlonkVerifier. Counterparties import this as a composable auth check in their own x402 payment contracts without needing to understand the full IdentityRegistry.

x402-bolyra-auth profile: a one-page extension to draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 specifying how the SpendingAuthority proof maps to x402 header fields (Authorization: Bolyra proof=<b64>, verifier=<addr>, chain=<id>, scope=<hex>, expiry=<unix>). Needed so Theseus and counterparty implementors have an interop target without relying on SDK internals.

theseus_integration_surface: Three connection points: (1) Theseus agent runtime embeds @bolyra/sdk and calls proveSpendingAuthority() before each x402 negotiation — the proof is attached as an Authorization header to the payment request. (2) The x402 counterparty (merchant service or settlement contract) calls X402SpendingAuthorityVerifier.verifySpendingAuthority() on Base — this is a view call, gas-free if done off-chain via eth_call, or on-chain for settlement finality. (3) For Theseus's own chain (theseuschain.com), a cross-chain root relay is needed: IdentityRegistry's agentTree root gets posted to Theseus via a lightweight oracle or storage proof, so the X402SpendingAuthorityVerifier deployed on Theseus can validate against a recent-enough root without a Base round-trip. The root history buffer (30 entries) gives a ~5–10 minute freshness window assuming ~12s block times, which is sufficient for x402 negotiation latency.

deliverables.name: proveSpendingAuthority()
deliverables.description: Thin wrapper in @bolyra/sdk over the existing AgentPolicy prover. Accepts (credential, spendingCapUSD) and internally maps the USD threshold to the correct FINANCIAL_* bitmask (FINANCIAL_SMALL=bit2, FINANCIAL_MEDIUM=bits3+2, FINANCIAL_UNLIMITED=bits4+3+2), then calls the AgentPolicy witness generator with requiredScopeMask set to only those financial bits. Returns {proof, vkeyFingerprint, scopeCommitment, expiry}. No new circuit — pure call-path narrowing.

deliverables.name: x402AuthorizationHeader serializer
deliverables.description: Promotes integrations/payment-protocols/ stub to a working adapter. Takes the proveSpendingAuthority() output and serializes it into an x402-compatible Authorization header: base64(proof_bytes) + vkeyFingerprint + scopeCommitment + expiry as structured fields. Also implements the verifier side: counterparty calls verifySpendingAuthority(header, requiredCapUSD) which deserializes, checks expiry, and calls the PlonkVerifier on Base Sepolia (or snarkjs locally for demo). No new circuit invocation.

deliverables.name: Sunday demo script
deliverables.description: Single Node.js script (examples/theseus-x402-demo/demo.js) that runs end-to-end in fast witness mode (FULL_PROOF=0): (1) creates a Theseus agent credential with FINANCIAL_MEDIUM permission and 24h expiry, (2) calls proveSpendingAuthority(10000), (3) serializes to x402 Authorization header, (4) runs local verifySpendingAuthority() and prints pass/fail with timing. Screen-shareable in under 30 seconds. Separate .env.example documents what Theseus would need to plug in their agent keypair.

deliverables.name: Delegation chain demo extension
deliverables.description: Extend the Sunday demo to show sub-agent delegation: human operator delegates FINANCIAL_MEDIUM to a Theseus agent via the Delegation circuit (fast witness mode), agent then proves spending authority without revealing the human's identity or the full chain. Demonstrates the scope-narrowing guarantee (delegateeScope ⊆ delegatorScope) — the key differentiator vs Turnkey/Privy/Lit.
deliverables.reuses: Delegation circuit (fast witness) + existing proveHandshake path

deliverables.name: Bolyra x Theseus integration spec (1-pager)
deliverables.description: Single markdown doc (docs/theseus-integration.md) covering: the 5-step x402 preflight flow diagram, which Bolyra primitives map to which Theseus needs, the Authorization header wire format, Base Sepolia contract addresses for PlonkVerifier, and the open question list for the Sunday call (Theseus's current auth approach, agent keypair format, mainnet timeline, EVM compatibility). This is the leave-behind and also prep material.

spec.demo_description: Screen-share a running Node.js script showing: (1) agent credential created with FINANCIAL_MEDIUM bit, (2) proveSpendingAuthority($10K) completes in ~200ms fast-witness mode, (3) x402 Authorization header printed, (4) verifySpendingAuthority() returns true with scope commitment. Then replay with a delegated sub-agent to show chain. Concrete contrast: Turnkey signs the tx but can't prove scope without revealing policy; Privy checks server-side with no third-party-verifiable attestation; Lit requires trusting their node network. Bolyra produces a self-contained ZKP the Theseus counterparty verifies directly. The demo does NOT require Theseus to provide anything — it runs entirely on Bolyra's existing test fixtures and Base Sepolia.
risks: Fast-witness mode (FULL_PROOF=0) is the only feasible Sunday path — full Groth16 takes ~2min per proof, which kills live demo pacing. Must set expectation that production latency with rapidsnark is ~800ms, not 200ms.

x402 Authorization header format is not yet standardized — the serialization spec is Bolyra-proposed, not x402.org canonical. Theseus (and the x402 counterparty) would need to adopt Bolyra's format or negotiate a joint spec, which is a partnership dependency, not a technical one.

Theseus's agent keypair format is unknown. If they use something other than EdDSA/Baby Jubjub, the AgentPolicy circuit's credentialHash computation needs an adapter shim — still no circuit change, but an extra SDK day.
If Theseus is not EVM-compatible, the on-chain PlonkVerifier anchor is unavailable. Demo falls back to off-chain snarkjs verification, which weakens the trust model. Must ask on Sunday.

Solo founder: 2.5 days is tight before Sunday. The delegation extension (0.5 days) is the cut candidate if time pressure hits — the core proveSpendingAuthority demo is sufficient to validate the concept.
spec.success_criteria: The Sunday demo script runs without error, verifySpendingAuthority() returns true for a valid FINANCIAL_MEDIUM credential and false for a credential with only READ_DATA bits set, and the Theseus team confirms their agents produce EdDSA-compatible keypairs that can plug into createAgentCredential() with no format shim needed.
spec.theseus_dependencies: Nothing required before Sunday — demo runs on Bolyra fixtures. For production integration: (1) Theseus agent keypair format + signing curve (need EdDSA/Baby Jubjub or a documented adapter); (2) their chain's EVM compatibility status so we know whether PlonkVerifier deploys natively or needs a bridge; (3) a testnet RPC endpoint so the x402 counterparty can call verifySpendingAuthority on-chain; (4) confirmation of which x402 counterparties they're targeting first (helps prioritize the Authorization header format negotiation).

tier3_findings: AXIS 1 — CONCERN: A simple on-chain spending-tier registry (agent self-registers as FINANCIAL_SMALL/MEDIUM/UNLIMITED at enrollment, counterparty does a single contract read) achieves 80% of the described value in under 2 weeks with no ZKP expertise. Theseus engineers could build this with Solidity + OpenZeppelin. The proposal never seriously engages with why the counterparty cannot just read a public on-chain ACL entry, which is the most obvious objection.

AXIS 2 — CONCERN: The privacy claim is 'hide operator identity and full permission bitmask from the x402 counterparty.' But in B2B agent commerce at the Theseus scale ($100K funded, few launch agents), counterparties are unlikely to demand the agent prove scope without revealing operator. Most x402 integrations today resolve authorization through simple payment success/failure or pre-signed allowances — there is no documented market demand for ZKP-level spending-scope privacy in the x402 stack specifically. The proposal asserts the privacy value but does not demonstrate that revealing financial tier (SMALL vs MEDIUM vs UNLIMITED) causes a concrete harm or competitive disadvantage.

AXIS 3 — CONCERN: No specific Theseus agent is named, no documented Theseus API exists for x402 preflight, and Theseus has not launched. The x402 $600M volume figure belongs to Base broadly, not Theseus agents specifically. At Theseus launch (likely <100 agents), the preflight ZKP proof is engineering overhead before product-market fit exists. This is a real future need but not a Day 1 requirement.

AXIS 4 — FAIL: Theseus's on-chain execution environment is unknown — the open questions explicitly flag whether it is EVM-compatible or a custom VM. If non-EVM, deploying a Solidity Groth16 verifier is blocked entirely and requires a custom verifier implementation. Even if EVM, Groth16 on-chain verification costs ~200K–400K gas per proof, which may be economically prohibitive for routine x402 preflight checks on a nascent chain. The proving side is also unresolved: agents running on Theseus would need access to snarkjs or rapidsnark; if Bolyra hosts a proving service, it reintroduces a trust assumption that ZKP is meant to eliminate. The proposal does not address any of this.

AXIS 5 — PASS: The x402 preflight proof concept is chain-agnostic in intent. If Theseus fails, the AgentPolicy circuit financial-bit selective disclosure and the x402 integration SDK module are directly reusable on Base, Solana (if a verifier is ported), or any other agent platform adopting x402. The work is not Theseus-specific.
tier3_summary: The ZKP value proposition for x402 preflight is real but undefended against simpler on-chain ACL alternatives, and the unknown Theseus VM architecture is a hard blocker that must be resolved before any integration work begins — demote from PROMOTE to CONSIDER pending EVM confirmation and a concrete Theseus launch agent use case.

Theseus engineers could build this with Solidity + OpenZeppelin. The proposal never seriously engages with why the counterparty cannot just read a public on-chain ACL entry, which is the most obvious objection.

AXIS 2 — CONCERN: The privacy claim is 'hide operator identity and full permission bitmask from the x402 counterparty.' The proving side is also unresolved: agents running on Theseus would need access to snarkjs or rapidsnark; if Bolyra hosts a proving service, it reintroduces a trust assumption that ZKP is meant to eliminate. The proposal does not address any of this.

The work is not Theseus-specific.

title: Selective Disclosure of Agent Spending Authority for DeFi and API Access
description: When Agent A needs to access a DeFi protocol or paid API on Theseus (or via Base MCP), the counterparty needs to verify Agent A has spending authority up to $X — but revealing the full permission set (all 8 bits) leaks operational intelligence about the agent's operator. Bolyra's AgentPolicy circuit supports selective disclosure: Agent A proves it holds permission bit 2 (FINANCIAL_SMALL < $100) without revealing bits 5-7 (SIGN_ON_BEHALF, SUB_DELEGATE, ACCESS_PII). This matters specifically for agent-native chains because agents transact with other autonomous agents that have no human judgment to assess revealed metadata — everything revealed on-chain is permanently exploitable by adversarial agents.

rationale.agent_need: Theseus's no-human-key-path architecture with raw EdDSA keypairs is confirmed, and Base MCP/Coinbase agentic wallets evidence shows agents actively transacting DeFi with no permission-privacy layer — the need is structural, not hypothetical.
rationale.zkp_edge: The non-ZKP alternatives (Lit Protocol MPC, Turnkey TEE, Privy centralized policies) all expose spending authority in plaintext to counterparties or network nodes, whereas AgentPolicy's requiredScopeMask enables cryptographic selective disclosure of only the needed bit — the failure of alternatives is structural (on-chain visibility), not merely stylistic.
rationale.primitive_readiness: AgentPolicy circuit already supports requiredScopeMask selective disclosure, PlonkVerifier is deployed on Base Sepolia, and the integration spec correctly identifies only a 1-day SDK wrapper and 1-day thin Solidity adapter are needed — no new circuit development required.
rationale.partnership_leverage: The expertise gap argument (ZKP circuit design, Circom, trusted setup) is real but weakened by the fact that Theseus could plausibly adopt Lit Protocol or Turnkey as drop-in alternatives without ZKP expertise, and there is no evidence Theseus has engaged Bolyra or is blocked on this problem today.

evidence.summary: Theseus is a $100K Orange DAO-funded L1 designed for agent-first execution with no human key path, meaning raw EdDSA keypairs are the likely current identity primitive — no selective disclosure capability exists natively.

evidence.source: Coinbase Developer Platform — Agentic Wallets launch
evidence.summary: Coinbase's agentic wallets give agents custodied keys with spending limits but no ZKP-based selective disclosure — operator policies are stored in plaintext and visible to counterparties.
Base MCP enables agents to call DeFi protocols on-chain but bundles no permission-privacy layer — agents expose their full capability set when authenticating to protocols.

evidence.source: Lit Protocol (litprotocol.com) — PKP and conditional signing
evidence.summary: Lit Protocol's Programmable Key Pairs support access conditions for agents but rely on threshold MPC with on-chain condition evaluation — no ZKP, so conditions themselves are visible to the network.
Turnkey provides agent wallet infrastructure with policy enforcement via TEE attestation, but spending authority is declared in plaintext policy configs — no selective disclosure to counterparties.

evidence.source: Privy (privy.io) — server wallets and agent policies
evidence.summary: Privy's server wallets support policy-gated agent transactions but policies are centralized and visible to Privy infrastructure — no cryptographic privacy guarantee for permission metadata.

evidence.source: IETF draft-klrc-aiagent (AI Agent Authorization) — referenced in bolyra-bookmarks skill
evidence.summary: Emerging IETF draft for AI agent authorization uses OAuth 2.0 token scopes — no selective disclosure primitive, scopes are fully visible in the token, creating the exact metadata leakage problem Bolyra solves.

evidence.source: W3C Verifiable Credentials + Selective Disclosure JWT (SD-JWT)
evidence.summary: SD-JWT provides selective disclosure for VCs but requires trusted issuer infrastructure and does not produce on-chain verifiable ZK proofs — unsuitable for trustless agent-to-agent verification on Theseus.

evidence.source: Fetch.ai / Agentverse — agent identity and capability advertising
evidence.summary: Fetch.ai uses DID-based agent identity with capability manifests but publishes full capability sets to its registry — adversarial agents can enumerate operator intelligence from the manifest.

evidence.source: SingularityNET — agent service metadata on Cardano
evidence.summary: SingularityNET publishes agent service metadata on-chain with no privacy layer — full service capabilities visible, similar leakage problem but not EVM-compatible.

evidence.source: Semaphore v4 (Ethereum Foundation PSE) — on-chain ZKP identity
evidence.summary: Semaphore v4 is the closest deployed ZKP identity primitive on EVM chains but targets human uniqueness proofs only — no agent permission bit selective disclosure capability.

evidence.source: Polygon ID / Iden3 — ZKP verifiable credentials on EVM
evidence.summary: Polygon ID supports selective disclosure of credential attributes on EVM chains but is designed for human DIDs and requires centralized issuer registry — not agent-native.
Aztec provides general-purpose private state on EVM via ZKP but requires porting to Aztec's own VM — agents on Theseus/Base cannot use it natively without bridging.

mapped_primitives.usage: Core selective disclosure engine. The requiredScopeMask input already lets an agent prove FINANCIAL_SMALL (bit 2) is set while keeping bits 5–7 (SIGN_ON_BEHALF, SUB_DELEGATE, ACCESS_PII) hidden. The identity-bound scope commitment (Poseidon3) ensures the partial disclosure cannot be replayed with a different full bitmask. This is the exact circuit capability the opportunity requires — no architectural change needed.

Already deployed on Base Sepolia (0x28b99c48f19F1680a7D88733ffeb9AFbcB3B95Db). Theseus contracts or Base MCP gateway can call this verifier directly to check spending-authority proofs submitted by agents before executing DeFi transactions. No redeployment required.

Root history buffers (30-entry agentTree) provide staleness tolerance for async DeFi calls. The atomic handshake path is heavier than needed for pure spending-authority checks; DeFi protocols should call PlonkVerifier directly rather than go through verifyHandshake. IdentityRegistry's agentTree root history is still needed to anchor the Merkle inclusion proof inside AgentPolicy.

Handles the sub-agent case: if Agent A received FINANCIAL_SMALL authority via a human→agent delegation, the Delegation circuit proves the narrowed scope commitment chain is intact before AgentPolicy proves selective disclosure of the delegated bit. The one-way scope narrowing (delegateeScope ⊆ delegatorScope) prevents a sub-agent from falsely claiming higher spending tiers.

createAgentCredential sets requiredScopeMask to 0x04 (bit 2 only) when calling proveHandshake with selective-disclosure intent. The existing permissionsToBitmask and validateCumulativeBitEncoding helpers already enforce implication rules. A thin proveSpendingAuthority wrapper over proveHandshake would give DeFi integrators a one-call API without exposing the full handshake ceremony.

The integrations/payment-protocols directory is the natural home for a Base MCP gateway adapter — it bridges Bolyra proof submission to Coinbase's agentic-wallet transaction signing flow, acting as the relay between off-chain proof generation and on-chain DeFi authorization.

new_components_needed.description: SpendingAuthorityVerifier — a lightweight Solidity contract deployable on Theseus (or as a Base Sepolia reference) that wraps PlonkVerifier with a single checkSpendingAuthority(proof, publicSignals, requiredBit) view function. DeFi protocols and Base MCP gateway call this instead of the full IdentityRegistry handshake, eliminating the Groth16 co-proof requirement for pure agent authorization flows.

proveSpendingAuthority(credential, requiredBit, agentTreeRoot, currentTimestamp) — a focused SDK function that calls AgentPolicy under the hood with requiredScopeMask = (1 << requiredBit) and returns a compact {proof, publicSignals} payload shaped for SpendingAuthorityVerifier. Removes handshake ceremony overhead for DeFi-only flows.

Base MCP spending-authority middleware — a Node.js middleware for the Base MCP server that intercepts agent tool calls tagged with financial scope, calls proveSpendingAuthority off-chain, and attaches the proof as a transaction calldata prefix before forwarding to the DeFi protocol. Handles proof caching keyed on (credentialCommitment, agentTreeRoot, requiredBit) with TTL = block finality window.

Spending-authority proof envelope spec — a short addendum to draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 defining the wire format for standalone AgentPolicy proofs (no HumanUniqueness co-proof), the requiredBit encoding convention, and the staleness tolerance window for agentTree root history. Needed for Theseus and Base MCP to standardize on a single proof format.

theseus_integration_surface: Three connection points: (1) Contract layer — SpendingAuthorityVerifier deployed on Theseus (or as a verified Base Sepolia contract that Theseus bridges to), called by DeFi protocols as a pre-flight check before processing agent-initiated transactions; PlonkVerifier at 0x28b99c48f19F1680a7D88733ffeb9AFbcB3B95Db is already available on Base Sepolia and can be reused if Theseus is Base-compatible. (2) SDK bridge — @bolyra/sdk proveSpendingAuthority generates the PLONK proof off-chain inside the agent runtime; the proof + publicSignals are attached as calldata to the agent's DeFi transaction. (3) Base MCP middleware — the spending-authority middleware intercepts financial tool calls in the Base MCP server, invokes proveSpendingAuthority, and forwards proof-annotated transactions to Coinbase's agentic-wallet signing endpoint. This keeps proof generation off-chain (fast, no gas) while putting verification on-chain (trustless, permanent, adversary-resistant).

deliverables.name: proveSpendingAuthority SDK wrapper
deliverables.description: Single-call wrapper over existing proveHandshake that sets requiredScopeMask=0x04 (FINANCIAL_SMALL) and strips unrevealed bits from the public outputs. DeFi integrators call proveSpendingAuthority(agentCredential, maxAmount) and get back a proof + public signals with no leakage of bits 5-7.

deliverables.name: SpendingAuthorityVerifier.sol
deliverables.description: Thin Solidity wrapper around the already-deployed PlonkVerifier (0x28b99c48f19F1680a7D88733ffeb9AFbcB3B95Db on Base Sepolia). Exposes a single verifySpendingAuthority(proof, agentMerkleRoot, scopeMask, nonce) view function that DeFi protocols and Base MCP gateways can call before executing transactions. Emits SpendingAuthorityVerified event for on-chain auditability.

deliverables.name: Sunday demo script
deliverables.description: End-to-end CLI demo: (1) createAgentCredential with full 8-bit mask, (2) call proveSpendingAuthority to generate selective-disclosure proof revealing only bit 2, (3) submit to SpendingAuthorityVerifier on Base Sepolia and show tx receipt, (4) attempt replay with wrong mask and show revert. All runnable in under 60 seconds from a terminal — no browser required.
deliverables.reuses: AgentPolicy circuit (build artifacts) + PlonkVerifier + IdentityRegistry agentTree

deliverables.name: Base MCP gateway adapter
deliverables.description: Express middleware in integrations/payment-protocols that intercepts Base MCP tool-call auth headers, calls SpendingAuthorityVerifier.sol to validate the agent's spending proof, and forwards or rejects the DeFi transaction. Coinbase agentic-wallet flow: agent generates proof off-chain, middleware validates on-chain, wallet executes if valid. This is the only net-new component with no existing Bolyra analog.
deliverables.reuses: new — wraps SpendingAuthorityVerifier + Coinbase AgentKit SDK

deliverables.name: Theseus integration spec
deliverables.description: One-page technical spec for the Theseus team: how an agent running on Theseus L1 generates a Bolyra spending-authority proof off-chain (or via Base MCP bridge), submits it to SpendingAuthorityVerifier before any agent-to-agent transaction, and how the Theseus VM can cache verified proofs to avoid redundant on-chain verification per session. Includes wire format, gas estimate (<50k on Base), and Delegation circuit path for sub-agents.

spec.demo_description: Live terminal demo: generate an AgentPolicy proof with requiredScopeMask=0x04 (FINANCIAL_SMALL only, hiding SIGN_ON_BEHALF/SUB_DELEGATE/ACCESS_PII), verify it against the already-deployed PlonkVerifier on Base Sepolia, show the on-chain tx confirmation. Then show a failed attempt where an agent tries to claim FINANCIAL_MEDIUM (bit 3) and the verifier reverts. No new infrastructure needed — everything runs against existing Base Sepolia deployment. If Theseus has a testnet endpoint, swap the RPC and show the same flow against their chain.
risks: Theseus VM is not EVM-compatible: PlonkVerifier reuse breaks, requires porting the Groth16/PLONK verifier to their native execution environment — adds 2-3 weeks
Theseus has no testnet yet: demo stays on Base Sepolia and the Theseus story is forward-looking rather than live
Base MCP auth header format changes between now and mainnet: gateway adapter needs version pinning
Sunday call is tomorrow — proveSpendingAuthority wrapper and demo script must be built today in a single session
spec.success_criteria: An agent on Theseus (or Base MCP) submits a Bolyra spending-authority proof to SpendingAuthorityVerifier.sol, the verifier confirms FINANCIAL_SMALL without any bits above 2 appearing in public signals, and a DeFi transaction executes. A second agent with only READ_DATA (bit 0) is rejected. Both paths verifiable via on-chain tx hashes.
spec.theseus_dependencies: EVM compatibility confirmation (or VM spec if custom), testnet RPC endpoint + faucet, clarification on whether agents hold EdDSA keys natively (needed to map to Bolyra's credentialCommitment input), and any existing agent-to-agent auth interface we need to slot into rather than replace

tier3_findings: AXIS 1 (Theseus Builds It): PASS. Selective disclosure of a subset of bits from a committed bitmask — while keeping the rest cryptographically hidden AND producing an on-chain-verifiable proof — genuinely requires Circom/ZKP expertise. The naive alternatives (e.g., separate EdDSA signatures per permission level) break the identity-binding guarantee: a rogue operator can issue a FINANCIAL_SMALL credential for an agent that doesn't hold it, with no on-chain enforcement that the disclosed bit is drawn from the same committed bitmask the agent enrolled with. Theseus cannot replicate this in 3 months without ZKP expertise.

AXIS 2 (Standard Crypto Suffices): CONCERN. The strongest non-ZKP alternative is a per-permission credential model: the operator signs 'this agent has FINANCIAL_SMALL' as a standalone EdDSA-signed VC, never including higher bits in that credential. This achieves selective disclosure. The ZKP's marginal advantage is (a) the scope commitment binding the disclosed bit to a specific enrolled identity without a central issuer lookup, and (b) on-chain verifiability without trusting the operator's honesty. However, SD-JWT + operator signature achieves (b) off-chain, and for DeFi protocols that already trust the operator's signing key, (a) may be unnecessary. The failure of standard crypto is real but only if Theseus specifically requires trustless on-chain verification without issuer trust — an assumption not yet confirmed.

AXIS 3 (No Agent Needs This): CONCERN. Theseus has $100K in funding and is pre-mainnet. The adversarial agent threat — autonomous counterparty agents mining operator intelligence from exposed permission bits — is structurally real but practically vacuous at Theseus's current scale. No specific named agent use case on Theseus is operating at a volume where metadata leakage across thousands of transactions is an exploitable signal. The first 12 months of Theseus will have manual/trusted operator relationships dominating; the selective disclosure value arrives at thousands of autonomous agents, not at launch.

AXIS 4 (Integration Complexity vs Value): CONCERN. The integration card assumes EVM compatibility for the PlonkVerifier on Base Sepolia, but Theseus's execution environment is explicitly listed as an open question. If Theseus runs a custom non-EVM VM, the 'deployed verifier' is useless, and integration jumps from a 2-day SDK wrapper to a multi-week custom verifier port. The '2-day sprint' estimate is not credible without confirming EVM compatibility in the Sunday call. This is the highest-risk axis and must be resolved before any sprint commitment.

AXIS 5 (Single-Partner Dependency): PASS. The AgentPolicy circuit with requiredScopeMask selective disclosure is chain-agnostic in design. The SDK wrapper, Solidity adapter, and circuit modification (if any) would serve any agent-native EVM chain — Autonomys, Fetch.ai EVM compatibility layer, or future Base-native agent platforms. The work is not Theseus-specific and directly advances Bolyra's protocol regardless of Theseus's outcome.
tier3_summary: The selective disclosure capability is cryptographically real and circuit-ready, but three unresolved concerns — standard per-permission VCs as a simpler substitute, Theseus's pre-mainnet scale making the threat model premature, and EVM compatibility being unconfirmed — prevent full approval; demote from PROMOTE to CONDITIONAL pending Sunday call resolution of the EVM question and a named launch-day agent use case.

title: Autonomous Credential Expiry and Revocation Without Human Intervention
description: On human-centric systems, a compromised credential triggers a human-initiated revocation flow (call support, rotate keys, notify users). On Theseus, there is no human fallback — if an agent's EdDSA key is compromised, the attacker operates indefinitely unless the system has autonomous expiry enforcement. Bolyra's AgentPolicy circuit encodes expiry as a public signal committed at issuance time; any proof generated after expiry fails on-chain verification without requiring a revocation oracle or human action. Combined with Theseus's on-chain agent persistence model, this creates a self-enforcing credential lifecycle that is strictly better than ACL-based revocation lists (which require a trusted admin with a human key to update).

rationale.agent_need: The 'no human key path' constraint on Theseus makes autonomous credential expiry architecturally necessary — Fetch.ai's same trusted-admin bottleneck confirms this need emerges structurally in agent-native L1s.
rationale.zkp_edge: The non-ZKP alternative (ACL revocation list with trusted admin key) is named and its structural failure mode (requires human key to update) is demonstrated; Lit Protocol's oracle dependency and Turnkey's centralized API are concrete comparisons showing ZKP's advantage.
rationale.primitive_readiness: AgentPolicy circuit's expiryTimestamp public signal and Delegation circuit's cascading expiry are both production-ready with no circuit modifications needed; only a thin Solidity adapter (TheseusCredentialGate.sol) is new work estimated at 2 days.
rationale.partnership_leverage: The argument that Theseus cannot build this in <3 months relies on ZKP expertise gap but is weakened by the fact that Lit Protocol already provides a competing offering and Theseus could potentially adopt it; the specific Circom/trusted-setup gap is asserted but not evidenced against Theseus's actual team composition.

evidence.summary: Theseus is an agent-native L1 with no human key path; agents hold their own keys and persist on-chain, creating an unsolved credential lifecycle problem since human-initiated revocation is architecturally impossible.

Lit offers Programmable Key Pairs (PKPs) with threshold-MPC signing for agents, but relies on off-chain Lit node network for revocation and expiry enforcement — not purely on-chain and requires a trusted oracle layer.

Turnkey provides sub-organization wallets for agents with policy-gated signing, but expiry and revocation are managed via centralized API calls, not verifiable on-chain proofs — single point of failure for autonomous agents.
Privy's embedded wallets target user-facing apps; their server wallets for agents lack on-chain expiry enforcement and still require human admin to rotate or revoke compromised credentials.
Fetch.ai's Autonomous Economic Agents use DID-based identities but delegate revocation to the Fetch ledger's ACL admin — the same trusted-admin bottleneck Bolyra's circuit-encoded expiry eliminates.
SingularityNET uses on-chain service registry but has no ZKP-based expiry primitive; credential lifecycle is handled by service operator keys, not by the agent itself.
W3C DIDs provide a decentralized identity standard but offer no native expiry or revocation primitive — applications must layer ACL lists or OCSP-style oracles on top.
Emerging IETF agent-auth drafts propose DPoP-style proof-of-possession tokens with short TTLs but require a token endpoint (human-operated AS) to re-issue, breaking autonomous agent workflows.

evidence.source: Semaphore v4 / Worldcoin identity — on-chain nullifier pattern
evidence.summary: Semaphore's nullifier commitment pattern (which Bolyra's HumanUniqueness circuit reuses) demonstrates that ZKP public signals can enforce protocol-level constraints on-chain without an admin key — the same principle applies to expiry timestamps in AgentPolicy.

evidence.source: Aztec Protocol — private ZKP state on L1
evidence.summary: Aztec deploys ZKP-verified state transitions on Ethereum L1; their approach to time-bounded notes validates that expiry encoded in circuit public signals is verifiable without oracle calls.

ERC-4337 session keys support expiry via UserOperation validation logic, but expiry enforcement requires a bundler to reject stale ops — not a ZKP commitment, so it can be bypassed by a colluding bundler.

mapped_primitives.usage: Core primitive for this integration. The circuit's expiryTimestamp public signal is committed at issuance via Poseidon3(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment, expiryTimestamp), making expiry tamper-evident and cryptographically enforced. Any AgentPolicy proof submitted after expiryTimestamp fails the on-chain constraint expiryTimestamp > currentTimestamp without requiring a revocation oracle, admin key, or ACL update. Theseus agents would include a valid AgentPolicy proof in every on-chain action; stale credentials are self-invalidating.

Cascading expiry narrowing (delegateeExpiry <= delegatorExpiry) means that if the root credential expires, all downstream delegated sub-agent credentials are simultaneously invalidated — no separate revocation sweep needed. The current-time liveness check in the Delegation circuit enforces this at proof-generation time, not just at registry verification. Theseus's multi-agent task graphs benefit directly: revoking the top-level credential collapses the entire delegation tree atomically.

The agentTree root history buffer (30 entries) provides the Merkle membership anchor for AgentPolicy proofs. On Theseus, agent persistence state lives on-chain; IdentityRegistry's atomic handshake verification and delegation nullifier replay protection slot into Theseus's action settlement layer. The contract's nonce equality enforcement also prevents replayed expired proofs from being accepted during the buffer window.

Not the primary verifier for this opportunity (AgentPolicy uses PLONK), but referenced for HumanUniqueness proofs if Theseus integrates human-in-the-loop authorization gates alongside autonomous agents.

Primary on-chain verifier for AgentPolicy and Delegation proofs. Needs to be deployed to Theseus's chain (or bridged via a cross-chain state proof if Theseus uses its own L1). The existing verifier contract address on Base Sepolia is the reference; a cross-chain deployment is a mechanical lift.

new_components_needed.description: TheseusCredentialGate: a thin Solidity adapter that wraps PlonkVerifier.verifyProof() and exposes a single function `authorizeAction(agentProof, requiredScopeMask, currentTimestamp)` callable by Theseus's on-chain agent execution engine. Handles the currentTimestamp injection (block.timestamp or Theseus oracle) so agents cannot self-report a stale timestamp to bypass expiry. Emits CredentialExpired events for off-chain indexers.

Cross-chain verifier deployment script: deploy DelegationPlonkVerifier + PlonkVerifier to Theseus chain (or Base Sepolia if Theseus settles there). Verification keys are circuit-deterministic — no new trusted setup. Hardhat task with network config for Theseus RPC.

@bolyra/theseus adapter: wraps @bolyra/sdk createAgentCredential + proveHandshake with Theseus-specific credential issuance policy (short TTLs, auto-refresh before expiry threshold, Lit Protocol PKP key rotation hook). Exposes TheseusAgent class with credential lifecycle managed transparently — agent operators set maxTTL and refreshThreshold, adapter handles re-proving before expiry without human intervention.

Theseus Credential Lifecycle Profile appended to draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01: specifies TTL policy recommendations (max 24h for autonomous agents, 1h for financial permissions), refresh window semantics, and how Theseus's on-chain agent persistence model maps to Bolyra's agentTree leaf format. Required for Theseus partnership documentation and any future IETF submission.

Lit Protocol key rotation bridge: when an agent EdDSA key is rotated via Lit PKP, automatically re-issue an AgentCredential with the new operatorKey and matching expiry, then push the new leaf commitment to IdentityRegistry. Closes the gap between cryptographic key rotation (Lit) and credential re-issuance (Bolyra) without human action. Exposed as LitBolyraKeyManager utility class.

theseus_integration_surface: Three connection points: (1) Contract layer — deploy PlonkVerifier + DelegationPlonkVerifier to Theseus chain, wire TheseusCredentialGate as an authorization middleware that Theseus's agent execution runtime calls before settling any on-chain action; expiry check is block.timestamp-native, no oracle. (2) SDK layer — @bolyra/theseus adapter runs inside each Theseus agent process, manages credential TTL and auto-refresh, surfaces TheseusAgent as a drop-in for Theseus's existing agent abstraction; proving happens client-side (16s PLONK, offloadable to rapidsnark for <1s). (3) Lit Protocol bridge — optional but high-value: LitBolyraKeyManager listens for Lit PKP rotation events and triggers createAgentCredential re-issuance + IdentityRegistry leaf update atomically, completing the autonomous key lifecycle loop without any human in the critical path.

deliverables.name: TheseusCredentialGate.sol
deliverables.description: Thin Solidity adapter wrapping PlonkVerifier.verifyProof(). Exposes authorizeAction(agentProof, requiredScopeMask, currentTimestamp) using block.timestamp to prevent self-reported stale timestamps. Emits CredentialExpired on failure. Deploy to Base Sepolia as reference; Theseus ports to their chain or bridges via state proof.

deliverables.name: expiry-demo.js
deliverables.description: End-to-end Node.js script: (1) issue AgentPolicy credential with expiryTimestamp = now+60s, (2) submit valid proof and show authorizeAction succeeds, (3) wait 61s, (4) submit same proof and show on-chain revert without any human action or oracle call. Runs against Base Sepolia with a live TheseusCredentialGate.

deliverables.name: delegation-collapse-demo.js
deliverables.description: Shows cascading expiry: root credential expires → all downstream delegated sub-agent proofs simultaneously fail Delegation circuit's liveness check. Demonstrates atomic revocation of entire delegation tree without admin key or ACL sweep.

deliverables.name: theseus-integration-spec.md
deliverables.description: One-pager for Sunday call: integration architecture diagram, the three attack surfaces Bolyra closes (compromised key, stale delegation tree, replay after expiry), deployment steps for Theseus's chain, and what Theseus needs to provide. Doubles as partnership leave-behind.

deliverables.name: IdentityRegistry nonce guard patch
deliverables.description: Minor tweak to IdentityRegistry: add Theseus-compatible agentTree root history lookup so Theseus's on-chain agent persistence state can anchor AgentPolicy Merkle membership proofs. Change is additive (no ABI break).

spec.demo_description: Live Base Sepolia demo: deploy TheseusCredentialGate, issue a 90-second AgentPolicy credential, run expiry-demo.js showing accepted proof → wait → auto-rejected proof → zero human keystrokes between issuance and revocation. Terminal output only; no UI needed. Optionally show delegation-collapse-demo.js to illustrate the multi-agent task graph benefit. The Sunday call ask: Theseus deploys TheseusCredentialGate.sol to their testnet and hooks authorizeAction() into their agent execution settlement layer.
risks: Theseus chain is not EVM-compatible: TheseusCredentialGate.sol cannot be deployed directly; would need a Circom→native verifier port or a cross-chain state proof bridge — adds 2-3 weeks
block.timestamp on Theseus diverges from wall-clock time (common on early L1s): expiry enforcement becomes unreliable until their oracle is stable
Theseus testnet not accessible before the call: demo stays on Base Sepolia, which weakens the 'Theseus-native' story but keeps Sunday viable
AgentPolicy PLONK verifier gas cost unknown on Theseus's execution environment: if their VM has no precompiles for BN254 pairings, proof verification may be cost-prohibitive
Solo capacity: all 5 deliverables must land before Sunday call — if TheseusCredentialGate.sol hits a Hardhat config snag, drop the IdentityRegistry patch and ship the gate + two demos only
spec.success_criteria: authorizeAction() on-chain reverts for any AgentPolicy proof where expiryTimestamp < block.timestamp, accepts valid in-window proofs, and the delegation-collapse demo shows a root expiry atomically invalidating a two-level sub-agent credential — all without any admin key call, ACL write, or oracle interaction. Gas per verification < 300k on Base Sepolia.
spec.theseus_dependencies: EVM compatibility confirmation (or their VM's BN254 pairing support), testnet RPC + faucet access, their on-chain agent execution engine interface (specifically: what hook point can call authorizeAction() before settling an agent action), and clarity on whether block.timestamp is their canonical time source or whether they run an oracle

tier3_findings: AXIS 1 — CONCERN: The core expiry-without-oracle property can be replicated with signed credentials containing an inline expiry timestamp checked against block.timestamp in a simple Solidity verifier. This is 1-2 weeks of work, not 3 months. The proposal's defense attacks ACL revocation lists but ignores the simpler alternative: EdDSA-signed credential with expiry field. The ZKP is only indispensable if Theseus requires credential privacy (hiding which agent or what permissions are being used), which is unconfirmed and structurally unlikely given agents are already first-class on-chain citizens with public identities.

AXIS 1 — PARTIAL SAVE: The cascading delegation invalidation (revoking root collapses entire sub-agent tree atomically, without on-chain traversal) is harder to replicate without ZKP. Standard crypto alternatives require either expensive on-chain delegation tree traversal or each child credential querying parent liveness at verification time (which reintroduces oracle dependency). This is a genuine ZKP advantage — but only if Theseus actually has multi-tier agent delegation trees at launch, which is unconfirmed.

AXIS 2 — CONCERN: The proposal's strongest framing ('eliminates trusted-admin bottleneck') is valid for ACL-style revocation but misleading as the sole comparison. A signed expiry field in an EdDSA credential is equally oracle-free and admin-free. The marginal ZKP value reduces to: (a) hiding credential details in the proof, and (b) enabling delegation tree collapse. On an L1 where agent identities are transparent by design, (a) has questionable economic value. The proposal should have scoped its defense to (b) exclusively.

AXIS 3 — PASS: The structural constraint is real. On a no-human-key-path L1, an autonomous expiry mechanism is architecturally necessary at launch, not at 10,000 agents. A compromised key with no autonomous expiry is a security hole from day one. Use case is concrete: any financial-action agent with a time-bounded authorization window. Demand strength rated 'strong' is defensible.

AXIS 4 — CONCERN: Theseus's VM compatibility is unknown. If non-EVM, porting a Groth16 verifier is weeks of non-trivial cryptographic engineering — vastly exceeding the claimed '2-day adapter.' Even if EVM-compatible, every on-chain agent action now carries snarkjs/rapidsnark proof generation overhead (1-5 seconds on commodity hardware) plus ~300K gas for Groth16 verification. On an L1 designed for fast autonomous agent execution, this latency profile needs explicit validation from Theseus before committing. The proposal treats this as a solved problem but it is the highest-risk integration unknown.

AXIS 5 — PASS: New development is minimal (~2 days for TheseusCredentialGate.sol). Existing circuits require zero modification. If Theseus fails, no sunk cost beyond a thin Solidity file that demonstrates Bolyra's real-world deployment pattern — reusable as a reference implementation for any EVM-compatible agent-native chain. Risk is low.
tier3_summary: The autonomous expiry use case is real and structurally necessary for Theseus, but the ZKP advantage is only defensible for cascading delegation tree invalidation — not simple expiry — and the proposal must first confirm EVM compatibility and agent delegation depth at launch before committing to the integration.

The ZKP is only indispensable if Theseus requires credential privacy (hiding which agent or what permissions are being used), which is unconfirmed and structurally unlikely given agents are already first-class on-chain citizens with public identities.

title: Circuit-enforced cumulative spending caps for autonomous agent wallets
description: Theseus's 'no human key path' architecture means there is no human approving transactions after the fact — spending limits must be enforced at the cryptographic layer, not the UI layer. Bolyra's Delegation circuit enforces one-way scope narrowing on-chain: a parent agent can issue a credential to a sub-agent with FINANCIAL_MEDIUM (bit 3) but not FINANCIAL_UNLIMITED (bit 4), and the circuit rejects any proof that exceeds the delegated cap. This directly solves the autonomous commerce control problem that Coinbase Agentic Wallets and AWS AgentCore Payments leave open — both provide payment rails but no authorization proof that the spending agent was actually permitted to transact at that amount. Theseus cannot build this without ZKP expertise; it requires a circuit-level constraint, not just a smart contract balance check (which an adversarial agent can route around).

rationale.agent_need: The 'no human key path' constraint creates a genuine structural need for cryptographic spending enforcement, but the evidence relies on architectural inference rather than direct Theseus documentation or community signals confirming they lack this capability.
rationale.zkp_edge: The argument that a smart contract balance check can be routed around by an adversarial agent is structurally sound, but cumulative spend tracking (the harder problem) still requires on-chain state, and the ZKP advantage narrows to privacy of the permission tier rather than a fundamental capability gap.
rationale.primitive_readiness: Delegation and AgentPolicy circuits exist and are correctly mapped, but the CapAccumulatorRegistry is new contract work and the x402 middleware integration stubs are incomplete — realistic effort is closer to 2-3 weeks including testing and Base Sepolia deployment.
rationale.partnership_leverage: The ZKP circuit expertise gap is real, but Theseus could adopt Lit Protocol's threshold access control or Privy's embedded wallet delegation within 3 months, and the analysis doesn't address whether Theseus already has a payments authorization layer in progress.

mapped_primitives.usage: Enforces one-way permission-tier narrowing at circuit level: a parent agent holding FINANCIAL_UNLIMITED (bit 4) can delegate only FINANCIAL_MEDIUM (bit 3) to a sub-agent; the circuit's bitwise-subset check rejects any proof where delegateeScope is not a subset of delegatorScope. This is the primary cryptographic primitive for Theseus's 'no human key path' requirement — tier caps are enforced before the transaction reaches any payment rail.

Provides selective disclosure of the financial permission bit to a verifier without revealing the full bitmask or credential commitment. Theseus's payment router calls this proof to confirm the transacting agent has FINANCIAL_MEDIUM or FINANCIAL_SMALL set before forwarding to Coinbase Agentic Wallets / x402. The expiry enforcement (expiryTimestamp > currentTimestamp) doubles as a spending-window boundary.

Already deployed on Base Sepolia. Delegation verification with on-chain chain-state tracking and delegation nullifier replay protection covers the 'same sub-agent can't re-use a spent delegation token' requirement. Theseus's settlement contract calls verifyDelegation() here before releasing funds.

Existing x402 payment-protocol integration stubs in integrations/payment-protocols/ give the wiring surface for injecting a Bolyra spending-cap proof as a required HTTP header before the x402 facilitator accepts a payment request from an agent.

SDK-level enforcement that bit 3 (FINANCIAL_MEDIUM) implies bit 2 (FINANCIAL_SMALL), preventing an operator from issuing a credential that bypasses lower-tier limits. Used at credential issuance time before any on-chain proof is generated.

new_components_needed.description: SpendingCapAccumulator circuit (PLONK): takes (agentNullifier, periodStart, accumulatedSpend, transactionAmount, authorizedCap) as private inputs and (periodAccumulatorRoot, capCommitment) as public inputs. Proves accumulatedSpend + transactionAmount <= authorizedCap without revealing individual transaction history. The accumulator root is a Poseidon hash of the on-chain CapAccumulatorRegistry state, chained identically to how HumanUniqueness reads the humanMerkleRoot. This is the piece that neither Coinbase Agentic Wallets nor AWS AgentCore provides.

CapAccumulatorRegistry: on-chain mapping from (agentCredentialNullifier, periodStart) to accumulatedSpend. Atomically updates spend counter and verifies the SpendingCapAccumulator proof in the same transaction. Emits CapUtilized events for off-chain monitoring. Integrates with IdentityRegistry by reading the agentTree root for nullifier validity.

proveSpendingCap(agentCredential, transactionAmount, onchainAccumulatorState) → SpendingCapProof. Fetches current accumulator state from CapAccumulatorRegistry, generates SpendingCapAccumulator proof, bundles with the AgentPolicy proof into a composite authorization envelope that Theseus's payment router can verify in a single call.

Theseus bridge adapter: intercepts agent payment intents before they reach Coinbase Agentic Wallets / x402 facilitator, calls proveSpendingCap(), attaches the proof as a 'Bolyra-Cap-Proof' HTTP header (mirroring the x402 'X-Payment' header pattern), and validates on-chain via CapAccumulatorRegistry before forwarding. Can be deployed as a sidecar to Theseus's no-human-key-path orchestrator.

SpendingCapProof wire format extension to draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01: define the capEnvelope field alongside the existing handshakeEnvelope, specify the accumulator Merkle scheme, and add 8–10 new conformance test vectors covering cap-exactly-met, cap-exceeded, period-rollover, and nullifier-replay scenarios.

theseus_integration_surface: Three-layer surface: (1) Verifier contracts on Base Sepolia — Theseus's settlement contract calls CapAccumulatorRegistry.verifyAndAccumulate(spendingCapProof, agentNullifier, amount), which internally calls PlonkVerifier for the SpendingCapAccumulator proof and DelegationPlonkVerifier for the delegation chain, all in one atomic transaction. (2) SDK bridge — Theseus's TypeScript orchestrator installs @bolyra/sdk and calls proveSpendingCap() before dispatching any agent payment; the resulting proof envelope travels with the payment request as an HTTP header understood by the x402 facilitator. (3) If Theseus runs on its own EVM chain rather than Base Sepolia, a lightweight cross-chain option is a Bolyra-signed attestation (off-chain proof + ECDSA operator signature) that Theseus's chain verifies without needing the full Groth16/PLONK verifier deployed locally — this mirrors the OpenClaw trust-verification adapter pattern already in @bolyra/openclaw.

deliverables.name: sunday-demo-script
deliverables.description: Node script using existing AgentPolicy + Delegation circuits to show: operator issues FINANCIAL_MEDIUM credential to sub-agent, sub-agent generates proof, a mock 'Theseus payment router' verifies the proof before accepting an x402 payment. No new circuits — uses circuits/build/ artifacts already compiled. Run in ~30 seconds live on the call.

deliverables.name: theseus-integration-spec
deliverables.description: Single markdown doc covering: (1) proof flow diagram for agent spending caps, (2) wire format for spending-cap proof as HTTP header injected before x402 facilitator, (3) CapAccumulatorRegistry interface, (4) open questions requiring Theseus answers (VM type, nullifier storage, settlement contract hooks). This is the artifact to share with Eric Wang after the call.

deliverables.name: x402-spending-cap-middleware
deliverables.description: Thin Express/Node middleware that wraps the existing x402 payment-protocol integration stub. Before forwarding any payment request, it calls sdk.verifyHandshake() to confirm the agent holds a valid FINANCIAL_SMALL or FINANCIAL_MEDIUM proof. Injects X-Bolyra-Cap-Proof header. Does NOT yet check accumulated spend — that requires SpendingCapAccumulator. Designed as a drop-in for Theseus's payment router.

deliverables.name: CapAccumulatorRegistry
deliverables.description: Solidity contract deployed on Base Sepolia. Stores per-agent per-period accumulated spend commitments (Poseidon hash chain). Exposes: recordSpend(agentNullifier, amount, proof), getAccumulatorRoot(agentNullifier, periodStart), verifyCapProof(proof, publicInputs). Called by Theseus settlement contract before releasing funds. Deliberately thin — all cap logic lives in the circuit, not here.

deliverables.name: SpendingCapAccumulator
deliverables.description: PLONK circuit: private inputs (agentNullifier, periodStart, accumulatedSpend, transactionAmount, authorizedCap); public inputs (periodAccumulatorRoot, capCommitment). Proves accumulatedSpend + transactionAmount <= authorizedCap without revealing history. Poseidon accumulator root chains identically to HumanUniqueness humanMerkleRoot pattern. This is the critical differentiator — neither Coinbase Agentic Wallets nor AWS AgentCore provides cryptographic spend accumulation.
deliverables.reuses: Poseidon hash gadgets (existing in circuits/src/), pot16.ptau (existing), PLONK build pipeline (existing from AgentPolicy/Delegation)

deliverables.name: theseus-sdk-adapter
deliverables.description: Thin TypeScript wrapper around @bolyra/sdk that exposes Theseus-idiomatic API: registerAgentOnTheseus(modelHash, permissions, expiry), proveSpend(agentCredential, amount, accumulatorState), verifySpend(proof, capCommitment). Handles the round-trip: generate AgentPolicy proof → generate SpendingCapAccumulator proof → package both as a Theseus transaction payload. Published as @bolyra/theseus-adapter.

spec.demo_description: Live terminal demo: (1) operator issues FINANCIAL_MEDIUM credential to a mock sub-agent using createAgentCredential(), (2) sub-agent calls proveHandshake() to produce an AgentPolicy proof asserting bit 3 is set, (3) mock Theseus payment router calls verifyHandshake() — approves the $500 payment, rejects a $15,000 payment that requires FINANCIAL_UNLIMITED. Takes ~30 seconds. No new code needed — script uses compiled build/ artifacts. Visually shows the 'no human key path' property: the circuit enforces the cap, not a UI approval.
risks: SpendingCapAccumulator circuit constraint count may exceed pot16.ptau (2^16). If accumulator history depth is >10 entries, constraint count could hit 80K+. Mitigation: prototype with depth=5 first, benchmark before committing to PLONK.
Theseus VM compatibility unknown — if they're not EVM-compatible, CapAccumulatorRegistry needs a rewrite. Do not write Solidity until Eric confirms EVM on the call.
x402 facilitator API may change — the existing payment-protocols stubs were written against x402.org draft. Verify the header format hasn't shifted before the middleware goes live.

Solo-founder bandwidth: SpendingCapAccumulator at 10 days is the critical path. Any circuit debugging or trusted-setup re-run can double that. Timebox to 12 days max; if not working, ship AgentPolicy-only cap enforcement (weaker but shippable) and document the limitation.
Theseus may already be building their own auth primitive — validate on Sunday before investing circuit days.
spec.success_criteria: A Theseus agent wallet attempts two payments via the x402 middleware: one within its delegated FINANCIAL_MEDIUM cap, one above it. The within-cap payment produces a valid SpendingCapAccumulator proof accepted by CapAccumulatorRegistry.verifyCapProof(). The over-cap payment fails proof generation client-side (circuit rejects the witness). Both outcomes are deterministic, require no human approval, and produce verifiable on-chain state on Base Sepolia.
spec.theseus_dependencies: VM type confirmation (EVM-compatible or custom — blocks contract work), testnet RPC endpoint or confirmation Base Sepolia is acceptable, settlement contract interface (what hook does their payment router expose for pre-flight authorization checks), agent key format (EdDSA keypair spec — must match AgentPolicy circuit's expected credential structure), confirmation of whether they need human-rooted trust chain (HumanUniqueness) or pure agent-rooted chain (AgentPolicy only).

tier3_findings: AXIS 1 (Theseus Builds It Themselves) — CONCERN: The cumulative spending cap WITHOUT ZKP is straightforward Solidity: a mapping(agentNullifier => mapping(periodStart => uint256)) atomically updated at settlement time. A 1-engineer team builds this in under 2 weeks. The ZKP version adds private accumulation (hiding individual transaction amounts), but that privacy property is the only differentiator — and the proposal never establishes that Theseus needs transaction amount privacy. The new SpendingCapAccumulator circuit (10 days, high complexity) is the crux of the value proposition, and it can be sidestepped entirely with a public on-chain counter.

AXIS 2 (Standard Crypto Suffices) — FAIL: The proposal's core claim — 'a smart contract balance check can be routed around by an adversarial agent' — is only true for off-chain checks. A properly implemented on-chain counter that IS the settlement gate cannot be routed around; the EVM revert is atomic. The adversarial routing argument applies to middleware-layer checks (e.g., an HTTP sidecar), but the proposal itself architectures the enforcement at the contract layer (CapAccumulatorRegistry.verifyAndAccumulate). The ZKP overhead narrows to: hiding per-transaction amounts from the public ledger. In Theseus's 'agents are first-class on-chain entities' model, agents transacting on a public L1 already expose activity — the marginal privacy value is unclear and unpriced. Standard Solidity achieves the same spending cap enforcement with zero circuit complexity.

AXIS 3 (No Agent Actually Needs This) — CONCERN: The structural need is real — autonomous agents with no human approval path must have cryptographic spend enforcement. But this proposal layers the hardest possible version (private accumulation ZKP circuit) on top of a basic need that a tier-enforcement credential (existing Delegation circuit, zero new work) already partially addresses. The first agent use case named in the card is generic ('autonomous commerce control') rather than a specific Theseus agent type with a named function. At Theseus's current scale (pre-launch, $100K funded), the operational need is basic identity, not sophisticated private spend accounting. The demand_strength field in the card itself is rated 'none'.

AXIS 4 (Integration Complexity vs Value) — FAIL: Total new-component effort is 25 days (10 circuit + 5 contract + 3 SDK + 5 integration + 2 spec). Theseus's VM architecture is unknown — if non-EVM, the Solidity CapAccumulatorRegistry and PlonkVerifier do not deploy without a custom verifier implementation. The CapAccumulatorRegistry must co-locate with Theseus's settlement contract, meaning either Theseus runs on Base (unlikely for a custom L1) or Bolyra must re-architect cross-chain settlement verification. The Theseus-bridge-adapter sidecar introduces a liveness dependency: if the Bolyra proving service is down, agent payments halt. For a $100K-funded pre-mainnet chain, asking their 1-2 engineers to integrate a 4-layer ZKP stack (circuit, contract, SDK, bridge adapter) before their own chain is live is an adoption killer.

AXIS 5 (Single-Partner Dependency) — PASS WITH CAVEAT: The SpendingCapAccumulator circuit and CapAccumulatorRegistry are genuinely reusable primitives for any agent-native payment rail (Coinbase AgentKit, AWS AgentCore, Virtuals Protocol). The SDK module generalizes. However, Theseus's $100K Orange DAO seed is an extremely thin runway — they are the definition of pre-revenue pre-launch risk. The 25 days of new work produces IP that survives Theseus, but the Theseus-specific bridge adapter (5 days) is pure sunk cost if Theseus pivots or folds.

CONDITIONAL PATH: Strip the SpendingCapAccumulator circuit (10 days, high complexity) from scope. The existing Delegation circuit already enforces FINANCIAL_MEDIUM vs FINANCIAL_UNLIMITED tier separation at the credential level — that alone is the defensible ZKP advantage over standard Solidity. A two-week sprint using only existing primitives (Delegation + AgentPolicy + IdentityRegistry) to demonstrate tier-enforced agent payments on Base Sepolia is the right first step. If Theseus confirms EVM compatibility and has a specific agent type that needs private cumulative accounting, the accumulator circuit becomes justified at a later phase.
tier3_summary: The core ZKP-enforced spending tier concept survives (Delegation circuit is genuinely defensible), but the new SpendingCapAccumulator circuit is premature — standard on-chain counters achieve the same enforcement without it, and the 25-day build scope is disproportionate to Theseus's pre-launch state and unknown VM architecture; scope down to existing primitives only.
