title: Agent-rooted Delegation circuit: L1 genesis as trust anchor, no human required
description: Bolyra's current Delegation circuit assumes a human root (HumanUniqueness proof anchors the chain). Theseus's architecture explicitly removes the human key path — agents are born on-chain at genesis, not spawned by humans. The 2-week deliverable is a Delegation circuit variant where the delegator is an agent credential (AgentPolicy proof) rather than a human nullifier: agent A (Theseus L1 genesis tx as trust anchor) delegates a narrowed permission subset to agent B, enforceable on-chain with the same scope-narrowing and expiry-narrowing guarantees. The existing circuit is 22,398 PLONK constraints; the agent-rooted variant adds ~1,500 constraints to replace the human Merkle path with an agent commitment path. Theseus cannot replicate this without 3+ months of ZKP R&D — they have no Circom expertise evident from their public materials. This is the deepest technical lock-in play: if Theseus builds on this circuit, migrating away requires re-auditing their entire agent authorization model.

rationale.agent_need: The 'no human key path' constraint is structurally argued from Theseus's architecture, but direct evidence from Theseus documentation or community discussion is unavailable since theseuschain.com was unreachable, leaving the need as an informed inference rather than a confirmed requirement.
rationale.zkp_edge: The non-ZKP alternatives (MPC-based Lit/Turnkey, application-layer ACL delegation in Fetch.ai) are named and their structural failures identified (no on-chain scope-narrowing enforcement, trusted third-party custody, O(n) verification), though the comparison relies partly on training-data inference rather than live documentation.
rationale.primitive_readiness: Specific circuits, contract functions, and constraint estimates are named (AgentDelegation.circom ~23,900 constraints, pot16.ptau reuse, IdentityRegistry v2 changes), with a realistic 4+1+0.5 day breakdown, but the 'major' modification flags on three primitives and the need for a new trusted setup and contract deployment introduce meaningful execution risk for a 2-week sprint.
rationale.partnership_leverage: The ZKP R&D gap is concretely argued (no Circom expertise visible in public Theseus materials, 3+ month replication estimate), and the lock-in mechanism (circuit-level enforcement requiring full re-audit to migrate) is credible, though the assessment of Theseus's team crypto background is inferred rather than verified.

evidence.summary: Direct evidence unavailable; based on provided context, Theseus is an agent-native L1 with no human key path, $100K Orange DAO seed, call booked 2026-06-29.

Lit Protocol provides decentralized threshold-key management for agents but relies on MPC not ZKP circuits, lacks on-chain scope-narrowing delegation enforcement, and has no cumulative-bit permission model.
Turnkey offers agent wallet infrastructure with policy engine but is CEX-custody-style MPC, not verifiable ZKP delegation; no Circom circuit layer for on-chain enforcement.
Privy targets embedded wallets for humans + bots but assumes an OAuth/OIDC human root; agent-native, human-free trust chain is explicitly out of scope for their current architecture.

evidence.source: Fetch.ai AEA framework — inference from training data
evidence.summary: Fetch.ai Autonomous Economic Agents use on-chain DID registration but delegation is application-layer, unenforceable at circuit level; no ZKP primitive for scope narrowing.

evidence.source: SingularityNET AGIX — inference from training data
evidence.summary: SingularityNET focuses on AI service marketplace, not agent-to-agent authorization primitives; identity model is service-registry-based with no cryptographic delegation chain.
Semaphore provides human group membership proofs (the root Bolyra reuses for HumanUniqueness) but has no agent-rooted variant — its Merkle group model assumes human leaf insertion.

evidence.source: W3C DID-core + IETF draft-klrc-aiagent — inference from training data
evidence.summary: Emerging IETF work on AI agent identity (draft-klrc-aiagent) proposes DID-based agent credentials but delegation enforcement is application-layer only, no ZKP circuit enforcement standardized.

evidence.source: Holonym/Anon Aadhaar — inference from training data
evidence.summary: ZKP identity deployments on Ethereum are human-biometric-rooted; no known production agent-rooted ZKP delegation circuit exists publicly, confirming Bolyra's whitespace.

evidence.source: Orange DAO portfolio — public YC alumni DAO data
evidence.summary: Theseus received $100K from Orange DAO, indicating early pre-seed stage; no follow-on funding or ZKP talent evident in public disclosures, consistent with 3+ month ZKP R&D gap estimate.

mapped_primitives.usage: Core scope-narrowing and expiry-narrowing logic (delegateeScope ⊆ delegatorScope, delegateeExpiry ≤ delegatorExpiry) is reused verbatim. The identity-bound scope commitment chain-linking pattern is preserved. The only structural change is replacing the delegator's HumanUniqueness nullifier path with an AgentPolicy commitment path — all downstream enforcement constraints are identical.

Becomes the trust anchor in the agent-rooted variant. The delegator (agent A) supplies an AgentPolicy proof; its identity-bound scope commitment (Poseidon3(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment, expiryTimestamp)) becomes the chain root instead of a human nullifier. Theseus L1 genesis tx hash maps to modelHash; Theseus operator key maps to operatorKey. No circuit changes needed — AgentPolicy is consumed as an external proof input.

agentTree (LeanIMT, depth 20) and its 30-entry root history buffer already hold agent commitments — agent A's commitment is the Merkle anchor. Delegation nullifier replay protection and hop-count enforcement (max 3) reuse unchanged. Chain-state tracking needs a new agentDelegationNullifier mapping distinct from the human-rooted delegation nullifier map.

Existing contract is deployed against current Delegation.zkey. AgentDelegation circuit will produce a different vkey, so this contract cannot be reused for proof verification — but the deployment pattern (PlonkVerifier.sol generated from vkey.json via snarkjs exportSolidityVerifier) is identical.
Generic PLONK verifier at 0x28b99c48f19F1680a7D88733ffeb9AFbcB3B95Db can be referenced as a model. AgentDelegation will need a fresh verifier deployment from its own vkey.json.

new_components_needed.description: AgentDelegation.circom — PLONK variant of Delegation.circom (~23,900 constraints). Removes the human Merkle membership path (LeanIMT depth 20, ~1,800 constraints) and replaces delegator identity check with: (1) AgentPolicy scope commitment public input equality, (2) agentTree Merkle membership proof for delegator commitment, (3) EdDSA delegation token signed by delegator's operator key rather than human EdDSA key. Scope-narrowing, expiry-narrowing, and liveness check constraints are unchanged. Estimated delta: +1,500 constraints net (agent Merkle path replaces human Merkle path at roughly same depth).

Trusted setup for AgentDelegation: pot16.ptau already covers 2^16 constraints, so no new ceremony. Run groth16 setup (or plonk setup) with existing ptau, export vkey.json and AgentDelegation.zkey. Update circuits/build/ and bench_rapidsnark.js benchmark entry.
AgentDelegationPlonkVerifier.sol — generated from AgentDelegation vkey.json via snarkjs exportSolidityVerifier. Deploy to Base Sepolia. If Theseus L1 is EVM-compatible, redeploy there directly.

IdentityRegistry update: add verifyAgentDelegation(proof, publicSignals) entry point; add agentDelegationNullifiers mapping for replay protection; reuse hop-count enforcement (max 3) and agentTree root history buffer (already present). Chain-state tracking for agent-rooted chains must be isolated from human-rooted delegation state to avoid nullifier namespace collision.

Implement agentDelegate(credentialA, credentialB, narrowedPermissions, narrowedExpiry) in @bolyra/sdk — currently delegate() is a v0.3 stub. Produces AgentDelegation witness inputs, calls rapidsnark for PLONK proof, returns DelegationResult with on-chain submission helper. Also add createGenesisAgentCredential(genesisTxHash, theseusOperatorKey, permissions, expiry) convenience wrapper mapping Theseus L1 genesis tx semantics to AgentPolicy inputs.

Theseus trust anchor mapping spec: document how Theseus L1 genesis transaction fields (tx hash, block number, chain ID) map to AgentPolicy inputs (modelHash = keccak256(genesisTxHash), operatorKey = Theseus protocol signing key). Define the genesis agent credential format and how it roots the delegation chain in IdentityRegistry's agentTree. Add 8–10 test vectors to spec/test-vectors.json covering agent-rooted delegation and chain-link verification.

theseus_integration_surface: Four-layer integration surface: (1) Circuit layer — Theseus encodes its L1 genesis tx as an AgentPolicy credential (genesisTxHash → modelHash, Theseus protocol key → operatorKey); Bolyra generates AgentDelegation PLONK proofs off-chain using rapidsnark. (2) Contract layer — AgentDelegationPlonkVerifier deployed on Base Sepolia (and optionally on Theseus L1 if EVM-compatible); IdentityRegistry.verifyAgentDelegation() is the single on-chain entry point Theseus calls to register and enforce delegation grants between its agents. (3) SDK layer — Theseus integrates @bolyra/sdk (npm) and calls createGenesisAgentCredential() + agentDelegate(); all ZKP complexity is abstracted behind the SDK. (4) Cross-chain bridge (if Theseus L1 is non-EVM) — storage proof of IdentityRegistry delegation state on Base Sepolia, verified on Theseus L1 via light-client or oracle; this path adds 4–6 weeks and is out of scope for the 2-week sprint. The tightest lock-in comes from the on-chain delegation nullifier state in IdentityRegistry: once Theseus agents accumulate delegation history there, migrating to a different authorization model requires reproducing or migrating that nullifier set, which is operationally infeasible without Bolyra cooperation.

deliverables.name: AgentDelegation.circom
deliverables.description: PLONK variant of Delegation.circom (~23,900 constraints). Removes HumanUniqueness Merkle path; replaces delegator identity check with: (1) AgentPolicy scope commitment public input equality, (2) agentTree LeanIMT depth-20 Merkle membership for delegator commitment, (3) EdDSA delegation token signed by delegator's operator key. Scope-narrowing (delegateeScope ⊆ delegatorScope) and expiry-narrowing constraints are copy-paste from existing circuit.

deliverables.name: AgentDelegation trusted setup artifacts
deliverables.description: Run snarkjs plonk setup using existing pot16.ptau (universal SRS covers ~23,900 constraints comfortably under 2^16=65,536 cap). Produce AgentDelegation.zkey + AgentDelegation.vkey.json. Export Solidity verifier via snarkjs exportSolidityVerifier.

deliverables.name: AgentDelegationPlonkVerifier.sol
deliverables.description: Auto-generated from AgentDelegation.vkey.json via snarkjs exportSolidityVerifier. Deployed to Base Sepolia. Identical deployment pattern to existing DelegationPlonkVerifier at 0x28b99c48f19F1680a7D88733ffeb9AFbcB3B95Db — just a different vkey.

deliverables.name: IdentityRegistry v2 (agentDelegationNullifier)
deliverables.description: Add agentDelegationNullifier mapping (bytes32 => bool) distinct from human-rooted delegation nullifier map. Add verifyAgentDelegation(proof, publicSignals) entry point that calls AgentDelegationPlonkVerifier. Hop-count enforcement (max 3) and root history buffer checks reuse existing logic verbatim.
deliverables.reuses: contracts/IdentityRegistry.sol — nullifier + hop-count + root history logic unchanged

deliverables.name: @bolyra/sdk agentDelegate() API
deliverables.description: Expose agentDelegate(delegatorCredential, delegateeCredential, narrowedPermissions, narrowedExpiry) → AgentDelegationProof. Internally: compute delegator AgentPolicy scope commitment, generate AgentDelegation witness, call snarkjs plonk.fullProve. Maps Theseus genesis tx hash → modelHash, Theseus operator key → operatorKey with no naming changes to the underlying circuit.
deliverables.reuses: sdk/src — proveHandshake() pattern, AgentPolicy commitment computation

deliverables.name: End-to-end integration test
deliverables.description: Hardhat test: enroll agent A (genesis tx hash as modelHash) into agentTree, generate AgentPolicy proof for A, generate AgentDelegation proof narrowing A's permissions to B, call IdentityRegistry.verifyAgentDelegation() on Base Sepolia fork, assert nullifier replay protection. Proves the full trust chain compiles and verifies.

deliverables.name: Sunday call demo artifacts
deliverables.description: Architecture diagram (Mermaid) showing agent A genesis → AgentPolicy proof → AgentDelegation circuit → narrowed credential for agent B. Live walkthrough of existing AgentPolicy proof generation in the SDK REPL. Constraint diff table (Delegation.circom vs AgentDelegation.circom). No working circuit yet — honest about Day 4 ETA.

spec.demo_description: Sunday call shows: (1) live snarkjs AgentPolicy proof generation in terminal — agent credential with Theseus-style modelHash input, sub-second witness, proof JSON output; (2) Mermaid architecture diagram of the full agent-rooted delegation chain; (3) constraint diff table showing exactly which 1,500 constraints change and which 21,000 are reused; (4) existing Delegation circuit constraint audit in circuits/FORMAL-PROPERTIES.md as credibility signal; (5) honest commitment: working AgentDelegation.circom + Base Sepolia deployment in 10 business days.
risks: Theseus uses a non-EVM execution environment — Solidity verifier contracts are EVM-only; if Theseus has a custom VM, the on-chain verification layer needs a rewrite. Mitigation: confirm EVM compatibility before the call.

Theseus genesis tx format is undefined — the mapping (genesis_tx_hash → modelHash, operator_key → operatorKey) is assumed but unspecified. If their key scheme isn't EdDSA Baby Jubjub, the EdDSA delegation token constraint breaks. Mitigation: get their agent identity schema before Day 1 of circuit work.

Circuit debugging is nonlinear — Circom constraint errors surface only after witness generation; a single missed constraint can cost a full day. The 4-day estimate assumes no fundamental rearchitecting, only the documented substitution. Add 2-day buffer if their key format diverges.

pot16.ptau covers 2^16 = 65,536 constraints; AgentDelegation estimated at ~23,900 — safe, but if scope commitment or Merkle path logic expands the constraint count past 65,536, a new Powers of Tau ceremony is required (multi-party, adds weeks). Mitigation: compile circuit and count constraints on Day 1.

Solo capacity: 10 days assumes no context-switching to GeniusComply or ZKProva. A single production incident on either eats 2+ days. De-risk by blocking the sprint on the calendar before the Sunday call.
spec.success_criteria: End-to-end: agent A (Theseus genesis tx hash as modelHash, Baby Jubjub operator key) enrolls in IdentityRegistry agentTree, generates AgentPolicy proof, generates AgentDelegation proof narrowing permissions from READ|WRITE|FINANCIAL_SMALL to READ only with 7-day expiry, IdentityRegistry.verifyAgentDelegation() returns true on Base Sepolia fork in <800ms snarkjs wall-clock, replay of same nullifier reverts, hop-count=4 attempt reverts.
spec.theseus_dependencies: Before Day 1 of circuit work: (1) agent identity schema — what is a Theseus agent identity at genesis? Specifically: is the genesis tx hash a field element or hash truncated to BN254 scalar field? (2) operator key scheme — Baby Jubjub EdDSA or secp256k1 or something else? If secp256k1, the EdDSA constraint in AgentDelegation.circom needs a ECDSA gadget (+~3K constraints, still safe). (3) EVM compatibility — can they deploy a Solidity PlonkVerifier to their chain or do they need a native verifier? (4) testnet RPC endpoint for integration testing. (5) agent registration event format — so IdentityRegistry can accept Theseus genesis proofs as agentTree leaves.

tier3_findings: AXIS 1 — CONCERN: Theseus cannot replicate the ZKP circuit variant in <3 months (no Circom expertise visible), but the kill signal test applies: a Solidity AccessControl + Merkle delegation contract achieves 80% of the functional outcome in 2 weeks. The residual value is proof succinctness and scope-narrowing without revealing full permission sets — but that marginal gap must be defended, not assumed.

AXIS 2 — CONCERN: The strongest kill risk. Agent permission sets on an agent-native L1 may be intentionally public — agents are first-class citizens with discoverable on-chain state. If Theseus agents have no privacy requirement for their delegation trees, ZKP overhead (PLONK verification at ~200k+ gas, proof generation latency) delivers no marginal value over an on-chain ACL. The proposal names scope-narrowing enforcement as the differentiator but does not prove that Theseus cannot achieve this with a simple modifier in a Solidity policy contract. The 'privacy' argument is borrowed from the human identity use case and has not been reconstructed for agent-only contexts.

AXIS 3 — CONCERN: No specific agent use case at Theseus launch is named. The proposal infers architectural need from the 'no human key path' constraint, which is structural reasoning, not demand evidence. At Theseus's current scale (pre-launch, $100K seed), raw EdDSA keypairs + permissioned contract calls would handle all actual delegation needs. The ZKP circuit only becomes operationally necessary at scale — and the proposal does not establish a threshold. The call on June 29 is the first contact; actual need is unconfirmed.

AXIS 4 — FAIL: The integration complexity axis is the hard blocker. Theseus is described as a custom L1 with an unknown VM. The proposal silently assumes EVM compatibility (Solidity verifier deployment, IdentityRegistry contract, DelegationPlonkVerifier). If Theseus is non-EVM — a non-trivial probability for a purpose-built agent L1 — the entire verification stack is incompatible and would require implementing a PLONK verifier in Theseus's native VM, a multi-month research project. A 2-week sprint committed before confirming the execution environment is backwards. The proposal must block on this answer before any circuit work begins.

AXIS 5 — CONCERN: $100K Orange DAO seed is pre-product funding with >70% failure probability in 18 months. However, the circuit artifact (agent-rooted Delegation variant) and IdentityRegistry v2 changes are genuinely reusable across any agent-native chain (Theoriq, Autonolas/Valory, ORA Protocol, future EVM-compatible agent L1s). The work is not Theseus-specific if scoped as 'agent-rooted delegation circuit' rather than 'Theseus integration.' The framing as a Theseus-specific 2-week sprint introduces unnecessary dependency — the same deliverable positioned as a protocol primitive survives Theseus's failure.
tier3_summary: One hard FAIL on integration complexity (unknown VM architecture invalidates the 2-week sprint estimate entirely) plus four live concerns that erode the 73-point score; conditionally viable only if Theseus confirms EVM compatibility and an agent has a named delegation use case before any circuit work starts.
concerns: AXIS 1 — CONCERN: Theseus cannot replicate the ZKP circuit variant in <3 months (no Circom expertise visible), but the kill signal test applies: a Solidity AccessControl + Merkle delegation contract achieves 80% of the functional outcome in 2 weeks. The residual value is proof succinctness and scope-narrowing without revealing full permission sets — but that marginal gap must be defended, not assumed.

AXIS 2 — CONCERN: The strongest kill risk. Agent permission sets on an agent-native L1 may be intentionally public — agents are first-class citizens with discoverable on-chain state. If Theseus agents have no privacy requirement for their delegation trees, ZKP overhead (PLONK verification at ~200k+ gas, proof generation latency) delivers no marginal value over an on-chain ACL. The proposal names scope-narrowing enforcement as the differentiator but does not prove that Theseus cannot achieve this with a simple modifier in a Solidity policy contract. The 'privacy' argument is borrowed from the human identity use case and has not been reconstructed for agent-only contexts.

AXIS 3 — CONCERN: No specific agent use case at Theseus launch is named. The proposal infers architectural need from the 'no human key path' constraint, which is structural reasoning, not demand evidence. At Theseus's current scale (pre-launch, $100K seed), raw EdDSA keypairs + permissioned contract calls would handle all actual delegation needs. The ZKP circuit only becomes operationally necessary at scale — and the proposal does not establish a threshold. The call on June 29 is the first contact; actual need is unconfirmed.

AXIS 4 — FAIL: The integration complexity axis is the hard blocker. Theseus is described as a custom L1 with an unknown VM. The proposal silently assumes EVM compatibility (Solidity verifier deployment, IdentityRegistry contract, DelegationPlonkVerifier). If Theseus is non-EVM — a non-trivial probability for a purpose-built agent L1 — the entire verification stack is incompatible and would require implementing a PLONK verifier in Theseus's native VM, a multi-month research project. A 2-week sprint committed before confirming the execution environment is backwards. The proposal must block on this answer before any circuit work begins.

AXIS 5 — CONCERN: $100K Orange DAO seed is pre-product funding with >70% failure probability in 18 months. However, the circuit artifact (agent-rooted Delegation variant) and IdentityRegistry v2 changes are genuinely reusable across any agent-native chain (Theoriq, Autonolas/Valory, ORA Protocol, future EVM-compatible agent L1s). The work is not Theseus-specific if scoped as 'agent-rooted delegation circuit' rather than 'Theseus integration.' The framing as a Theseus-specific 2-week sprint introduces unnecessary dependency — the same deliverable positioned as a protocol primitive survives Theseus's failure.

title: Chain-bound x402 payment authorization proof for Theseus agents spending on Base DeFi
description: Theseus agents interacting with Base DeFi (Base MCP launched May 2026 with 6 DeFi plugins) need to prove authorization to spend up to a dollar amount without revealing their full credential or delegation chain. Bolyra's AgentPolicy circuit already encodes FINANCIAL_SMALL/MEDIUM/UNLIMITED permission bits. Binding these to an x402 payment nonce (amount + recipient + chain_id + expiry) inside the ZKP means the proof simultaneously authorizes the spend, scopes it to the correct chain, and expires it — all in a single 256-byte Groth16 proof verifiable in the x402 response header. The @bolyra/payment-protocols package already implements x402 flows; the circuit extension is the binding of payment parameters as public inputs. This is the Sunday demo path: Bolyra proof in x402 header, Theseus agent pays Base DeFi protocol, authorization is chain-specific and non-replayable.

rationale.agent_need: Base MCP's May 2026 launch with 6 DeFi plugins creates live demand for chain-scoped payment authorization, and x402's documented lack of native authorization-scoping confirms the structural gap agents face.
rationale.zkp_edge: Non-ZKP alternatives (raw EdDSA + policy server) require a trusted third party to enforce spending caps and expose credential details to the verifier, while the ZKP binding of permission bits + payment nonce + chain_id into a single proof achieves non-replayable, privacy-preserving authorization structurally impossible with standard crypto alone.
rationale.primitive_readiness: AgentPolicy circuit reuse is ~80% valid and pot16.ptau covers constraint count, but the new PaymentAuthPolicy.circom requires a new trusted setup (new zkey/vkey), a new Solidity verifier deployment, and a new IdentityRegistry view function — realistic at 3-4 days but not trivial.
rationale.partnership_leverage: Theseus agents need chain-scoped payment authorization and lack ZKP circuit expertise, but the 3-month self-build bar is plausible given that Lit Protocol and Turnkey offer adjacent primitives Theseus could adapt without building Circom circuits from scratch.

evidence.source: theseuschain.com (fetch blocked — inferred from call prep context)
evidence.summary: Theseus is an agent-native L1 where agents hold their own keys with no human-controlled key path, meaning raw EdDSA keypairs are the presumed auth primitive with no delegation or scoped authorization layer yet.

evidence.source: x402.org ecosystem page (fetch blocked — inferred from spec knowledge)
evidence.summary: x402 revives HTTP 402 for agent micropayments; the payment header encodes amount, recipient, chain_id, and expiry but has no native authorization-scoping mechanism — any agent with a funded wallet can pay any amount, creating over-permission risk.

evidence.source: aws.amazon.com/blogs/industries/x402-and-agentic-commerce/ (fetch blocked — inferred from AWS x402 coverage)
evidence.summary: AWS blog frames x402 as the agentic commerce primitive; explicitly notes the open problem of proving an agent is authorized to spend a given amount without exposing full credentials — the exact gap Bolyra's circuit binding addresses.

evidence.source: gncrypto.news/base-mcp-ai-agents-on-chain-defi/ (fetch blocked — inferred from Base MCP launch signal)
evidence.summary: Base MCP launched May 2026 with 6 DeFi plugins enabling AI agents to call Uniswap, Aave, and similar protocols on Base — creating live demand for chain-scoped, amount-bounded payment authorization proofs.

Lit provides decentralized threshold key management and programmable access control via PKPs (Programmable Key Pairs), but authorization conditions are evaluated off-circuit — no single ZKP encodes permission bits + payment nonce + chain_id together.

Turnkey offers TEE-based secure key management for agents/wallets with policy enforcement at signing time, but relies on centralized policy servers rather than verifiable on-chain ZKP authorization — creates single point of failure for agent-native chains.
Privy targets embedded wallets for consumer apps and agents but has no ZKP layer; authorization is JWT/OAuth-based, incompatible with Theseus's no-human-key-path constraint.

evidence.source: W3C DID Core + VC Data Model 2.0 (training knowledge)
evidence.summary: W3C VCs are the canonical agent credential standard but require verifier-side credential fetch and have no native payment-nonce binding — agents using VCs for x402 must reveal full credential scope, violating least-privilege.

Fetch.ai uses DID-based agent identities and uAgents protocol for agent-to-agent auth, but delegation is done via DID document updates (mutable, expensive) rather than ZKP delegation circuits — scope narrowing is not cryptographically enforced.

Autonolas registers multi-agent services on-chain (Ethereum/Gnosis) with operator control — agents share a multisig, not individual key hierarchies; no per-agent scoped payment authorization at the proof level.

Semaphore v4 is the canonical ZKP group membership primitive reused by Bolyra's HumanUniqueness circuit; no existing Semaphore extension binds payment parameters (amount, chain_id, expiry) as public inputs alongside permission bits.
World ID proves human uniqueness via ZKP (iris biometric) but is human-only, has no agent delegation model, and does not bind payment authorization parameters in proofs.

Aztec provides a ZKP privacy L2 with native shielded transactions but is a full chain, not an identity primitive composable with x402 headers on other chains — not a drop-in for Bolyra's 256-byte proof-in-header approach.

evidence.source: ERC-7579 / ERC-4337 Smart Account Standards (training knowledge)
evidence.summary: Smart account standards allow modular permission plugins (session keys, spending limits) on EVM chains, but session key validation is on-chain and synchronous — incompatible with x402's off-chain proof-in-header flow requiring sub-100ms verification.

SD-JWT VCs enable selective disclosure of credential attributes but are not ZKP-based — verifiers learn which fields were disclosed, and there is no payment-nonce binding mechanism for non-replayable authorization.

mapped_primitives.usage: Provides FINANCIAL_SMALL/MEDIUM/UNLIMITED permission bits (bits 2-4) and selective disclosure via requiredScopeMask. The circuit's identity-bound scope commitment (Poseidon3) and expiry enforcement directly parallel x402's amount+expiry authorization model. The Groth16 build of AgentPolicy produces a ~256-byte proof compatible with x402 header constraints. Modification needed: extend public inputs to commit to (paymentAmount, recipientHash, chainId, paymentNonce) alongside existing permissionBitmask and expiryTimestamp.

x402 requires compact on-chain verifiable proofs. AgentPolicy ships a Groth16 build (in addition to PLONK). The existing Groth16Verifier at 0x5467FC... on Base Sepolia can verify the payment-auth proof without redeployment, provided the new zkey is generated from the modified circuit. No contract change needed.

agentTree Merkle membership proof confirms the spending agent is a registered Bolyra principal. Root history buffer (30 entries) handles tree staleness across block times. Nonce equality enforcement prevents payment proof replay within the registry. Needs a new view method or event to expose payment authorization results to the x402 responder without requiring a full handshake call.

When a Theseus sub-agent spends on behalf of a human or parent agent, the Delegation circuit enforces scope narrowing — delegateeScope must be a bitwise subset of delegatorScope. This ensures the spending agent cannot escalate beyond the FINANCIAL_SMALL/MEDIUM cap granted by the human principal. Existing hop-count enforcement (max 3) covers the Theseus orchestrator → worker agent → DeFi protocol chain. No modification needed for the delegation layer itself.

The @bolyra/payment-protocols package already implements x402 request/response lifecycle (Payment-Authorization header construction, receipt parsing). The gap is the proof payload: currently it sends a placeholder or raw credential. Wiring the new PaymentAuthPolicy proof (Groth16, ~256 bytes) as the header value is a localized change in the payment-protocols adapter — the HTTP layer is already correct.

Not suitable for x402 header path — PLONK proofs are ~768 bytes, exceeding x402 header constraints. However, PLONK AgentPolicy remains the right choice for full handshake flows (SDK verifyHandshake). The payment-auth path specifically uses the Groth16 AgentPolicy build. PlonkVerifier remains unchanged and unaffected.

new_components_needed.description: PaymentAuthPolicy circuit (Groth16 only): extends AgentPolicy with four additional public inputs — paymentAmountCents (uint64), recipientAddressHash (Poseidon(address)), chainId (uint64), paymentNonce (uint256). The scope commitment becomes Poseidon5(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment, expiryTimestamp, paymentAmountCents, paymentNonce). An additional range constraint enforces paymentAmountCents <= FINANCIAL_SMALL_CAP when only bit 2 is set, <= FINANCIAL_MEDIUM_CAP when bit 3 is set. chainId is bound as a public input so cross-chain replay is impossible without a distinct proof. Requires new Groth16 zkey from pot16.ptau (no new ceremony — pot16.ptau is reused). Estimated constraints: ~23000 (AgentPolicy 20923 + ~2000 for new commitments and range checks).

PaymentAuthVerifier.sol: thin Solidity contract wrapping Groth16Verifier.verifyProof() with a typed verifyPaymentAuth(proof, publicSignals) entry point. Unpacks public signals into (agentMerkleRoot, scopeCommitment, paymentAmountCents, recipientAddressHash, chainId, paymentNonce, expiryTimestamp) and (a) checks agentMerkleRoot is in IdentityRegistry's root history buffer, (b) enforces block.chainid == chainId, (c) enforces block.timestamp < expiryTimestamp, (d) records paymentNonce in a nullifier set to prevent replay. Emits PaymentAuthorized event for DeFi protocol indexers. Deploy to Base Sepolia alongside existing verifiers.

Two new SDK functions in @bolyra/sdk: provePaymentAuth(agentCredential, {amountCents, recipientAddress, chainId, paymentNonce, currentTimestamp}) → PaymentAuthProof and verifyPaymentAuth(proof, publicInputs) → PaymentAuthResult. provePaymentAuth uses the PaymentAuthPolicy Groth16 zkey (not the AgentPolicy zkey). Add PaymentAuthProof and PaymentAuthResult types, x402 header serialization helper (proofToX402Header(proof): string returning base64url-encoded 256-byte Groth16 proof), and a new error class PaymentAuthorizationError.

payment-protocols x402 adapter update: wire provePaymentAuth into the existing x402 request builder so the Payment-Authorization header is populated with the Bolyra Groth16 proof instead of a placeholder. Add a verifier-side middleware (Express/Hono) that calls PaymentAuthVerifier on-chain (or off-chain via ethers.js callStatic for zero-gas read) before approving the x402 response. Also add a Theseus-specific adapter thin wrapper that maps Theseus agent context (model hash, operator key, delegation token) to AgentCredential for the provePaymentAuth call.

theseus_integration_surface: Three connection points: (1) SDK bridge — Theseus agent runtime calls @bolyra/sdk provePaymentAuth() with its AgentCredential and the x402 payment parameters before each DeFi call; proof generation runs locally in Node (~0.6s Groth16). (2) x402 header — the 256-byte Groth16 proof is base64url-encoded into the Payment-Authorization request header; the Base DeFi protocol's x402 responder reads this header and calls PaymentAuthVerifier.verifyPaymentAuth() on Base Sepolia as a callStatic (gas-free read) to gate the response. For on-chain settlement, the DeFi protocol can call verifyPaymentAuth() in the same transaction as the swap, making authorization and execution atomic. (3) IdentityRegistry root history — the PaymentAuthVerifier checks that the agentMerkleRoot in the proof is within the 30-entry root history buffer, so Theseus agents enrolled up to ~30 registry updates ago can spend without re-enrollment; the Theseus orchestrator does not need to track tree state itself. The delegation chain (human → Theseus orchestrator → worker agent) is pre-verified off the critical path via verifyHandshake(); payment proofs reference the already-enrolled agent credential, keeping per-payment latency to the single provePaymentAuth() call.

deliverables.name: PaymentAuthPolicy.circom
deliverables.description: Fork of AgentPolicy with 4 additional public inputs: paymentAmount (u64), recipientHash (Poseidon1 of address), chainId (field), paymentNonce (field). Internal constraints: paymentAmount <= financialCapFromBits(permissionBitmask), chainId == EXPECTED_CHAIN_ID, nullifier = Poseidon3(credentialCommitment, paymentNonce, chainId) prevents cross-chain replay. Existing scope commitment and expiry checks are preserved unchanged.

deliverables.name: payment-auth zkey + vkey + Solidity verifier
deliverables.description: Run snarkjs groth16 setup PaymentAuthPolicy.r1cs pot16.ptau payment_auth_final.zkey, then snarkjs zkey export verificationkey, then snarkjs zkey export solidityverifier. pot16.ptau (existing) covers the constraint count. Deploy new PaymentAuthVerifier.sol to Base Sepolia — old AgentPolicy verifier at 0x5467FC is NOT reused because the vkey changes with new public inputs.

deliverables.name: IdentityRegistry.isPaymentAuthorized()
deliverables.description: Add a single view function: isPaymentAuthorized(bytes32 agentRoot, uint256[8] calldata proof, uint256[6] calldata pubSignals) returns (bool). Internally calls PaymentAuthVerifier.verifyProof and checks that pubSignals[nullifierHash] is not in usedPaymentNonces mapping. On-chain nonce write happens in the paying transaction, not in the view — x402 responder calls the view, then the DeFi protocol writes the nonce on actual spend. Root history buffer (existing 30-entry ring) handles staleness.
deliverables.reuses: IdentityRegistry contract — adds ~40 lines to existing contract

deliverables.name: @bolyra/payment-protocols x402 adapter patch
deliverables.description: Replace placeholder credential bytes in Payment-Authorization header with: base64url(groth16Proof.pi_a || pi_b || pi_c) + ':' + base64url(pubSignals). Add buildPaymentAuthProof(agentCredential, paymentAmount, recipientAddress, chainId) helper that calls snarkjs groth16 fullProve with the new circuit artifacts. Total header value stays under 512 bytes (Groth16 proof is ~256 bytes, pubSignals 6 x 32 = 192 bytes, base64 overhead ~30%). Wire into existing sendX402Payment() call site.

deliverables.name: Theseus agent demo script
deliverables.description: Single Node script: (1) create AgentCredential with FINANCIAL_SMALL bit set, (2) call buildPaymentAuthProof for a $0.10 USDC transfer to a mock DeFi vault on Base Sepolia, (3) POST the x402 request with the Bolyra proof in Payment-Authorization header, (4) x402 responder calls IdentityRegistry.isPaymentAuthorized() on Base Sepolia, (5) on success, log proof bytes, gas cost of verifyProof call (~280k gas), and nullifier hash. No Theseus node required — simulates Theseus agent behavior with a local keypair.
deliverables.reuses: existing demo pattern from examples/mcp-demo + payment-protocols adapter

spec.demo_description: Sunday call (June 29) shows: (1) live walkthrough of PaymentAuthPolicy.circom diff against AgentPolicy — the 4 new public inputs and the amount-cap constraint are readable in ~20 lines of Circom, (2) the existing @bolyra/payment-protocols demo with a MOCKED proof in the header to show the HTTP flow end-to-end, (3) architecture diagram: Theseus agent -> buildPaymentAuthProof() -> x402 header -> Base Sepolia IdentityRegistry.isPaymentAuthorized() -> DeFi protocol. The circuit is not yet compiled with real zkey — frame Sunday as 'here is the design and the working HTTP plumbing; the circuit artifacts are 3 days out.' The real working proof needs the 5-day build.
risks: Circuit trusted setup (snarkjs groth16 setup) for PaymentAuthPolicy takes 20-40 min on a laptop and must run to completion before any real proof is possible — if the constraint count exceeds 2^16, pot16.ptau is insufficient and a new pot18.ptau ceremony (~2h) is needed. Audit constraint count before starting setup.

Theseus EVM compatibility is unconfirmed. If Theseus L1 uses a custom VM rather than EVM, the PaymentAuthVerifier.sol Solidity contract cannot be deployed there — proof verification would have to happen on Base Sepolia as a settlement layer instead, adding a cross-chain message step that is out of scope for the Sunday demo.

x402 header size: spec allows up to 8KB but individual server implementations (Cloudflare Workers, AWS API GW) may enforce 4KB or 8KB header limits. Groth16 + pubSignals base64 is ~600 bytes — well within limit — but must verify the DeFi protocol's x402 responder does not reject non-standard headers.

Solo-founder context switch cost: circuit work (Circom) and Solidity work cannot be parallelized — the verifier contract depends on the compiled circuit. The 5-day estimate assumes sequential execution with no interruptions. If Theseus prep, sales calls, or other work intrudes, slide to 8 days.

snarkjs proving time in Node for PaymentAuthPolicy will be 5-15 seconds (comparable to AgentPolicy). For a demo this is acceptable but Theseus will ask about latency in production. Real answer: rapidsnark binary brings it to ~200ms; the existing bench_rapidsnark.js script already measures this for AgentPolicy and can be adapted.
spec.success_criteria: Running `node demo/theseus-payment-demo.js` produces: (a) a valid Groth16 proof for a $0.10 USDC payment with FINANCIAL_SMALL bit set, (b) Base Sepolia IdentityRegistry.isPaymentAuthorized() returns true in a live eth_call, (c) re-submitting the same proof (replay attack) returns false because the nullifier is recorded, (d) submitting a proof for $150 with only FINANCIAL_SMALL bit fails circuit constraint validation before proof generation completes.
spec.theseus_dependencies: 1. Confirmation that Theseus L1 is EVM-compatible or has a Groth16 verifier precompile — determines whether IdentityRegistry can deploy on Theseus or must live on Base as settlement. 2. A testnet RPC endpoint or confirmation that Base Sepolia is the intended settlement chain for the Sunday demo. 3. An example Theseus agent keypair format (Ed25519 or secp256k1) — Bolyra's AgentCredential currently uses EdDSA (Baby Jubjub) for the credential commitment; if Theseus uses secp256k1 natively, the credential import path needs a hash bridge. 4. Any existing x402 responder they have running, or confirmation that we are mocking the DeFi protocol side for the demo.

tier3_findings: AXIS 1 — CONCERN: ERC-4337 session keys with spending limit modules (e.g., Kernel's SpendingLimit plugin) let Theseus engineers enforce per-agent spending caps on Base in under 2 weeks with zero ZKP expertise. Lit Protocol PKPs add programmable spending conditions off-circuit. The ZKP binding gets Bolyra's unique succinctness property (256-byte header proof) but Theseus can ship a functional demo without it. The 3-month self-build bar is plausible only for the full ZKP path; the 80% substitute is 2 weeks of Solidity.

AXIS 2 — CONCERN: The 'non-replayable' property cited as ZKP-exclusive is achievable with standard EdDSA nonce+signature from a Turnkey policy server. The real ZKP-only value is (a) proof in HTTP header without round-trip to policy server, and (b) no credential details revealed to verifier. But if Theseus agents are already identified by public keys on-chain, the privacy benefit of hiding their permission bits is marginal — their spend history is already public. The succinctness benefit (256-byte header vs. policy server call) is real but not survival-critical for an agent at $100K-funded scale.

AXIS 3 — CONCERN: Theseus has $100K in funding and is pre-mainnet. 'Theseus agents spending on Base DeFi' is currently hypothetical — no mainnet agents, no confirmed Base MCP integration on Theseus's side. The use case is structurally correct but operationally it's a demo scenario, not a 12-month operational need. At Theseus's current scale, even testnet DeFi interactions are developer experiments. The need only becomes pressing at hundreds of agents with real funds, which Theseus's funding trajectory doesn't support in 12 months.

AXIS 4 — FAIL: The proposal requires a new PaymentAuthPolicy.circom circuit, new trusted setup (zkey/vkey generation on pot16.ptau), a new Solidity verifier deployed on Base, and a new IdentityRegistry view function — Bolyra's own estimate is 3-4 days. Sunday demo is 1-2 days away. This cannot be delivered as a production proof for Sunday; it can only be mocked. Additionally, Theseus's execution environment is unconfirmed as EVM-compatible. If Theseus runs a custom VM, verification of the Groth16 proof on their chain requires implementing a custom Groth16 verifier — a months-long effort that completely breaks the integration model.

AXIS 5 — PASS: x402 payment authorization is not Theseus-specific. The PaymentAuthPolicy.circom circuit binding amount+chain_id+expiry to permission bits is directly applicable to any agent economy player using x402 (AWS-covered, Coinbase-built, broadly adopted). The circuit, Solidity verifier, and @bolyra/payment-protocols extension all land on Bolyra's roadmap regardless of Theseus's outcome. This axis cleanly passes.
tier3_summary: The circuit concept is technically valid and genuinely reusable, but it fails the integration timeline axis (new trusted setup cannot be real for Sunday), faces credible substitutes on axes 1-2, and addresses a hypothetical rather than operational need for a $100K pre-mainnet team — CONDITIONAL on scoping Sunday to a mock-proof demo and deferring the real circuit build to post-call if Theseus confirms EVM compatibility and live DeFi agent demand.
concerns: AXIS 1 — CONCERN: ERC-4337 session keys with spending limit modules (e.g., Kernel's SpendingLimit plugin) let Theseus engineers enforce per-agent spending caps on Base in under 2 weeks with zero ZKP expertise. Lit Protocol PKPs add programmable spending conditions off-circuit. The ZKP binding gets Bolyra's unique succinctness property (256-byte header proof) but Theseus can ship a functional demo without it. The 3-month self-build bar is plausible only for the full ZKP path; the 80% substitute is 2 weeks of Solidity.

AXIS 2 — CONCERN: The 'non-replayable' property cited as ZKP-exclusive is achievable with standard EdDSA nonce+signature from a Turnkey policy server. The real ZKP-only value is (a) proof in HTTP header without round-trip to policy server, and (b) no credential details revealed to verifier. But if Theseus agents are already identified by public keys on-chain, the privacy benefit of hiding their permission bits is marginal — their spend history is already public. The succinctness benefit (256-byte header vs. policy server call) is real but not survival-critical for an agent at $100K-funded scale.

AXIS 3 — CONCERN: Theseus has $100K in funding and is pre-mainnet. 'Theseus agents spending on Base DeFi' is currently hypothetical — no mainnet agents, no confirmed Base MCP integration on Theseus's side. The use case is structurally correct but operationally it's a demo scenario, not a 12-month operational need. At Theseus's current scale, even testnet DeFi interactions are developer experiments. The need only becomes pressing at hundreds of agents with real funds, which Theseus's funding trajectory doesn't support in 12 months.

AXIS 4 — FAIL: The proposal requires a new PaymentAuthPolicy.circom circuit, new trusted setup (zkey/vkey generation on pot16.ptau), a new Solidity verifier deployed on Base, and a new IdentityRegistry view function — Bolyra's own estimate is 3-4 days. Sunday demo is 1-2 days away. This cannot be delivered as a production proof for Sunday; it can only be mocked. Additionally, Theseus's execution environment is unconfirmed as EVM-compatible. If Theseus runs a custom VM, verification of the Groth16 proof on their chain requires implementing a custom Groth16 verifier — a months-long effort that completely breaks the integration model.

AXIS 5 — PASS: x402 payment authorization is not Theseus-specific. The PaymentAuthPolicy.circom circuit binding amount+chain_id+expiry to permission bits is directly applicable to any agent economy player using x402 (AWS-covered, Coinbase-built, broadly adopted). The circuit, Solidity verifier, and @bolyra/payment-protocols extension all land on Bolyra's roadmap regardless of Theseus's outcome. This axis cleanly passes.

title: Live AgentPolicy proof on Base Sepolia as Theseus agent credential primitive
description: Bolyra's AgentPolicy circuit (PLONK, deployed at 0x28b99c48f19F1680a7D88733ffeb9AFbcB3B95Db on Base Sepolia) already does exactly what Theseus needs for agent credential verification: EdDSA operator signature over a credential commitment, 8-bit permission bitmask with cumulative enforcement, selective disclosure of required scope bits, and expiry gating — all proven on-chain in a single transaction. On Sunday's call we run a live terminal demo: spin up an agent credential with permissions 0b00000111 (READ+WRITE+FINANCIAL_SMALL), generate a PLONK proof, and call IdentityRegistry.verifyHandshake() on Base Sepolia in real time. This is not a slide — it's a working primitive Theseus can fork today. The signal is Theseus's 'no human key path' constraint: their agents need programmatic credential issuance and scope enforcement without a human signing off at runtime, which is exactly what AgentPolicy delivers.

rationale.agent_need: The 'no human key path' structural argument is sound and maps cleanly to AgentPolicy, but the primary source (theseuschain.com) was fetch-blocked so the constraint is asserted from provided context rather than independently verified documentation or community citation.
rationale.zkp_edge: Lit Protocol (MPC attestations, no on-chain scope bitmask) and Turnkey (server-side JSON, operator-trusted) are named with structural failure modes identified, but the argument that a simple on-chain ACL contract couldn't serve Theseus equally well is underdeveloped.
rationale.primitive_readiness: Strongest dimension — exact circuit (AgentPolicy PLONK), deployed contract address, specific SDK methods, and scoped new work (verifyAgentCredential ~80 lines, proveAgentOnly 1 day) are all named with realistic day-level estimates.
rationale.partnership_leverage: Circom/ZKP expertise gap is identified, but the case for why Theseus would choose Bolyra over Lit Protocol's existing agent PKP product is not made, and Theseus team's actual crypto background is unassessed.

evidence.summary: Theseus is an agent-native L1 with a hard 'no human key path' constraint: agents hold their own keys, manage balances, persist on-chain, and require verifiable AI execution — no human signs off at runtime.

Lit's Programmable Key Pairs (PKPs) give agents threshold-custody of ECDSA/EdDSA keys with Lit Action conditions for access control, but proofs are MPC attestations, not ZKPs — no on-chain scope bitmask or expiry circuit enforcement.

Turnkey provides API-driven non-custodial agent wallets (EdDSA/ECDSA), but authorization policies are server-side JSON rules with no ZKP proof — an operator-trusted model, not a trustless on-chain primitive.

Privy is human-facing embedded wallet infra; its server wallet feature can sign on behalf of agents but has no delegation circuit or on-chain scope enforcement — not designed for agent-rooted trust chains.

Polygon ID uses Circom-based ZKP Verifiable Credentials (BabyJubJub signatures, Groth16 proofs) deployed on Polygon PoS — the closest deployed analogue to Bolyra AgentPolicy, but the credential model is human/entity-centric and has no cumulative 8-bit permission bitmask or agent delegation circuit.
Semaphore v4 (which Bolyra HumanUniqueness already reuses) proves group membership without revealing identity, but is human-uniqueness scoped — no agent credential or permission-bitmask model exists.

Fetch.ai uses Cosmos DID-style agent identity with WASM execution; credentials are PKI-signed DIDComm messages — no ZKP proving, no cumulative permission bits, and no EVM-compatible on-chain verifier.
Autonolas runs multi-chain agent services using on-chain service NFTs for identity; no ZKP for credential proofs, authorization is ACL-based on-chain.
SingularityNET uses on-chain service registries and AGIX-gated access; identity is wallet-address-based with no ZKP or delegation circuit.

Emerging IETF draft for AI agent authentication proposes OAuth 2.0 extension with agent-specific claims; no ZKP proof requirement — leaves on-chain verifiable scope enforcement as an open problem that Bolyra AgentPolicy directly fills.

evidence.source: W3C DID + Verifiable Credentials spec — w3.org
evidence.summary: W3C VCs define a credential envelope and selective disclosure model (BBS+ or SD-JWT) but no cumulative permission bitmask semantics or ZKP circuit-enforced scope narrowing for agent delegation.

ERC-4337 smart account abstraction lets agents own accounts with custom validation logic, but does not define a credential issuance primitive or scope-bitmask ZKP — Bolyra AgentPolicy could plug in as the validation circuit for AA-based agent accounts.

Orange DAO (YC alumni) backed Theseus at $100K pre-seed; their typical portfolio companies at this stage are pre-mainnet with raw keypair identity — no mature identity stack, making early primitive partnerships high-leverage.

mapped_primitives.usage: Primary primitive. EdDSA operator signature over credential commitment, 8-bit cumulative permission bitmask, selective disclosure of requiredScopeMask, and expiry enforcement map 1:1 to Theseus's 'no human key path at runtime' constraint. Operator signs once at issuance; the circuit proves capability at runtime without any human involvement.

On-chain PLONK verifier at 0x28b99c48f19F1680a7D88733ffeb9AFbcB3B95Db. Theseus's verifier contract or relayer calls verifyProof() directly for standalone agent credential checks without requiring the paired Groth16 human proof.

verifyHandshake() currently enforces atomic dual-proof (Groth16 + PLONK). For the Sunday demo, this is fine — pair a placeholder human identity with the agent credential. For production Theseus integration, a standalone verifyAgentCredential() entry point is needed to skip the HumanUniqueness side.

createAgentCredential(modelHash, operatorKey, 0b00000111, expiry) issues the credential; proveHandshake() generates the PLONK proof. The demo script is 3 SDK calls. For Theseus, a proveAgentOnly() path that skips HumanIdentity construction removes an unnecessary dependency.

Optional but high-value for Theseus: sub-agent spawning with scope narrowing (e.g., orchestrator agent delegates READ_DATA+FINANCIAL_SMALL to a child without granting WRITE_DATA). The 3-hop chain limit and on-chain nullifier replay protection address Theseus's multi-agent orchestration patterns.

new_components_needed.description: IdentityRegistry.verifyAgentCredential(agentProof, requiredScopeMask, nonce) — standalone PLONK-only verification path that skips the Groth16 human side. Reuses existing PlonkVerifier and agentTree root history buffer. Emits AgentVerified event for Theseus's relayer to index.

proveAgentOnly(credential, requiredScopeMask, options) in @bolyra/sdk — wraps AgentPolicy witness generation + PLONK proof without constructing a HumanIdentity. Eliminates the paired-proof mental model for pure-agent consumers like Theseus.

theseus-bolyra demo script: 50-line Node.js terminal demo for Sunday's call. createAgentCredential with permissions=0b00000111, generateProof, broadcast to Base Sepolia, print tx hash and on-chain AgentVerified event. Zero slides.

@bolyra/theseus adapter package (post-demo): thin wrapper exposing issueCredential(agentConfig) + verifyOnChain(proof, scopeMask) in Theseus's expected interface shape, with Base Sepolia RPC config baked in.

theseus_integration_surface: Three-layer surface: (1) Contract — Theseus's relayer or verifier contract calls IdentityRegistry.verifyAgentCredential() on Base Sepolia (or PlonkVerifier.verifyProof() directly if they prefer raw verification); events are indexable for their agent registry. (2) SDK — Theseus's agent runtime calls @bolyra/sdk proveAgentOnly() to generate PLONK proofs on the fly; credential issuance stays off-chain via createAgentCredential(). (3) Optional DID layer — agent identities registered as did:bolyra:<agentCommitment> with W3C DID method, enabling interop with any DID-aware trust registry Theseus already integrates with. The Sunday demo covers layer 1+2 end-to-end in a single terminal session.

deliverables.name: theseus-demo.js
deliverables.description: 50-line Node.js terminal script: createAgentCredential(modelHash, operatorKey, 0b00000111, expiry) → proveHandshake (paired with placeholder human identity) → IdentityRegistry.verifyHandshake() on Base Sepolia → log tx hash. Pre-generates and caches the PLONK proof to avoid 20-30s live latency, replays cached proof against fresh nonce on the call.
deliverables.reuses: @bolyra/sdk createAgentCredential + proveHandshake, existing PlonkVerifier at 0x28b99c48f19F1680a7D88733ffeb9AFbcB3B95Db, IdentityRegistry on Base Sepolia

deliverables.name: IdentityRegistry.verifyAgentCredential()
deliverables.description: New entry point on IdentityRegistry that accepts (agentProof, requiredScopeMask, nonce) and calls PlonkVerifier.verifyProof() directly — no Groth16 HumanUniqueness side. Reuses existing agentTree root history buffer and nonce deduplication. Emits AgentVerified(agentNullifier, scopeMask, expiry) for Theseus relayer indexing. ~80 lines of Solidity.
deliverables.reuses: existing PlonkVerifier, agentTree root history buffer, nonce registry — new function only

deliverables.name: proveAgentOnly() SDK method
deliverables.description: New export in @bolyra/sdk: proveAgentOnly(credential, requiredScopeMask, options) that runs AgentPolicy witness generation + snarkjs PLONK prove without constructing a HumanIdentity. Returns { proof, publicSignals } compatible with verifyAgentCredential(). Eliminates paired-proof mental model for pure-agent consumers.
deliverables.reuses: AgentPolicy.circom witness generation, existing PLONK .zkey artifacts in circuits/build/

deliverables.name: bolyra-theseus integration spec
deliverables.description: 1-page technical spec covering: (1) credential issuance flow — Theseus operator calls createAgentCredential() off-chain, commits agentLeaf to on-chain tree; (2) runtime verification flow — Theseus relayer calls verifyAgentCredential() per agent action; (3) delegation flow — orchestrator agent issues sub-agent credential via Delegation circuit with scope narrowing; (4) nullifier replay protection design. Doubles as leave-behind for Theseus eng team.

deliverables.name: Hardhat integration test for verifyAgentCredential
deliverables.description: Hardhat test that deploys updated IdentityRegistry, issues a credential with permissions=0b00000111, calls proveAgentOnly() (mock proof mode), and asserts verifyAgentCredential() emits AgentVerified with correct scopeMask and expiry. Gates the contract change — no ship without this passing.
deliverables.reuses: existing Hardhat test fixtures in contracts/test/, mock proof helpers

spec.demo_description: Live Base Sepolia terminal demo (no slides). Show: (1) createAgentCredential call with permissions=0b00000111 — credential commitment logged to console; (2) PLONK proof loaded from pre-generated cache, fresh nonce bound in <1s; (3) ethers.js call to IdentityRegistry.verifyHandshake() on Base Sepolia — tx hash returned in ~15s; (4) tx link opened in Basescan showing AgentVerified event with scopeMask=7 and expiry. Fallback: if RPC is flaky, replay a pre-recorded asciinema cast with the actual tx hash for Basescan verification. Day 1 is demo script only — this is ready Saturday night.
risks: Base Sepolia RPC instability during live call — mitigated by pre-generating proof + having Basescan tx link ready as fallback
Theseus runs a non-EVM custom VM — if confirmed before Sunday, pivot demo to showing the SDK + proof artifacts as portable primitives Theseus can wrap in their own verifier
snarkjs PLONK prover takes 20-30s on demo machine — pre-cache proof, bind fresh nonce only (milliseconds), avoid live proving on the call
Theseus eng is pre-mainnet with no stable contract environment — don't commit to a Theseus-side integration contract until they have a testnet
Solo bandwidth: verifyAgentCredential contract + test takes ~1.5 days total — do not start this until demo script is confirmed working
spec.success_criteria: Sunday demo produces a real Base Sepolia tx hash with AgentVerified event showing scopeMask=7, expiry in the future, and no human-side proof required. Theseus team can fork the demo script and run it against their own operatorKey within 30 minutes of the call.
spec.theseus_dependencies: No hard dependencies for Sunday demo — runs entirely on Bolyra's existing Base Sepolia deployment. For production integration: (1) confirmation that Theseus is EVM-compatible or has an EVM bridge; (2) their testnet RPC endpoint and chain ID for deploying a Theseus-side IdentityRegistry fork; (3) their agent key format (confirming EdDSA BabyJubJub or specifying alternate curve); (4) their relayer indexer schema so AgentVerified event fields match what they consume.

tier3_findings: AXIS 1 — CONCERN: Theseus cannot replicate AgentPolicy in <3 months without ZKP expertise (Circom circuit design, PLONK trusted-setup-free ceremony, on-chain verifier generation are genuine barriers), BUT a simpler non-ZKP path exists: on-chain ACL contract (Solidity mapping of agent address → uint8 bitmask) with OpenZeppelin AccessControl achieves 80% of the scope-enforcement value in 2 weeks. The card never closes this gap. The ZKP moat is real for 'prove scope without revealing credential' but that specific property is never shown to be operationally required by Theseus at launch.

AXIS 2 — FAIL: The ZKP advantage is selective disclosure of scope bits without revealing the full credential and without the operator signing at runtime. But the card does not establish that Theseus agents need to hide their permission set from the services they call. If agents are transparent participants on an L1 ledger, an on-chain registry (agent_address → permission_bitmask) provides equivalent scope enforcement with zero proof-generation latency, zero PLONK verification gas overhead, and trivially auditable state. The card mentions Turnkey and Lit as lacking ZKP but never explains why ZKP's marginal property (credential privacy) has economic or security value in Theseus's specific architecture. This axis is not defended.

AXIS 3 — CONCERN: Theseus is pre-mainnet, funded at $100K, with no production agents. The card names the use case class ('agents proving scope without human sign-off at runtime') but names zero specific agent types, zero specific functions that fail without AgentPolicy, and zero launch-day scenarios. The 'no human key path' constraint is about wallet custody, not proof-based credential scoping — these are conflated. The need for ZKP-based scope proofs likely only emerges at significant agent volume and interaction complexity, neither of which exists today.

AXIS 4 — FAIL: Critical blocker unaddressed. The Theseus context explicitly lists 'What's the on-chain execution environment? (EVM-compatible? Custom VM?)' as an open question. If Theseus runs a custom non-EVM L1 — plausible for an agent-native chain optimizing for AI execution — then the PLONK verifier at 0x28b99c48f19F1680a7D88733ffeb9AFbcB3B95Db cannot be deployed. Implementing a Groth16/PLONK verifier in a custom VM from scratch is a multi-month effort that would dwarf any partnership value. The card treats Base Sepolia deployment as proof of Theseus-readiness; it is not. The proving side also requires agents to run snarkjs/rapidsnark (a Node.js binary), which may be incompatible with Theseus's agent execution environment.

AXIS 5 — PASS: The Sunday demo consumes no new Theseus-specific engineering — it uses the already-deployed AgentPolicy contract, existing PLONK verifier, and ~80 lines of new SDK glue. If Theseus disappears, Bolyra's sunk cost is one demo call. The primitives (circuit, verifier, SDK methods) are chain-agnostic and serve Bolyra's broader roadmap for any agent-native platform. This axis is clean.

DEMO VS INTEGRATION DISTINCTION: The Sunday live demo itself is low-risk and appropriate — showing existing working infrastructure costs nothing and creates visibility. The adversarial concern is against positioning AgentPolicy as 'the primitive Theseus should adopt' before verifying EVM compatibility and before establishing that standard crypto is insufficient. The call should be diagnostic (discover Theseus VM, current auth approach, agent count) rather than pitching ZKP as the solution to a problem not yet confirmed.
tier3_summary: Demo the primitive on Sunday but treat the call as discovery — two hard FAILs (standard crypto likely suffices at pre-mainnet scale, EVM compatibility unverified) mean the integration premise is unvalidated and pitching AgentPolicy as Theseus's credential primitive is premature.
