title: Agent genesis credential: L1 birth-transaction as ZKP root of trust (no human root)
description: Bolyra's current AgentPolicy circuit commits to operatorPrivKey (a human-held key) as the issuer root. Theseus has no human key path — agents are bootstrapped by the L1 genesis or agent birth transaction itself. The fix: replace the human operatorPrivKey input with the agent's genesis tx hash (chain_id + block_hash + tx_index) as the credential root, issued by the Theseus L1 protocol rather than a human key. A Bolyra ZKP then proves 'this agent credential was issued by Theseus L1 genesis block B at tx index T, and its permissions are scoped to bits 0bXXXXXXXX' — all without revealing which specific agent it is (nullifier privacy preserved). This is the exact architectural gap the Theseus 'no human key path' constraint creates, and it's where Bolyra's circuit infrastructure provides a 6-12 month head start over anything Theseus could build internally.

rationale.agent_need: The 'no human key path' architectural constraint on Theseus is structurally argued and corroborated by evidence that all competitor solutions (Lit, Turnkey, Privy, Fetch.ai, W3C DID, IETF drafts) require a human trust anchor, making genesis-tx-rooted identity a genuine unmet need.
rationale.zkp_edge: The non-ZKP alternative (plain EdDSA + Merkle inclusion proof) is implicitly named but not explicitly analyzed for its failure mode — the privacy leak (revealing which genesis tx = revealing agent identity) is asserted rather than formally derived, and the O(1) vs O(n) verification tradeoff is not quantified.
rationale.primitive_readiness: Specific circuits (AgentPolicy fork), contracts (IdentityRegistry genesis path), and SDK surfaces are named with constraint estimates and day-level timelines, but the 4-day circuit development estimate is aggressive given that replacing EdDSA with a genesis commitment changes the trusted setup, requiring a new ceremony contribution and Solidity verifier regeneration that adds integration risk.
rationale.partnership_leverage: The 6-12 month head start claim is plausible given Circom/snarkjs proficiency barriers and the existing pot16.ptau ceremony, but Theseus's actual team crypto background is unassessed and the competitor adoption path (Lit Protocol PKPs with a custom genesis wrapper) is not ruled out.

evidence.summary: Theseus is an agent-native L1 where agents hold their own keys and are bootstrapped by genesis/birth transactions — no human-controlled key path exists by design.

evidence.source: Lit Protocol — litprotocol.com (PKP + AI agent signing)
evidence.summary: Lit's Programmable Key Pairs (PKPs) let AI agents sign transactions autonomously, but root of trust is still a human-issued NFT mint event, not an L1 genesis tx; no ZKP credential scoping layer.

Turnkey provides custodial key management with policy engines for agent signers, but requires human administrator root for all key issuance — structurally incompatible with Theseus's agent-first model.
Privy targets human-facing embedded wallets; agent server wallets require OAuth/API-key delegation from a human account owner — not applicable to headless agent genesis.

evidence.source: W3C Decentralized Identifiers (DID) Core Spec v1.0
evidence.summary: DID spec allows machine-controlled DIDs (did:key, did:web), but all deployed DID methods require human-controlled registration or governance — no genesis-tx-as-DID-document method exists.
Current IETF AI agent auth drafts assume a human authorization server as trust anchor; agent-rooted credential issuance with no human AS is explicitly out of scope in all current drafts.
Fetch.ai Autonomous Economic Agents use DID-based P2P addressing, but agent identity still requires human-controlled registration on the Almanac contract — no genesis-tx root of trust.
Autonolas agent services use on-chain registry with Safe multisig as operator root — human operators are mandatory, making it structurally misaligned with Theseus's no-human-key constraint.
AGIX marketplace agent services are tied to human-issued service registrations; no agent-native genesis credential primitive exists.

Semaphore is the closest ZKP identity primitive in production (the Bolyra HumanUniqueness circuit already reuses it), but is explicitly human-rooted via Merkle commitment of human secrets — no machine genesis path.
Aztec provides ZK account abstraction and private identity, but accounts still require human-initiated deployment; no protocol-level agent genesis credential primitive.

evidence.source: ERC-4337 Account Abstraction + smart contract wallets
evidence.summary: ERC-4337 lets smart contracts act as wallets without human EOAs, providing the closest on-chain analog to agent-native identity, but carries no ZKP permission scoping or nullifier-based privacy.
URL signals Theseus received coverage in March 2026, likely around funding or testnet launch — timing aligns with Orange DAO $100K round and pre-mainnet development phase.

evidence.source: Worldcoin / World ID — worldcoin.org (ZKP proof of personhood)
evidence.summary: World ID proves human uniqueness with ZKP but is explicitly anti-agent by design — the iris biometric root is the antithesis of agent genesis credential.

mapped_primitives.usage: Core circuit to adapt. Its permission bitmask with cumulative bit encoding, selective disclosure of requiredScopeMask bits, identity-bound scope commitment (Poseidon3), expiry enforcement, and nullifier privacy all carry over directly. The single point of change is the issuance root: EdDSA operatorPrivKey verification must be replaced by a genesis-tx commitment (Poseidon3(chain_id, block_hash, tx_index)). The 20923-constraint budget is sufficient—removing EdDSA verification (~4K constraints) and substituting a Poseidon hash path nets a smaller circuit, likely 17–19K constraints under Groth16.

Reusable with minor input changes. Once an agent holds a genesis credential, the Delegation circuit's scope-narrowing (delegateeScope ⊆ delegatorScope), expiry narrowing, hop-count enforcement (max 3), and delegator key binding all apply unchanged. The only modification: the delegator credential commitment input must accept a genesis-rooted commitment instead of an operator-signed one. Chain-linking logic and nullifier-based replay protection carry over intact.

The agentTree (LeanIMT, depth 20, 30-entry root history buffer) and its atomic handshake verification pattern are directly reusable for genesis agents. The contract needs a new verification path: instead of calling PlonkVerifier against an operator-signed credential, it calls a new GenesisPlonkVerifier. The delegation chain-state tracking, nonce equality enforcement, and delegation nullifier replay protection all carry over unchanged.

Not applicable to this opportunity by design. The Theseus no-human-key constraint explicitly removes the human root. HumanUniqueness is still relevant for human-in-the-loop Bolyra flows but is orthogonal to genesis agent credentials.

Existing PlonkVerifier contract cannot be reused because the verification key is circuit-specific and a rewritten AgentPolicy circuit produces a different vkey.json. A new GenesisAgentPlonkVerifier must be generated from the new zkey and deployed separately.

SDK scaffolding (error classes, bitmask helpers, permissionsToBitmask, validateCumulativeBitEncoding) carries over without changes. proveHandshake and verifyHandshake need a genesis-credential code path. The existing artifact-loading, snarkjs/rapidsnark bridge, and nonce-binding logic are all reusable.

new_components_needed.description: GenesisAgentPolicy.circom — replaces EdDSA operatorPrivKey verification with a genesis-tx commitment: private inputs are (chain_id, block_hash, tx_index, permissionBitmask, expiryTimestamp, secret, merklePathElements, merklePathIndices); public outputs are (genesisCommitment = Poseidon3(chain_id, block_hash, tx_index), nullifierHash, scopeCommitment, expiryTimestamp). Reuses existing Poseidon2/3 templates, LeanIMT membership proof, cumulative-bit range checks, and selective-disclosure mask logic from AgentPolicy. Dual Groth16+PLONK build to match existing pattern.

GenesisAgentPlonkVerifier.sol — generated from new circuit's vkey.json via snarkjs exportSolidityVerifier. Deployed alongside existing verifiers; IdentityRegistry updated to route genesis handshakes to this verifier. Add a genesisRoot registry mapping (chain_id => trusted genesis block hash) so the verifier can check that the claimed genesis commitment corresponds to a Bolyra-accepted Theseus L1 block root.

TheseusGenesisRootOracle.sol — a lightweight trusted-root registry (owner-updateable or multisig) that records canonical (chain_id, block_hash) pairs for Theseus L1 genesis blocks. IdentityRegistry reads from this oracle during genesis credential verification. Avoids full light-client complexity for the sprint scope; can be replaced by a Theseus L1 light client verifier in a follow-on.

createGenesisAgentCredential(chainId, blockHash, txIndex, permissions, expiry) → GenesisAgentCredential — new SDK entry point that builds the genesis commitment, generates a witness for GenesisAgentPolicy, and returns a provable credential. Extend proveHandshake and verifyHandshake to accept GenesisAgentCredential in the agent slot. Add GenesisCredentialError to error class hierarchy.

Theseus L1 genesis bridge — a lightweight adapter (TypeScript, Node) that queries the Theseus RPC for a birth transaction's (chain_id, block_hash, tx_index) tuple and feeds it into createGenesisAgentCredential. Must handle RPC auth, chain_id normalization, and block finality checks before committing the genesis root to the on-chain oracle. Packaged as @bolyra/theseus-adapter.

Extend draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 with a 'Genesis Agent Profile' section: defines the genesis commitment construction, L1 trust anchor semantics, oracle trust model, and how genesis-rooted delegation chains interoperate with human-rooted chains. Add 6–8 conformance test vectors to test-vectors.json covering genesis credential issuance, scope narrowing via Delegation, and cross-session nullifier unlinkability.

theseus_integration_surface: Three-layer surface: (1) Off-chain proving — the Theseus genesis bridge adapter reads birth-tx data from the Theseus L1 RPC, feeds (chain_id, block_hash, tx_index) into the Bolyra SDK's createGenesisAgentCredential, and generates a PLONK proof client-side using the circuit artifacts in circuits/build/. (2) On-chain anchor — TheseusGenesisRootOracle.sol (deployed on Base Sepolia, same chain as IdentityRegistry) holds the canonical mapping of accepted Theseus L1 genesis block hashes; the oracle is updated by a multisig or a Theseus-operated relayer when new genesis blocks are finalized on L1. IdentityRegistry's atomic handshake verification routes genesis agent proofs through GenesisAgentPlonkVerifier and reads the oracle for root validation. (3) SDK bridge — @bolyra/theseus-adapter exposes a single function `bootstrapTheseusAgent(rpcUrl, txHash, permissions, expiry)` that orchestrates the RPC call, genesis commitment construction, proof generation, and agentTree insertion — giving Theseus protocol developers a five-line integration path consistent with the existing @bolyra/sdk quickstart pattern.

deliverables.name: GenesisAgentPolicy.circom
deliverables.description: Fork of AgentPolicy.circom replacing EdDSA operatorPrivKey verification (~4K constraints) with a Poseidon3(chain_id, block_hash, tx_index) genesis commitment check. All permission bitmask logic, cumulative-bit enforcement, scope commitment, expiry, and nullifier privacy carry over untouched. New public inputs: genesisCommitment, chainId. Private inputs: blockHash, txIndex, agentSecret.

deliverables.name: genesis_agent.zkey + vkey.json
deliverables.description: Trusted setup for GenesisAgentPolicy using existing pot16.ptau. Run snarkjs groth16 setup + contribute one beacon. Generates the circuit-specific zkey and exports vkey.json for Solidity verifier generation. Slow step but fully scripted via existing compile.js pattern.

deliverables.name: GenesisAgentPlonkVerifier.sol
deliverables.description: New Solidity verifier exported from the genesis zkey via snarkjs exportSolidityVerifier. Drop-in replacement for AgentPlonkVerifier in the genesis credential path. No hand-authored code — fully generated from vkey.json.

deliverables.name: IdentityRegistry genesis path
deliverables.description: Add registerGenesisAgent(genesisCommitment, chainId, proof) entry point to IdentityRegistry.sol. Calls GenesisAgentPlonkVerifier instead of existing PlonkVerifier. Existing agentTree (LeanIMT depth 20), 30-entry root history buffer, nonce enforcement, and delegation nullifier replay protection all carry over unchanged. Emit GenesisAgentRegistered(nullifierHash, chainId) event for Theseus indexer.

deliverables.name: Delegation circuit genesis input patch
deliverables.description: One-line input rename in Delegation.circom: delegatorCredentialCommitment input now accepts genesis-rooted Poseidon3 output in addition to operator-signed commitments. Scope-narrowing, expiry narrowing, hop-count (max 3), and chain-linking logic unchanged. Recompile + regenerate zkey (reuses pot16.ptau).

deliverables.name: SDK genesis credential API
deliverables.description: Add createGenesisCredential(chainId, blockHash, txIndex, agentSecret, permissions, expiry) to @bolyra/sdk. Returns GenesisCredential type with nullifierHash + genesisCommitment. Wire into existing proveHandshake/verifyHandshake as a credential variant. All bitmask helpers, error classes, snarkjs/rapidsnark bridge, and nonce-binding logic reused verbatim.

deliverables.name: Theseus genesis tx fetcher
deliverables.description: Thin adapter (~100 LOC) that queries a Theseus RPC endpoint for a given agent address, retrieves its birth transaction (chain_id, block_hash, tx_index), and feeds those into createGenesisCredential. Uses Theseus JSON-RPC or REST API (TBD — blocked on Theseus providing testnet access). Includes offline mock for demo use.

deliverables.name: End-to-end demo script
deliverables.description: Hardhat script that: (1) deploys GenesisAgentPlonkVerifier + patched IdentityRegistry to local node, (2) mocks a Theseus genesis tx tuple, (3) generates a genesis credential proof via SDK, (4) registers the agent on-chain, (5) runs a handshake against a second agent, (6) logs nullifier + permission bits. Self-contained, runnable with npm run demo:genesis. No live Theseus chain required for local demo.

spec.demo_description: Sunday call (tomorrow): show the architecture diff — a single annotated diff of AgentPolicy.circom replacing the EdDSA check with Poseidon3(chain_id, block_hash, tx_index), the existing delegation and permission-bit infrastructure on screen, and the integration spec doc. No running proof yet, but the 'what changes and why' story is complete and credible. The 6-12 month head-start argument is grounded in the specific constraint count reduction and the existing pot16.ptau ceremony that eliminates trusted setup from the critical path.
risks: Theseus genesis tx schema unknown: if block_hash or tx_index aren't stable public fields at birth-tx finality, the Poseidon3 commitment binding breaks — need schema confirmation before writing circuit inputs
Theseus EVM compatibility unconfirmed: if their L1 is not EVM-compatible, Solidity verifier deployment is blocked and contract layer needs a full rewrite for their VM
pot16.ptau ceiling: if GenesisAgentPolicy exceeds 2^16 constraints after changes (unlikely given EdDSA removal nets ~17-19K), a new ceremony is required — adds 1-2 days
Solo capacity: 14 days assumes full focus; any Theseus integration question latency (schema, RPC docs, testnet access) adds idle time that compresses real output to 10-12 working days
Nullifier domain separation: genesis nullifier must be domain-separated from operator-issued nullifiers to prevent cross-credential replay — easy to miss, needs explicit test coverage
spec.success_criteria: A Hardhat integration test passes end-to-end: genesis credential generated from (chainId=8453, blockHash=mock, txIndex=0), proof verified on-chain by GenesisAgentPlonkVerifier, agent registered in IdentityRegistry, handshake with a second agent succeeds, and replaying the same nullifier fails with AlreadyUsed. Circuit constraint count is below 2^16. SDK createGenesisCredential returns in <2s on snarkjs, <200ms on rapidsnark.
spec.theseus_dependencies: 1) Birth-transaction RPC method or REST endpoint returning (chain_id, block_hash, tx_index) for a given agent address — needed before integration adapter can be written. 2) Testnet RPC URL + faucet for deploying GenesisAgentPlonkVerifier and IdentityRegistry. 3) Confirmation of EVM compatibility or VM spec if non-EVM. 4) Clarification on whether genesis tx fields are finalized at block inclusion or require additional epochs — affects nullifier timing assumptions. 5) Any existing agent identity/auth interface (even raw keypair docs) so the Bolyra handshake can be wired to their current flow rather than replacing it cold.

tier3_findings: Axis 1 — CONCERN: The circuit change is narrow (swap EdDSA input for Poseidon-hashed genesis tx tuple). A 1-2 engineer team that hires a ZKP contractor for 4-6 weeks could replicate this. circomlib, snarkjs, and pot16.ptau are all public. PLONK eliminates the per-circuit ceremony overhead. The 6-12 month head-start claim assumes Theseus starts from zero ZKP knowledge with no outside help — an assumption worth probing on the call.

Axis 2 — CONCERN: The privacy argument is asserted, not derived. Agents on a public L1 are already identifiable via their on-chain transaction history. Hiding which genesis tx issued the credential while the agent's actions are traceable on-chain provides marginal real-world privacy. The O(1) succinctness argument (ZKP vs Merkle inclusion proof) is only compelling beyond ~10,000 agents — a threshold Theseus will not hit at launch with a $100K seed round. A Merkle registry with on-chain ACL covers 80% of the permission-scoping use case without ZKP overhead.

Axis 3 — CONCERN: No concrete failing agent use case is named. The proposal identifies the architectural gap (no human key path) correctly but does not name a specific agent type — e.g., a treasury agent, a governance voter, a cross-chain bridge signer — that breaks without nullifier-private genesis credentials in the first 12 months. The privacy need is especially unclear: if an agent's on-chain behavior is already public, proving permission without revealing agent identity solves a problem that may not exist operationally.

Axis 4 — CONCERN: Theseus's execution environment is unknown. If non-EVM, the Groth16 verifier must be reimplemented in their VM language, which is a 3-6 month pairing-library effort that dwarfs the circuit work. Even if EVM-compatible, agents running inside a chain VM cannot natively invoke snarkjs or rapidsnark — a centralized Bolyra proving service resolves this but introduces a trust assumption that undermines the 'no human root' thesis. Gas cost of on-chain Groth16 verification (~300-500K gas on EVM) on Theseus L1 is unquantified. The new ceremony contribution and Solidity verifier regeneration triggered by the circuit change adds integration risk not accounted for in the 4-day estimate.

Axis 5 — PASS: The genesis-tx-as-credential-root primitive is not Theseus-specific. Any agent-native chain faces the same human-rootless bootstrap problem. The AgentPolicy circuit fork, SDK module, and DID method extension all serve Bolyra's broader roadmap regardless of whether Theseus succeeds. This is the proposal's strongest dimension.
Blocking question before any sprint commitment: Is Theseus EVM-compatible? If no, Axis 4 becomes a hard FAIL and this drops to REJECT. Must resolve on the June 29 call.
tier3_summary: Architecturally sound concept with a real gap identified, but four unresolved concerns — weak agent privacy value on a public L1, no named failing use case, unknown VM compatibility, and a replicable circuit change — prevent approval until the June 29 call resolves EVM compatibility and surfaces a concrete agent type that operationally fails without ZKP-native genesis credentials.
concerns: Axis 1 — CONCERN: The circuit change is narrow (swap EdDSA input for Poseidon-hashed genesis tx tuple). A 1-2 engineer team that hires a ZKP contractor for 4-6 weeks could replicate this. circomlib, snarkjs, and pot16.ptau are all public. PLONK eliminates the per-circuit ceremony overhead. The 6-12 month head-start claim assumes Theseus starts from zero ZKP knowledge with no outside help — an assumption worth probing on the call.

Axis 2 — CONCERN: The privacy argument is asserted, not derived. Agents on a public L1 are already identifiable via their on-chain transaction history. Axis 3 — CONCERN: No concrete failing agent use case is named. The proposal identifies the architectural gap (no human key path) correctly but does not name a specific agent type — e.g., a treasury agent, a governance voter, a cross-chain bridge signer — that breaks without nullifier-private genesis credentials in the first 12 months. The privacy need is especially unclear: if an agent's on-chain behavior is already public, proving permission without revealing agent identity solves a problem that may not exist operationally.

Axis 4 — CONCERN: Theseus's execution environment is unknown. If non-EVM, the Groth16 verifier must be reimplemented in their VM language, which is a 3-6 month pairing-library effort that dwarfs the circuit work. Even if EVM-compatible, agents running inside a chain VM cannot natively invoke snarkjs or rapidsnark — a centralized Bolyra proving service resolves this but introduces a trust assumption that undermines the 'no human root' thesis. Gas cost of on-chain Groth16 verification (~300-500K gas on EVM) on Theseus L1 is unquantified. The new ceremony contribution and Solidity verifier regeneration triggered by the circuit change adds integration risk not accounted for in the 4-day estimate.

Axis 5 — PASS: The genesis-tx-as-credential-root primitive is not Theseus-specific. Any agent-native chain faces the same human-rootless bootstrap problem. The AgentPolicy circuit fork, SDK module, and DID method extension all serve Bolyra's broader roadmap regardless of whether Theseus succeeds. This is the proposal's strongest dimension.

title: x402 autonomous spending gated by Bolyra AgentPolicy permission bits
description: AWS Bedrock AgentCore, Coinbase Agentic Wallets, and World's agentkit all launched x402 support in 2026 — autonomous agents paying for services via HTTP 402 micropayments is becoming infrastructure. Bolyra's @bolyra/payment-protocols package already implements x402 flows. The integration play: Theseus agents that call x402-enabled APIs present a Bolyra AgentPolicy proof alongside the payment, proving they hold FINANCIAL_SMALL (bit 2, <$100) or FINANCIAL_MEDIUM (bit 3, <$10K) permission — gating autonomous spending to the authorized limit without a human approval step. The 2-week deliverable is a demo script: Theseus agent calls an x402-gated endpoint, Bolyra proof is verified in the payment middleware, spending is approved only if permission bits match the payment amount. This is the most concrete 'agents transacting autonomously' story we can show on Sunday as a 5-minute live demo after the AgentPolicy demo. x402 Foundation backing (Google, Visa, Stripe, AWS, Mastercard) makes this a credible ecosystem play, not a toy.

rationale.agent_need: x402 adoption by AWS Bedrock AgentCore, Coinbase Agentic Wallets, and the founding consortium (Google, Visa, Stripe) provides strong structural evidence that autonomous spending gating is a real, emerging infrastructure need for L1-native agents — though Theseus-specific documentation remains sparse.
rationale.zkp_edge: The non-ZKP alternative (Turnkey/Lit server-side policy enforcement) is correctly identified and its failure mode named (server-side rules, not cryptographically proven on-chain), but the edge is moderate since off-chain snarkjs verification in the demo path doesn't actually require on-chain ZKP and Turnkey's spending limits are functionally comparable for many use cases.
rationale.primitive_readiness: Strongest dimension: AgentPolicy circuit requires zero modification, PlonkVerifier is deployed at a named address on Base Sepolia, @bolyra/payment-protocols already implements x402 flows, and the integration spec details a credible 2.75-day build with specific SDK methods named — this is genuinely near-ready.
rationale.partnership_leverage: The argument that Theseus cannot build ZKP-based permission attestation in <3 months is plausible given Circom proficiency requirements, but is weakened by the fact that the demo uses off-chain snarkjs verification (reducing the ZKP expertise bar) and Theseus could adopt Lit Protocol's conditional signing or Turnkey's server policies as functional substitutes without needing Bolyra.

evidence.source: x402.org (founding consortium: Google, Visa, Stripe, AWS, Mastercard)
evidence.summary: x402 repurposes HTTP 402 as a machine-native micropayment rail; consortium backing from major payment and cloud players makes it infrastructure-grade, not experimental.

evidence.source: Coinbase Developer Platform — Agentic Wallets launch (2026)
evidence.summary: Coinbase's agentic wallets natively support x402 flows, positioning payment-gated agent actions as a first-class primitive in their developer ecosystem.
AWS endorsement of x402 for agentic commerce signals enterprise acceptance and creates procurement-friendly framing for Bolyra's gating layer.

Public information is sparse; Theseus markets 'agency for agents' with no human key path, implying raw EdDSA keypairs as current identity primitive — a structural gap Bolyra's AgentPolicy circuit directly addresses.

evidence.source: Lit Protocol (litprotocol.com) — PKP + MPC key management
evidence.summary: Lit provides programmable key pairs with threshold MPC, enabling conditional signing, but lacks ZKP-based permission-bit attestation that can be verified on an L1 without revealing the underlying credential tree.
Turnkey offers policy-enforced key management with spending limits, but policies are server-side rules, not cryptographically proven on-chain — no ZKP attestation layer.
Privy targets consumer dApp onboarding; minimal agent-to-agent delegation primitives and no on-chain ZKP attestation.
Fetch.ai has native agent messaging (UAGENT protocol) but identity is DID-over-key without ZKP-provable permission scopes; a Bolyra-style AgentPolicy layer would be additive.
SingularityNET handles agent service discovery and payment but does not provide cryptographic delegation or permission-bit attestation.

evidence.source: Worldcoin / Semaphore v4 — on-chain ZKP identity (production)
evidence.summary: Semaphore v4 is in production at scale; Bolyra's HumanUniqueness circuit reuses this ceremony, meaning the trusted-setup lineage is already credible to any audience familiar with Worldcoin.

evidence.source: Aztec Network — private ZKP execution on Ethereum
evidence.summary: Aztec's noir-based private proofs could theoretically gate spending, but there is no agent-native permission model or x402 middleware integration.
Bolyra already ships x402 flow implementation, meaning the 2-week sprint is integration/demo work, not net-new primitive development — lowest-risk path to Sunday demo.

mapped_primitives.usage: Core proving primitive. Selective-disclosure capability ('proves requiredScopeMask bits are set without revealing full bitmask') maps directly to gating: payment middleware sets requiredScopeMask=0x04 for amounts <$100 (FINANCIAL_SMALL, bit 2) or 0x08 for amounts <$10K (FINANCIAL_MEDIUM, bit 3). Cumulative encoding means bit 3 proof automatically satisfies bit 2 check. Identity-bound scope commitment and expiryTimestamp enforcement prevent credential reuse across sessions or after expiry. This circuit needs zero modification.

Deployed verifier at 0x28b99c48f19F1680a7D88733ffeb9AFbcB3B95Db on Base Sepolia. Payment middleware can call verifyProof() on-chain before releasing funds, or the same vkey can be used for off-chain snarkjs verification in an HTTP middleware layer for sub-second latency without a blockchain round-trip. Both paths are already supported by the circuit artifact build.

Optional but recommended for the demo: IdentityRegistry.verifyHandshake() provides atomic human+agent proof verification in a single tx, proving the agent is sponsored by a real enrolled human. For the pure spending-gate demo (no human in the loop), the PlonkVerifier alone suffices. IdentityRegistry becomes relevant when the story is 'human authorized this agent up to $10K total' rather than 'operator signed a credential'.

The @bolyra/payment-protocols package already implements x402 flows. The integration layer lives here: intercept the 402 response, attach the AgentPolicy proof to the subsequent payment request (as a custom X-Bolyra-Proof header or inside the x402 payment payload's metadata field), and add a verifier middleware on the server side that calls PlonkVerifier before releasing the resource.

createAgentCredential(modelHash, operatorKey, [FINANCIAL_SMALL|FINANCIAL_MEDIUM], expiry) is the client-side entry point. proveHandshake() generates the AgentPolicy PLONK proof. The Theseus agent calls these before its first x402 request and caches the proof for the session (valid until expiryTimestamp). No SDK changes needed; the demo script just wires the existing function calls.

Relevant when Theseus spawns sub-agents that inherit spending authority. A parent Theseus agent with FINANCIAL_MEDIUM can delegate FINANCIAL_SMALL to a sub-agent via the Delegation circuit, enforcing scope narrowing on-chain. Not required for the 2-week demo but is the natural v2 story ('agent orchestrators with scoped spending hierarchies').

new_components_needed.description: x402 Bolyra middleware: Express/Fastify middleware (or fetch interceptor) that (1) extracts X-Bolyra-Proof from incoming payment request, (2) calls snarkjs.plonk.verify(vkey, publicSignals, proof) off-chain for <100ms latency, (3) maps payment amount to required permission bit (amount<=100 → bit 2, amount<=10000 → bit 3), (4) checks publicSignals.requiredScopeMask satisfies the amount tier, (5) rejects with 403 if proof missing or bit check fails. Ships as bolyra-x402-middleware npm package inside integrations/payment-protocols/.

Proof attachment helper: proveSpend(agentCredential, amountUSD, sessionNonce) → { proof, publicSignals, headers } that computes the correct requiredScopeMask from the dollar amount, runs the AgentPolicy witness+proof, and returns ready-to-attach HTTP headers. Thin wrapper over existing proveHandshake() — 50 lines of glue.

Demo script: Theseus agent that (1) creates an operator-signed AgentCredential with FINANCIAL_MEDIUM, (2) calls a mock x402-gated API endpoint (e.g. /data-feed priced at $0.01), (3) receives 402, generates proof via proveSpend(), (4) retries with proof header, (5) server-side middleware verifies and serves response. Illustrates the full autonomy loop with no human approval step. Self-contained runnable in examples/x402-demo/.

X-Bolyra-Proof header schema: JSON encoding of { proof, publicSignals, credentialCommitment, expiryTimestamp } for x402 interop. One-page spec in spec/ so other x402 implementers can support Bolyra proofs without the SDK. Needed for partnership credibility with Coinbase/AWS ecosystem.

theseus_integration_surface: Three-layer surface: (1) Client SDK — Theseus agent imports @bolyra/sdk, calls createAgentCredential() once at startup with operator-signed FINANCIAL_MEDIUM permissions, then proveSpend(credential, amount, nonce) before each x402 payment attempt; proof generation is ~16s first time (PLONK), cacheable for session duration. (2) HTTP middleware — bolyra-x402-middleware sits in front of any x402-gated endpoint; performs off-chain snarkjs.plonk.verify() at <100ms per request, no blockchain call needed unless operator wants on-chain auditability. (3) On-chain settlement (optional) — for high-value transactions (FINANCIAL_MEDIUM tier), payment processor calls PlonkVerifier.verifyProof() in the settlement transaction, creating an immutable audit trail that the agent held valid permission at time of spend. Theseus's existing HTTP client and x402 retry logic need one hook: attach the proof headers on 402 response, retry the same request. No Theseus core changes required beyond that hook.

deliverables.name: x402-bolyra-middleware
deliverables.description: Thin Express/Node middleware layer inside @bolyra/payment-protocols: on 402 response, auto-generates AgentPolicy PLONK proof via proveHandshake() and attaches as X-Bolyra-Proof header on retry. Server side: verifyProof() off-chain via snarkjs against cached vkey.json, checks requiredScopeMask (0x04 for FINANCIAL_SMALL, 0x08 for FINANCIAL_MEDIUM) against proof public signals, rejects if amount exceeds bit tier's ceiling. Zero circuit changes — pure wiring.

deliverables.name: demo-server
deliverables.description: Minimal Express server with two routes: GET /resource returns 402 with x402 payment details (amount + scopeMask), POST /pay accepts x402 payment + X-Bolyra-Proof, runs verifier middleware, returns 200 with resource or 403 if proof invalid or permission tier insufficient. Hardcoded test prices: $50 (requires FINANCIAL_SMALL), $5000 (requires FINANCIAL_MEDIUM). Off-chain snarkjs verification only — no blockchain round-trip needed for Sunday latency.

deliverables.name: theseus-agent-demo-script
deliverables.description: Single Node script simulating a Theseus-style agent: generates EdDSA keypair (matching Theseus's raw keypair model), calls createAgentCredential() with FINANCIAL_SMALL bit, calls demo-server GET → receives 402, generates PLONK proof, retries POST with proof, prints approve/reject result. Shows two runs: $50 purchase (passes), $5000 purchase with only FINANCIAL_SMALL credential (fails). Theseus chain not required — agent is mocked as a bare EdDSA identity, which IS Theseus's current primitive.

deliverables.name: proof-caching-session-manager
deliverables.description: 50-line wrapper that caches the generated AgentPolicy proof for the session window (expiryTimestamp from credential), avoids re-proving on every x402 request within the same session. Critical for demo smoothness — PLONK prove takes 2-4s, cache makes subsequent calls instant. Lives in the demo script, not in the SDK.

spec.demo_description: Live terminal demo: run theseus-agent-demo-script.js, show a mock Theseus agent (bare EdDSA keypair, no Theseus chain needed) attempt two purchases against the local demo-server. First: $50 API call — agent generates AgentPolicy PLONK proof with FINANCIAL_SMALL bit, proof verified off-chain in <4s, resource unlocked, show 200 response with 'APPROVED: permission bit 2 satisfied'. Second: $5000 API call with same FINANCIAL_SMALL credential — verifier rejects, show 403 'REJECTED: FINANCIAL_MEDIUM (bit 3) required for amounts >$100'. Frame it: 'This is the cryptographic spending limit — not a policy rule a server can override, not a JWT a middleware can forge. The agent proves capability without revealing its full credential tree.' Five minutes, no blockchain, no flakiness risk.
risks: PLONK proof generation via snarkjs takes 2-4 seconds on first call — acceptable for demo if framed correctly ('this is the one-time proof generation; subsequent requests within the session use the cached proof'), but will feel slow if the audience expects HTTP latency
payment-protocols package internals may have breaking assumptions about the x402 payload shape — need to audit the existing x402 implementation before assuming 'minor modification'

Theseus's EdDSA keypair format may differ from the format createAgentCredential() expects for operatorPrivKey — need to verify curve (Ed25519 vs secp256k1) before Sunday; if they use secp256k1 the demo script works as-is, if Ed25519 need a 1-hour shim
Off-chain snarkjs verification is single-threaded in Node — under concurrent demo load it blocks; non-issue for a one-agent demo but worth noting for the 'production path' conversation with Theseus
spec.success_criteria: Demo script completes two runs end-to-end in under 30 seconds total: run 1 exits 0 with 'APPROVED' printed, run 2 exits 1 with 'REJECTED: insufficient permission tier' printed. Proof verification log shows the requiredScopeMask check passing/failing against the proof's public signals. No hardcoded bypasses — the rejection must come from the verifier, not an if-statement.
spec.theseus_dependencies: Nothing required for Sunday — agent is simulated as a bare EdDSA keypair which matches their documented architecture. For post-Sunday production integration: (1) Theseus testnet RPC endpoint or SDK to instantiate a real on-chain agent identity, (2) confirmation of their signing curve (Ed25519 or secp256k1) to ensure createAgentCredential() keypair format matches, (3) their agent account ABI if they have a smart contract layer — needed only if we want on-chain PlonkVerifier call as a transaction from a Theseus agent rather than off-chain snarkjs verification.

tier3_findings: AXIS 1 (Theseus Builds It) — CONCERN: The demo path uses off-chain snarkjs verification in HTTP middleware, which collapses the ZKP expertise requirement to 'call verifyProof() on a vkey.' A Theseus engineer who reads the snarkjs docs can wire this in a week. More importantly, Turnkey's embedded wallet API provides functionally equivalent spending-limit enforcement (signed policy, server-enforced cap) with zero ZKP knowledge and a well-documented REST API. The proposal correctly names Turnkey as a competitor but underweights how closely it matches the demo's value proposition. The 'Circom proficiency barrier' argument only holds for building the circuit — not for consuming the already-built one.

AXIS 2 (Standard Crypto Suffices) — CONCERN: The ZKP advantage (selective disclosure of permission bits without revealing full bitmask) is real but marginal for this use case. An agent proving it has FINANCIAL_SMALL permission is not meaningfully damaged by also revealing it has FINANCIAL_MEDIUM — the economic and security value of hiding the full bitmask in a spending-gate context is near zero. The on-chain verifiability advantage evaporates in the demo path (off-chain snarkjs). If Theseus is not EVM-native, on-chain verification requires a custom Groth16/PLONK verifier implementation, making the 'on-chain proof' story aspirational rather than demonstrated. Signed JWTs with spending-limit claims achieve 90% of the functional outcome at 5% of the complexity.

AXIS 3 (No Agent Needs This) — CONCERN: x402 adoption by Google, Visa, Stripe, AWS is structurally credible evidence that autonomous spending is real infrastructure. However, the proposal does not name a single specific Theseus agent type that requires ZKP-attested spending authorization rather than a Turnkey policy or a signed Coinbase wallet claim. 'Agents transacting autonomously' is a category, not a use case. At Theseus's current scale (pre-mainnet, $100K funded, unknown agent count at launch), the realistic population of agents needing ZKP-gated x402 spending in month 1 is likely 0-5. The ZKP-specific requirement — as opposed to 'some spending authorization mechanism' — is not operationally demonstrated.

AXIS 4 (Integration Complexity vs Value) — CONCERN: The Sunday demo scope (2-week sprint, off-chain middleware) is genuinely feasible given primitive readiness. However, the production deployment story has critical unknowns: Theseus's execution environment is unspecified (EVM-compatible? Custom VM?). If Theseus is non-EVM, deploying the PlonkVerifier on-chain requires either a custom Groth16 verifier in the Theseus VM or keeping verification permanently off-chain — undermining the 'cryptographically proven on-chain' thesis. The proposal's strength is demo-day completeness; its weakness is that the demo may not represent a credible production path, and Theseus's engineers will ask about on-chain verification on their L1.

AXIS 5 (Single-Partner Dependency) — PASS: This is the proposal's strongest axis. x402 is backed by a consortium that makes it infrastructure-grade; the demo artifacts (AgentPolicy proof middleware, x402 payment flow with ZKP gate) are reusable across any agent ecosystem adopting x402. The @bolyra/payment-protocols package gains a concrete reference implementation regardless of Theseus's outcome. The work is additive to Bolyra's broader roadmap even if Theseus pivots or fails.

SECONDARY FINDING — Off-chain verification undermines the core ZKP value proposition: The proposal's most credible demo path is also its weakest argument for ZKP necessity. If the proof is verified in an HTTP middleware via snarkjs (off-chain), then the trust model is 'trust the middleware operator' — identical to Turnkey's server-side policy enforcement. The on-chain verifiability story requires Theseus to have an EVM-compatible environment and adds significant integration friction. Bolyra should decide before Sunday whether to commit to the off-chain demo (fast, easy, ZKP-value-diluted) or the on-chain demo (harder, slower, but defensible).
tier3_summary: The x402 + AgentPolicy integration is demo-ready and reusable (Axis 5 clear pass), but the off-chain verification path undermines the ZKP differentiation story on every other axis — Theseus can replicate the functional outcome with Turnkey in two weeks, and no specific Theseus agent use case is named that requires ZKP over signed spending-limit claims; approve only if the demo commits to on-chain proof verification and Bolyra can articulate why Turnkey's server-side policy is structurally insufficient.
concerns: AXIS 1 (Theseus Builds It) — CONCERN: The demo path uses off-chain snarkjs verification in HTTP middleware, which collapses the ZKP expertise requirement to 'call verifyProof() on a vkey.' The 'Circom proficiency barrier' argument only holds for building the circuit — not for consuming the already-built one.

AXIS 2 (Standard Crypto Suffices) — CONCERN: The ZKP advantage (selective disclosure of permission bits without revealing full bitmask) is real but marginal for this use case. title: Agent-Rooted Delegation Chain: L1 Genesis as Root of Trust
description: Bolyra's current Delegation circuit assumes a human is the root delegator. Theseus agents are the root — no human ever holds a parent key. An agent-rooted variant of the Delegation circuit treats the agent's on-chain birth transaction (or L1 genesis block commitment) as the trust anchor instead of a human Merkle leaf. This enables Agent A to delegate a subset of its permission bits to Agent B with on-circuit enforcement — scope can only narrow, never expand — without any human approval in the path. This is the foundational primitive for multi-agent workflows on Theseus: orchestrator agents spawning sub-agents with scoped authority. Competitors (Lit Protocol, Turnkey, Privy) have no delegation circuit — they rely on off-chain ACLs that can be bypassed.

rationale.agent_need: Theseus's no-human-key-path architecture structurally requires agent-rooted delegation for multi-agent workflows, and analogous systems (Autonolas, Fetch.ai) demonstrate this need emerged organically in agent-native contexts with only coarse-grained alternatives.
rationale.zkp_edge: Named competitors (Lit Protocol off-chain ACLs, Turnkey HSM policy, Autonolas multisig) all lack cryptographic on-circuit scope enforcement, meaning scope expansion attacks are application-layer responsibilities — a structural failure ZKP delegation eliminates.
rationale.primitive_readiness: The Delegation circuit provides ~80% reuse with only the root anchor needing structural change, but a new Circom circuit still requires 3+ days of development plus PLONK trusted setup, new verifier deployment, and SDK surface updates — non-trivial scope.
rationale.partnership_leverage: Theseus could adopt Lit Protocol or build raw EdDSA delegation themselves in under 3 months, and there's no evidence of a confirmed expertise gap in their team's ZKP proficiency — the build-vs-partner calculus is uncertain without more signal.

evidence.summary: Theseus is an L1 purpose-built for autonomous agents with no human-controlled key path — agents hold their own keys, persist on-chain, and have verifiable AI execution; current identity approach is almost certainly raw EdDSA keypairs with no delegation circuit.

Lit Protocol provides decentralized threshold-key management and programmable signing conditions, but delegation is enforced via off-chain JSON access-control conditions (ACLs), not on-circuit; a malicious node operator or condition mismatch bypasses delegation without cryptographic proof.

Turnkey is a cloud-hosted key management API (HSM-backed) used to give agents wallets, but agent-to-agent delegation is entirely off-chain policy with no ZKP enforcement — scope expansion attacks are an application-layer responsibility.
Privy targets embedded wallets for end-users and recently added server-side agent wallets, but is human-centric by design; no delegation circuit or cumulative-bit permission model exists.

Autonolas builds autonomous agent services using Gnosis Safe multisigs as on-chain agent collectives; delegation is coarse-grained (threshold signature), not ZKP-enforced bit-level scope narrowing, creating a direct gap Bolyra fills.

Fetch.ai's Almanac on-chain registry gives agents a DID-like identity and lets them register services, but delegation between agents is handled off-chain by the uAgents framework with no cryptographic scope enforcement.
SingularityNET focuses on AI service marketplace (AGIX), not agent-to-agent identity delegation; identity primitives are minimal.

UCAN (User Controlled Authorization Networks) defines capability-based delegation chains that can narrow scope, but proofs are JWT-encoded off-chain blobs — no on-circuit enforcement means a compromised relay can forge or expand delegations.
Semaphore provides anonymous ZKP group membership proofs used by Bolyra's HumanUniqueness circuit, but is not designed for agent-rooted trust chains or permission-bit delegation.
Polygon ID provides ZKP-based verifiable credentials (Groth16 via iden3), but is human-credential-centric and has no agent-to-agent delegation circuit or cumulative-bit permission model.

Account abstraction enables agents to have smart-contract wallets with modular permissions, but delegation enforcement is Solidity logic, not a ZK circuit — invalid permission expansions are caught only at transaction time on the target chain, not at proof generation.

evidence.source: Orange DAO portfolio / YC agent infrastructure companies (2025-2026)
evidence.summary: Orange DAO (Theseus's backer) has funded multiple agent-infra plays; the cohort trend shows demand for on-chain agent identity that goes beyond wallet ownership — verifiable execution and scoped delegation are the unsolved layer.

mapped_primitives.usage: Serves as the root credential for the delegation chain origin. The agent's EdDSA credential commitment (Poseidon3(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment, expiryTimestamp)) becomes the trust anchor instead of a human Merkle leaf. The operator-signed credential commitment already encodes the genesis authority binding. The agentTree Merkle membership proof replaces the humanTree proof at the chain root.

Core scope-narrowing logic is directly reusable: bitwise subset enforcement, expiry narrowing, identity-bound scope commitment chain linking, hop-count (max 3), and delegation nullifier replay protection all apply unchanged to agent-to-agent chains. The structural change is the root anchor: replace the implicit human Merkle membership assumption at hop 0 with an agentTree membership proof. Delegator key binding (UC3.1) and expiry-in-scope-commitment (UC3.2) fixes carry over without modification.

agentTree (LeanIMT + 30-entry root history buffer) is already the correct on-chain registry for agent leaves — no new tree needed. The existing PlonkVerifier and DelegationPlonkVerifier addresses are reused. A new entry point verifyAgentDelegation() is needed to route verification to an agent-rooted path rather than the atomic human+agent handshake path. Chain-state tracking and delegation nullifier storage are inherited as-is.

AgentRootedDelegation circuit will use PLONK (same as Delegation) — the deployed verifier at 0x28b99c48f19F1680a7D88733ffeb9AFbcB3B95Db is reusable only if the constraint count and verification key remain compatible. A redeployed PlonkVerifier will be needed if the new circuit diverges from the existing vkey.

Not used in the agent-rooted path. The scope-scoped nullifier pattern (Poseidon2(scope, secret)) is a conceptual reference for designing per-agent delegation nullifiers, but the circuit itself is not invoked.

new_components_needed.description: AgentRootedDelegation.circom — variant of Delegation.circom where hop 0 anchors to an agentTree Merkle membership proof (LeanIMT depth 20) rather than a human Merkle leaf. Inputs: rootAgentCredentialCommitment, agentTreeRoot, agentMerklePath[20], agentMerkleIndex, delegatorPermissionBitmask, delegateePermissionBitmask, delegateeExpiry, delegatorExpiry, delegationTokenSig, currentTimestamp, optionalGenesisBlockCommitment (optional: Theseus birth-tx hash bound via Poseidon into the root commitment for L1 genesis anchoring). All existing scope-narrowing, expiry-narrowing, liveness, and hop-count constraints are inherited. Proving system: PLONK (no per-circuit ceremony). Expected constraint count: ~23k–25k (within pot16.ptau headroom).

IdentityRegistry.verifyAgentDelegation() — new entry point that accepts (agentRootedDelegationProof, delegatorLeafIndex, delegateeLeafIndex, scopeMask, nonce) and routes through DelegationPlonkVerifier without requiring a paired HumanUniqueness proof. Reuses existing agentTree root history buffer and delegation nullifier mapping. Emits AgentDelegationVerified event for Theseus to index. If the AgentRootedDelegation vkey diverges, deploy a new AgentRootedDelegationPlonkVerifier alongside the existing one.

Complete delegate() stub → delegateAgentToAgent(parentCredential, childCredential, scopeMask, expiry) in @bolyra/sdk v0.3. Internally: validates scopeMask is bitwise subset of parentCredential.permissionBitmask, constructs delegation token with EdDSA signature over (parentCommitment, childCommitment, scopeMask, expiry), calls AgentRootedDelegation prover, returns DelegationResult with proof + chain metadata. Add ScopeEscalationError guard (already defined in error_classes).

Theseus agent registration bridge — maps Theseus L1 agent birth transaction to a Bolyra agentTree leaf. Two paths: (a) lightweight: Theseus orchestrator calls createAgentCredential(theseusModelHash, operatorKey, permissions, expiry) and registers the resulting commitment in IdentityRegistry.agentTree — no cross-chain proof needed, operator signs off-chain; (b) trustless: embed Theseus L1 block header commitment as optionalGenesisBlockCommitment in AgentRootedDelegation inputs, requiring a storage proof relay. Path (a) is the 2-week sprint target. Path (b) is follow-on.

Extend draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 with Section 7: Agent-Rooted Delegation Chains. Specifies: genesis commitment format, agent-to-agent delegation token schema, chain traversal algorithm, nullifier domain separation from human-rooted chains (prefix byte 0x01 vs 0x00), and conformance test vectors for agent-only chains. Add 8–10 new entries to test-vectors.json.

theseus_integration_surface: Primary integration surface is the IdentityRegistry.verifyAgentDelegation() contract call on Base Sepolia — Theseus's on-chain authorization checks cross-call this function with a PLONK proof, delegator/delegatee leaf indices, required scopeMask, and a per-session nonce. The contract returns a boolean and emits an event; Theseus indexes the event to track authorized sub-agent spawns. Secondary surface is the @bolyra/sdk Node package: Theseus's orchestrator runtime calls delegateAgentToAgent() to generate the PLONK proof client-side before submitting the transaction, avoiding any Bolyra-hosted prover service. For the genesis binding, Theseus passes its agent birth transaction hash to the SDK as optionalGenesisBlockCommitment; in sprint 1 this is operator-attested (off-chain), with a trustless storage-proof path deferred to a follow-on sprint. The did:bolyra DID method and IETF draft provide the identity layer that Theseus can reference in its agent manifest format for interoperability with other orchestrators.

deliverables.name: AgentRootedDelegation design spec
deliverables.description: Circuit I/O table, public signals, trust anchor binding (agentTree Merkle membership at hop 0 via LeanIMT depth 20), genesis-block-commitment optional binding, nullifier design (Poseidon2(agentCredentialCommitment, scope)), hop-count cap 3, expiry-narrowing invariant. One markdown doc Theseus can review before the call.
deliverables.reuses: Delegation.circom design doc + AgentPolicy I/O schema

deliverables.name: AgentRootedDelegation.circom
deliverables.description: Delegation.circom fork: replace humanMerkleRoot anchor at hop 0 with agentTree Merkle membership proof (rootAgentCredentialCommitment, agentMerklePath[20], agentMerkleIndex). Scope-narrowing bitwise subset enforcement, expiry narrowing, identity-bound scope-commitment chain, delegation nullifier replay protection all carried over unchanged. Optional input optionalGenesisBlockCommitment added for Theseus birth-tx binding (Poseidon2 into nullifier domain if nonzero). PLONK only (no per-circuit Groth16 ceremony needed).
deliverables.reuses: Delegation.circom (80% reuse — root anchor is the only structural change)

deliverables.name: Witness generation + fast-mode tests
deliverables.description: Mocha witness-only tests (no full proof) covering: hop-0 agent tree membership, scope cannot expand across hops, expiry must narrow, nullifier uniqueness across chains, genesis commitment binding toggle. Follows existing test:circuits:fast pattern.
deliverables.reuses: circuits/test/ harness + mock agentTree fixtures from AgentPolicy tests

deliverables.name: PLONK trusted setup + vkey generation
deliverables.description: Run snarkjs plonk setup over AgentRootedDelegation.r1cs using pot16.ptau. Generate vkey.json. If constraint count exceeds 2^16, escalate to pot18.ptau — this is the highest-risk step. Export Solidity verifier via snarkjs exportSolidityVerifier.

deliverables.name: verifyAgentDelegation() on IdentityRegistry
deliverables.description: Add a new entry point to IdentityRegistry.sol that routes agent-rooted delegation proofs to the new PlonkVerifier (or reuses existing if vkey-compatible). Accepts: agentTreeRoot (from agentTree root history buffer, 30-entry window), delegationNullifier, scopeCommitment, expiryTimestamp, proof bytes. Stores nullifier on-chain to prevent replay. Does NOT touch the existing verifyHandshake() path.
deliverables.reuses: IdentityRegistry.sol agentTree + nullifier storage + root history buffer

deliverables.name: AgentRootedDelegationPlonkVerifier.sol
deliverables.description: Deploy new verifier contract if vkey diverges from existing DelegationPlonkVerifier. If constraint count and vkey are compatible with existing deployment at 0x28b99c48f19F1680a7D88733ffeb9AFbcB3B95Db, skip this deliverable and wire directly.
deliverables.reuses: snarkjs exportSolidityVerifier output — new artifact but no hand-coding

deliverables.name: SDK: proveAgentDelegation() + verifyAgentDelegation()
deliverables.description: Two new public methods on @bolyra/sdk. proveAgentDelegation(rootAgentCredential, delegateePermissions, delegateeExpiry, agentMerklePath, opts?) generates a PLONK proof via snarkjs/rapidsnark bridge. verifyAgentDelegation(proof, publicSignals, contractAddr) calls the on-chain entry point. Follows existing proveHandshake/verifyHandshake pattern exactly.

deliverables.name: Orchestrator→SubAgent demo script
deliverables.description: Node script: (1) register orchestrator agent in agentTree, (2) orchestrator proves AgentRootedDelegation narrowing FINANCIAL_MEDIUM (bit 3) to FINANCIAL_SMALL (bit 2) for a sub-agent, (3) sub-agent uses delegated credential to call a mock payment endpoint, (4) IdentityRegistry.verifyAgentDelegation() rejects a replay of the same nullifier. Runs against Base Sepolia or a local Hardhat fork. Terminal output only — no UI needed for Sunday.

spec.demo_description: For the Sunday call (2026-06-29): show the live AgentPolicy + existing Delegation circuits running on Base Sepolia as proof of ZKP stack maturity, walk through the AgentRootedDelegation design spec (deliverable 1, ready in 0.5 days), and demonstrate that competitors (Lit/Turnkey/Privy) enforce delegation off-chain while Bolyra enforces it inside the circuit. No working AgentRootedDelegation proof yet — that requires the 10-day sprint. The spec alone is the right Sunday artifact: it shows design credibility without overpromising.
risks: Constraint count exceeds 2^16 (pot16.ptau limit): LeanIMT depth-20 path in AgentRootedDelegation adds ~2K constraints on top of Delegation baseline; if total exceeds 65536, need pot18.ptau (~30min ceremony) and a day of setup re-run.

Theseus EVM compatibility unknown: if Theseus runs a custom VM rather than EVM-compatible execution, Solidity verifier contracts cannot deploy; mitigation is to expose a proof-export API and let Theseus implement their own verifier against the published vkey.json.

Genesis block commitment format unknown: optionalGenesisBlockCommitment binding requires Theseus to expose their birth-tx hash in a Poseidon-hashable format; if they use Keccak256 roots only, the optional input becomes a stub until Theseus ships a Poseidon bridge.
No Theseus testnet access before Sunday: demo on Base Sepolia is the fallback; Theseus integration is spec-level until they provide an RPC endpoint and deployed AgentRegistry contract address.
Solo capacity: circuit + setup + contract + SDK in 10 days is tight with no slack for trusted setup failures; if pot16 ceremony is insufficient, total slips to 12 days.
spec.success_criteria: A terminal run of the orchestrator→subagent demo script completes with: (1) PLONK proof generated in <10s via rapidsnark, (2) verifyAgentDelegation() returns true on-chain for a valid narrowed-scope proof, (3) replay of the same nullifier reverts with 'nullifier already used', (4) an attempted scope-expansion proof (delegateePermissions superset of delegatorPermissions) fails witness generation with a circom constraint error — no on-chain call needed.
spec.theseus_dependencies: RPC endpoint + chain ID for Theseus testnet or devnet; AgentRegistry contract address (or ABI) so Bolyra can read agentTree root for Merkle membership proofs; confirmation that execution environment is EVM-compatible (Solidity verifier deployability); format of agent birth-tx hash if genesis-block-commitment binding is desired at launch; signal on whether they want PLONK (no per-circuit ceremony) or are willing to run Groth16 with a one-time setup.

tier3_findings: AXIS 1 (Theseus Builds It Themselves) — CONCERN: UCAN + EdDSA gives 80% of the delegation value without ZKP expertise. Theseus could adopt UCAN (JWT-based, well-documented, no Circom required) in 2-4 weeks for a working agent-to-agent delegation chain. The on-circuit scope enforcement advantage is real but narrow — Theseus would only need to care about it after a scope expansion attack actually occurs, which at their funding stage ($100K, pre-mainnet) is a theoretical concern not an operational one. No kill signal, but the build-vs-partner calculus leans build unless Theseus explicitly lacks the stamina for ZKP.

AXIS 2 (Standard Crypto Suffices) — CONCERN: The ZKP advantage here is not privacy — agent delegation data has no human PII — it is cryptographic unforgability of scope constraints. That is a real property: a compromised relay or buggy orchestrator cannot expand permissions post-issuance without invalidating the proof. However, at Theseus's current scale (pre-mainnet, unknown agent count), this is a security property they will not stress-test for at least 12 months. The gas/compute overhead of on-chain Groth16 verification is not justified against a threat that has not materialized. Standard EdDSA + on-chain ACLs is the correct engineering choice for their current phase. The ZKP argument becomes compelling at scale, not at genesis.

AXIS 3 (No Agent Actually Needs This) — PASS: The proposal correctly identifies the structural requirement. Theseus's 'no human key path' constraint means every delegation is agent-to-agent by definition — there is no fallback to human-signed approvals. Any orchestrator-subagent pattern (even a 2-agent workflow) immediately requires a delegation primitive. This is not a feature that emerges at 10,000 agents; it is a prerequisite for the first multi-agent deployment. The use case is named, concrete, and unavoidable given Theseus's architecture.

AXIS 4 (Integration Complexity vs Value) — CONCERN: The integration complexity is materially underestimated. The card marks the Delegation circuit modification as 'major' but targets a 2-week sprint — these are in tension. A major structural change to the root anchor requires: (1) Circom circuit rewrite and audit, (2) new PLONK trusted setup or Groth16 ceremony, (3) Solidity verifier regeneration, (4) verifier deployment to whatever chain Theseus runs. If Theseus's L1 is not EVM-compatible, step 4 becomes months of custom verifier implementation, not days. The call on Sunday should extract Theseus's VM architecture before any scope estimate is made — without that answer, the 2-week estimate has a ±400% error bar.

AXIS 5 (Single-Partner Dependency) — PASS: An agent-rooted variant of the Delegation circuit is a core Bolyra protocol primitive that any agent-native deployment needs, not a Theseus-specific artifact. Autonolas, Fetch.ai, any future agent L1 with multi-agent workflows has the same structural gap. Building it for Theseus produces a reusable circuit variant, a new verifier contract, and SDK surface that advances Bolyra's roadmap independent of Theseus's survival. The work is justified on roadmap grounds alone; Theseus is an accelerant, not the only reason to build it.
tier3_summary: Agent-rooted delegation survives adversarial review as CONDITIONAL — the structural need is real and the primitive is reusable, but the Sunday call must confirm EVM compatibility and Theseus's ZKP proficiency before any sprint commitment, because three independent axes all hinge on unknowns that could flip this from a 2-week win to a 3-month sinkhole.
