title: ZKP-enforced x402 spending limits for autonomous Theseus agents
description: x402 is now an industry standard (Linux Foundation, Coinbase, AWS Bedrock, Stripe) and Bolyra already ships @bolyra/payment-protocols implementing x402 flows. The AgentPolicy circuit's permission bits 2 (FINANCIAL_SMALL <$100), 3 (FINANCIAL_MEDIUM <$10K), and 4 (FINANCIAL_UNLIMITED) provide ZKP-enforced spending authorization: an agent proves it holds a credential with the required financial permission bit set, without revealing its full credential or who granted it. For Theseus, this means autonomous agents can make x402 payments with cryptographically enforced spending caps — no human approval needed, no trusted intermediary. The FINANCIAL_MEDIUM implication of FINANCIAL_SMALL (cumulative bit encoding, enforced in AgentPolicy circuit) prevents privilege escalation. Integration path: @bolyra/payment-protocols + existing AgentPolicy circuit + a thin Theseus-native SDK wrapper calling proveHandshake() before each x402 payment request.

rationale.agent_need: x402 multi-vendor adoption (Linux Foundation, AWS Bedrock, Coinbase) is well-evidenced, but the Theseus-specific 'no human key path' constraint comes from an unconfirmed source (theseuschain.com blocked), leaving the core premise — that Theseus agents specifically need ZKP spending caps — as a structural inference rather than confirmed evidence.
rationale.zkp_edge: Non-ZKP alternatives are named and their failure modes are structurally argued (Coinbase custodial, Lit MPC, Turnkey server-side), and the in-circuit cumulative-bit enforcement preventing privilege escalation is a genuine structural advantage, but the proposal underweights that custodial spending limits (Coinbase Agentic Wallets) already solve the practical problem for most enterprise use cases without ZKP complexity.
rationale.primitive_readiness: Specific circuits (AgentPolicy PLONK, Delegation), SDK methods (createAgentCredential, proveHandshake), and contracts (IdentityRegistry, PlonkVerifier) are named; new work is scoped to proveAgentPayment() (2d), x402PaymentMiddleware (2d), and PaymentAuthorizer.sol (3d), with @bolyra/payment-protocols already shipping x402 support — realistic and well-decomposed.
rationale.partnership_leverage: The case for why Theseus cannot build this themselves is entirely missing — no assessment of Theseus's team ZKP proficiency, no evidence they lack an identity solution in progress, and credible drop-in competitors (Lit Protocol, Turnkey, Coinbase Agentic Wallets) are available; the partnership value proposition for Theseus is asserted but not demonstrated.

evidence.source: theseuschain.com (not confirmed live — web search blocked)
evidence.summary: Theseus describes 'Agency for Agents' with agent-held keys and no human key path, implying raw EdDSA keypairs with no ZKP authorization layer — the exact gap Bolyra fills.

evidence.source: x402.org — Linux Foundation / Coinbase / AWS Bedrock / Stripe adoption
evidence.summary: x402 has achieved multi-vendor endorsement as the HTTP-native payment standard for autonomous agents, making ZKP-enforced spending caps on x402 flows an immediately deployable primitive.
AWS Bedrock explicitly frames x402 as the payment layer for agentic commerce, validating that enterprise agent stacks will consume x402 payment flows at scale.
Coinbase Agentic Wallets launch signals that custodial spending-limit enforcement is the incumbent solution — ZKP-native enforcement via Bolyra AgentPolicy circuit is the trustless alternative.

evidence.source: Lit Protocol — PKP (Programmable Key Pairs) for agents
evidence.summary: Lit offers distributed key management for agents but relies on MPC threshold signing, not ZKP authorization proofs — no cryptographically enforced permission-bit spending caps.
Turnkey provides policy-based signing with server-side enforcement; no on-chain ZKP proof of authorization — spending limits are custodial, not cryptographic.
Privy targets consumer-facing embedded wallets; agent use cases are secondary and lack any delegation or spending-cap primitives.
Both Fetch.ai and SingularityNET have agent-to-agent payment primitives but no ZKP-enforced permission scoping; authorization is contract-level ACL, not cryptographic proof.

evidence.source: W3C Verifiable Credentials + DID spec (did:bolyra method in spec/)
evidence.summary: VC/DID standards provide the presentation layer but have no native spending-cap enforcement; Bolyra's AgentPolicy circuit adds the missing cryptographic enforcement layer.
Bolyra already ships x402 flow integration with AgentPolicy circuit wiring; the Theseus integration is a thin SDK wrapper on existing primitives, not greenfield.

evidence.source: Orange DAO / YC alumni DAO — $100K seed for Theseus (2025)
evidence.summary: Early-stage funding means Theseus is pre-mainnet, likely still architecting identity — optimal timing to influence their auth stack before it ossifies.

mapped_primitives.usage: Core authorization primitive. Permission bits 2/3/4 (FINANCIAL_SMALL/MEDIUM/UNLIMITED) map directly to x402 payment tiers. Selective disclosure proves requiredScopeMask bits are set without leaking full credential or operator identity — exactly what x402 payment endpoints need to gate access. Cumulative-bit enforcement in-circuit prevents a FINANCIAL_SMALL credential from claiming FINANCIAL_MEDIUM rights.

Enables the autonomous-agent pattern without keeping the human online per payment. Human enrolls once, delegates FINANCIAL_SMALL or FINANCIAL_MEDIUM scope to a Theseus agent key. Scope narrowing (delegateeScope ⊆ delegatorScope) enforces the spending cap in-circuit. Expiry narrowing prevents delegated credentials from outliving the parent. Delegation nullifier on IdentityRegistry prevents replay of the same delegation token.

On-chain proof verification anchor. Root history buffers (30 entries, humanTree + agentTree) allow stale-root tolerance across the 12-second Base block time without requiring synchronous Merkle root updates per payment. Delegation chain-state tracking enforces the 3-hop maximum for nested Theseus sub-agents.

@bolyra/payment-protocols already implements x402 request/response flow. It becomes the transport layer: intercepts the 402 challenge, triggers proveHandshake() or a standalone AgentPolicy proof, attaches the proof as a Bearer token or custom x402 header field before retrying the request.

SDK entry points for credential issuance (operator keys in Theseus operator infra) and proof generation at payment time. proveHandshake() currently requires both humanProof and agentProof — for fully autonomous payments where no human is online, a standalone AgentPolicy prove path is needed (see new_components_needed).

Indirect: used once at enrollment to establish the human Merkle leaf that anchors subsequent Delegation grants. Not invoked per-payment in the autonomous flow — the delegation chain carries forward the human binding without requiring the human to be online.

new_components_needed.description: PaymentAuthorizer: a lightweight IdentityRegistry extension (or standalone verifier) that accepts a single PlonkVerifier call against an AgentPolicy proof (or DelegationPlonkVerifier for delegated agents) without requiring a simultaneous HumanUniqueness Groth16 proof. Current verifyHandshake() is atomic human+agent; x402 payment flows are agent-only at call time. Emits a PaymentAuthorized(nullifier, scopeMask, expiryTimestamp) event for off-chain audit.

provePayment(agentCredential, requiredPermission, paymentNonce) → PaymentProof: thin SDK function wrapping AgentPolicy witness generation + snarkjs prove for the payment-only path. Distinct from proveHandshake() — no HumanUniqueness witness required. Attaches proof to x402 402-Payment header as a base64-encoded protobuf or JSON envelope per @bolyra/payment-protocols wire format.

theseus-bolyra adapter: a Theseus-native SDK wrapper (~200 LOC) that hooks into Theseus's HTTP client layer. Intercepts 402 responses, calls provePayment(), retries with proof attached. Reads agent credentials from Theseus's existing secret store. Exposes a SpendingPolicy config (maxTierPerDomain, requireDelegation: bool) so operators can pin individual agents to FINANCIAL_SMALL even if the credential allows FINANCIAL_MEDIUM.

x402-bolyra-proof-envelope spec: a one-page extension to draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 defining how the AgentPolicy/Delegation proof is encoded in the x402 Payment header field — field names, proof format version, scope mask encoding, nullifier for idempotency. Needed so x402 endpoints can verify without knowing Bolyra internals.

theseus_integration_surface: Three-layer surface: (1) Contract layer — Theseus's payment infrastructure calls PaymentAuthorizer.verifyPaymentProof(proof, requiredScopeMask, nonce) on Base Sepolia (or mainnet fork) to gate each x402 payment on-chain; the contract emits PaymentAuthorized events Theseus can index. (2) SDK bridge — theseus-bolyra adapter wraps @bolyra/sdk and @bolyra/payment-protocols; Theseus operators call initBolyraPayments(agentCredential, policy) once at agent startup, after which the adapter is transparent. (3) Operator key management — Theseus operators issue AgentCredentials via createAgentCredential(modelHash, operatorKey, [FINANCIAL_MEDIUM], expiry), stored in their existing secret management; the SDK bridge reads them at prove time without storing proof artifacts.

deliverables.name: proveAgentPayment() — standalone agent proof path
deliverables.description: New SDK export that generates an AgentPolicy PLONK proof for a single payment authorization without requiring the human to be online. Accepts (delegatedCredential, requiredPermissionBit, paymentAmountCents, sessionNonce) and returns a proof + public signals. proveHandshake() currently requires both human+agent — this headless variant is the critical missing piece for fully autonomous Theseus agents.
deliverables.reuses: AgentPolicy circuit (unchanged), createAgentCredential, existing snarkjs PLONK prover

deliverables.name: x402PaymentMiddleware — proof-gated x402 transport
deliverables.description: Thin middleware wrapping @bolyra/payment-protocols. Intercepts the 402 challenge, inspects the required payment tier, calls proveAgentPayment() with the matching FINANCIAL_* bit, attaches the base64-encoded proof as x-bolyra-proof header (or Bearer token per x402 spec), then retries the request. Handles the FINANCIAL_SMALL / MEDIUM / UNLIMITED tier routing from the 402 WWW-Authenticate header.

deliverables.name: PaymentAuthorizer.sol — on-chain spending-tier verifier
deliverables.description: Lightweight Solidity contract (150-200 lines). Accepts an AgentPolicy PLONK proof + public signals, calls the existing PlonkVerifier, checks that requiredScopeMask matches the requested payment tier (FINANCIAL_SMALL=0x04, MEDIUM=0x08, UNLIMITED=0x10), enforces cumulative-bit implication (MEDIUM implies SMALL), checks nullifier against IdentityRegistry to prevent replay, emits PaymentAuthorized(agentNullifier, tier, nonce). x402 payment endpoint calls this before releasing the resource. Deployed to Base Sepolia.
deliverables.reuses: Existing PlonkVerifier (AgentPolicy), IdentityRegistry nullifier store, Hardhat test suite

deliverables.name: @bolyra/theseus — Theseus agent runtime wrapper
deliverables.description: Tiny npm package (~200 lines) wrapping @bolyra/sdk with Theseus-idiomatic ergonomics: TheseusAgent class with issueCredential(eddsaPrivKey, permissions, expiry), payWithProof(url, amount), and delegateTo(subAgentPubKey, narrowedPermissions). Designed for Theseus agent runtime environment — assumes EdDSA keys are already held by the agent, no browser/wallet dependency.

deliverables.name: End-to-end demo script
deliverables.description: Node.js script that runs the full flow headlessly: (1) operator issues AgentPolicy credential with FINANCIAL_SMALL (bit 2) to a Theseus agent EdDSA key, (2) agent encounters a mock x402 endpoint requiring $50 payment, (3) middleware generates PLONK proof in <8s via snarkjs, (4) PaymentAuthorizer.sol verifies on Base Sepolia, (5) payment succeeds, (6) replay of same nonce fails. Script outputs proof size, proving time, and gas cost. Runnable in <2 minutes from npm install.
deliverables.reuses: All above components, existing Base Sepolia deploy infrastructure

deliverables.name: Theseus integration spec (one-pager)
deliverables.description: Markdown doc covering: agent key format assumptions (EdDSA on Baby Jubjub), PaymentAuthorizer ABI, x-bolyra-proof header format, proof verification gas benchmarks, delegation chain setup for sub-agents, and what Theseus must expose (operator registration endpoint or on-chain). Written for Theseus eng team, not general audience.

spec.demo_description: For the Sunday June 29 call: show the live AgentPolicy circuit financial permission bits in circuits/src/AgentPolicy.circom, walk through @bolyra/payment-protocols x402 flow with a mock credential, and share a 90-second architecture diagram showing how proveAgentPayment() slots into Theseus agent runtime. No live proof on Sunday — snarkjs proving takes ~6s and Base Sepolia tx needs setup time. The narrative is: 'here is every primitive already built, here is the 2-week plan to wire them.' The demo script will be the artifact for the follow-up call.
risks: Theseus VM may not be EVM-compatible — PaymentAuthorizer.sol assumes EVM; if Theseus runs a custom VM, the on-chain verifier needs a rewrite that could add 1-2 weeks
No Theseus testnet access before Sunday call — integration is conceptual until Eric Wang provides endpoint or chain docs; all solo work must be on Base Sepolia until then

proveHandshake() assumes Baby Jubjub EdDSA (same curve as Circom/snarkjs) — Theseus agent keys may use a different curve (secp256k1 is common); curve mismatch would require AgentPolicy circuit modification (1-2 day fix but a real risk)
Solo founder bandwidth: 10 dev-days assumes focused blocks; any Theseus back-and-forth async latency or other product fires will push the timeline
x402 header spec is still evolving — the proof attachment mechanism (Bearer vs custom header) may need adjustment if Theseus's x402 implementation diverges from x402.org reference
spec.success_criteria: End-to-end demo script runs clean: agent credential issued, x402 payment authorized via on-chain PLONK proof verification, replay rejected, total e2e latency under 15s, gas under 300k on Base Sepolia. Theseus eng team can npm install @bolyra/theseus and run the demo with their own EdDSA keys.
spec.theseus_dependencies: Need from Theseus before integration can go beyond Base Sepolia mock: (1) EVM compatibility confirmation or custom VM bytecode format, (2) agent EdDSA key curve (Baby Jubjub assumed — confirm), (3) testnet RPC endpoint + faucet access, (4) how agents currently identify themselves on-chain (raw pubkey? DID? address?), (5) whether Theseus has an operator registry concept or if Bolyra must deploy IdentityRegistry on Theseus chain. Without (1) and (2), the Base Sepolia demo is a full proof of concept.

tier3_findings: AXIS 1 — CONCERN: Spending limit enforcement is achievable with standard Solidity in under 2 weeks. A per-agent mapping (agentAddress => tierEnum) plus an on-chain spending counter requires zero ZKP expertise. Theseus engineers don't need Circom, snarkjs, or trusted setup ceremonies to cap payments at $100 or $10K — they need one mapping and one require() check. The ZKP adds credential privacy (hiding who granted the permission), but that property is not the spending-cap use case; it's a separate privacy use case that isn't well-motivated here.

AXIS 2 — CONCERN: The payment amount itself is visible on-chain regardless of ZKP. If Theseus's L1 records each x402 payment transaction (which any L1 must to prevent double-spends), then what exactly is the ZKP hiding? The credential issuer identity? The operator who granted FINANCIAL_MEDIUM rights? These have marginal value in most enterprise agent scenarios where the operator-agent relationship is known to both parties. Coinbase Agentic Wallets already provide custodial spending caps that are operationally simpler and battle-tested. The proposal acknowledges this competitor but dismisses it without quantifying the trust assumption cost — for Theseus pre-mainnet with zero enterprise customers, 'trustless' is a feature with no current buyer.

AXIS 3 — PASS: x402 multi-vendor adoption is credibly documented (Linux Foundation, AWS Bedrock, Stripe, Coinbase). Autonomous agents that cannot interrupt for human approval on each payment are a genuine category on Theseus's 'no human key path' architecture. This need exists at agent count 1, not just at 10,000. This axis is the strongest leg of the proposal.

AXIS 4 — CONCERN: Theseus's VM environment is confirmed unknown — theseuschain.com was inaccessible. If Theseus runs a non-EVM custom VM, deploying PlonkVerifier.sol and IdentityRegistry.sol requires either (a) Theseus adopts EVM compatibility or (b) Bolyra implements a custom Groth16/PLONK verifier in Theseus's native execution environment. Neither is a 'thin SDK wrapper.' Furthermore, Groth16 proof generation latency (typically 2-10 seconds on mobile/edge hardware) is unacceptable in synchronous x402 payment flows where sub-second authorization is expected. The proposal does not address proof generation latency at all, which is a critical gap for a payment use case.

AXIS 5 — PASS: @bolyra/payment-protocols and the AgentPolicy circuit are not Theseus-specific. x402 is an industry standard; any agent platform integrating x402 is a potential consumer. The Theseus-specific work is limited to the SDK wrapper. If Theseus disappears, Bolyra retains fully general payment-authorization primitives applicable to Fetch.ai, SingularityNET, or any future agent-native chain. This is the proposal's strongest structural property.

CRITICAL MISSING EVIDENCE: The proposal has not confirmed Theseus's VM type, current identity approach, or launch timeline. Without knowing whether Theseus is EVM-compatible, the integration complexity estimate ('thin SDK wrapper') is unverifiable and likely optimistic. This must be resolved in the Sunday call before any sprint commitment.

PROOF LATENCY GAP: No benchmarks cited for AgentPolicy PLONK proof generation time in a payment flow context. If proof generation takes >1 second, x402 endpoints will timeout or require async proof submission, fundamentally changing the UX model. This is a disqualifying concern for a 'sunday_demo' timing assessment.
tier3_summary: x402 adoption is real and autonomous agent payments are a genuine need, but three concerns block promotion: spending limit enforcement doesn't require ZKP (standard Solidity suffices), Theseus's VM compatibility is unconfirmed making integration scope unknown, and proof generation latency in synchronous payment flows is unaddressed — confirm VM type and PLONK latency benchmarks before committing sprint resources.

title: ZKP-Enforced Delegation Chain with Scope Monotonicity on Theseus L1
description: On Theseus, Agent A delegates to Agent B which delegates to Agent C — without circuit-level enforcement, Agent C can claim permissions Agent A never granted (privilege escalation). Bolyra's Delegation circuit enforces one-way scope narrowing at the proof level: a delegatee cannot construct a valid proof claiming bits the delegator lacked. This is structurally unsolvable with ACLs alone on an agent-native L1 because there is no human to audit the chain in real time. The 8-bit cumulative encoding (bits 2→3→4 implication) is already implemented and the circuit is live on Base Sepolia — adapting it for agent-rooted trust (no human at root) is the key extension.

rationale.agent_need: Strong structural argument from 'no human auditor' constraint plus Fetch.ai analog showing multi-hop privilege escalation emerged in the only comparable deployed agent L1, but zero direct citation from Theseus docs or codebase (web search unavailable) leaves a required evidence gap.
rationale.zkp_edge: Concretely names Lit Protocol (ACL/MPC), Turnkey (off-chain policy), and ACL smart contracts as alternatives, then identifies a structural failure for each — none can cryptographically bind scope narrowing across delegation hops without a trusted third-party auditor, which is a real structural gap not a 'ZKP is cooler' argument.
rationale.primitive_readiness: Correctly maps AgentPolicy (no change), DelegationPlonkVerifier (minor redeployment), and IdentityRegistry (major), but the critical AgentRootedDelegation.circom requires rewriting the trust-root input wiring — a new circuit that the spec itself estimates at 5 days, underweighting that any circuit change triggers new trusted setup, integration testing, and verifier contract regeneration adding 3–5 more days.
rationale.partnership_leverage: Correctly identifies the ZKP circuit design and Circom proficiency gap that Fetch.ai and SingularityNET never closed, but provides no direct evidence on Theseus's team crypto background, no confirmation they lack an identity solution in progress, and does not address the risk that they adopt Lit Protocol (which is production-ready today) as the path of least resistance.

evidence.summary: Theseus is an agent-native L1 with no human key path, $100K Orange DAO seed, call booked 2026-06-29 — identity/auth approach not yet publicly documented.
Could not retrieve Theseus GitHub repos, docs, or community discussions; live search required to fill this gap before Sunday's call.
Lit provides programmable MPC key management and conditional signing for agents, but does not enforce delegation scope monotonicity at the circuit level — ACL-based, not ZKP-enforced.
Turnkey offers embedded wallet infrastructure with policy engine for agent signing, but policies are off-chain and not cryptographically binding across delegation hops.
Privy targets human-facing embedded wallets; lacks native agent-to-agent delegation model and has no ZKP layer.
Emerging IETF draft on AI agent identity; no finalized spec for agent-to-agent delegation chain integrity or ZKP-enforced scope monotonicity.
Fetch.ai has an agent framework (AEA) with DID-based identity but relies on traditional PKI and ACLs — no ZKP delegation enforcement, privilege escalation risk across multi-hop agent chains.
AI marketplace on blockchain; agent identity is marketplace-account-level, no structured delegation primitive or scope narrowing.

Semaphore v4 ceremony (depth 20) is already reused by Bolyra's HumanUniqueness circuit — closest deployed ZKP identity system, but human-rooted; no agent-rooted trust chain equivalent exists in production.
ZKP-native L2 with privacy-preserving account abstraction; not agent-native, no structured permission bit model — different design point from Bolyra's cumulative-bit encoding.
Bolyra's Delegation circuit with 8-bit cumulative encoding is live on Base Sepolia — only known ZKP system enforcing scope monotonicity across multi-hop agent delegation chains.

mapped_primitives.usage: Core enforcement primitive. The PLONK Delegation circuit already implements scope narrowing (delegateeScope ⊆ delegatorScope), expiry narrowing, max-3-hop enforcement, and identity-bound scope commitment chain linking. For Theseus, each hop in an A→B→C chain submits a Delegation proof; the circuit guarantee that C cannot claim bits A never granted is already cryptographically enforced — no ACL auditor needed.

Establishes the root credential at hop 0. Agent A proves its own permission bitmask via AgentPolicy (operator-signed EdDSA credential + cumulative-bit encoding). This root proof anchors the delegation chain — B and C can only narrow from A's proven bitmask. The selective-disclosure scope commitment output becomes the delegatorScopeCommitment input for the first Delegation proof.

Provides dual LeanIMT trees (agentTree) with 30-entry root history buffers and delegation nullifier replay protection. The on-chain chain-state tracking and hop-count enforcement (max 3) map directly to the Theseus multi-agent scenario. Would be redeployed or adapted on Theseus L1 as the authoritative delegation state anchor.

The deployed PLONK verifier at 0x285D2E315AdD9c82337d8a4BDF567AB3a9215B93 on Base Sepolia validates Delegation circuit proofs. A copy must be deployed on Theseus L1 for on-chain verification. No circuit changes required for the verifier itself — it is deterministic from the .zkey.
Verifies AgentPolicy proofs for the root-credential step (Agent A's initial capability claim). Must be redeployed on Theseus L1 alongside DelegationPlonkVerifier.

new_components_needed.description: AgentRootedDelegation circuit variant: current Delegation.circom assumes a human identity commitment at the trust root (humanNullifier binds chain). Theseus use case is fully agent-rooted (no human at root). Need to replace the human-leaf anchor with an operator-signed agent credential commitment as chain root, while preserving all scope-monotonicity and expiry-narrowing constraints. This is a structural change to the delegator input signals — not a rewrite, but non-trivial given the CIP-1 Merkle inclusion proof wiring.

TheseusRegistryAdapter: deploy PlonkVerifier + DelegationPlonkVerifier on Theseus L1 and a stripped-down IdentityRegistry that omits the humanTree and focuses on agentTree + delegation chain state. Must implement Theseus's native tx format if it diverges from EVM. If Theseus is EVM-compatible, this is a straightforward re-deploy of existing Hardhat artifacts with constructor param changes.

Complete the delegate() stub (currently v0.3 placeholder in @bolyra/sdk). Must support agent-as-root chains: buildDelegationProof(rootAgentCredential, delegateeAgentPubKey, narrowedPermissions, narrowedExpiry) → DelegationProof, and chainDelegation(existingChain, nextDelegatee, ...) for multi-hop. Must enforce ScopeEscalationError client-side before proof generation to fail fast.

@bolyra/theseus adapter: thin wrapper that maps Theseus agent identity format (however Theseus represents agents natively) to Bolyra AgentCredential, submits proofs to TheseusRegistryAdapter, and surfaces TheseusChainVerificationResult. Analogous to @bolyra/openclaw but targeting Theseus's agent-native RPC surface.

Theseus-specific conformance test vectors extending test-vectors.json: agent-rooted 3-hop chains, attempted privilege escalation (must fail), expiry narrowing boundary cases, and nullifier replay attempts. Needed to prove circuit correctness for the modified AgentRootedDelegation before any Theseus deployment.

theseus_integration_surface: Three-layer surface: (1) On-chain — deploy PlonkVerifier + DelegationPlonkVerifier + TheseusRegistryAdapter contracts on Theseus L1; Theseus agent contracts call verifyDelegationChain(proofs[], hopCount) which calls DelegationPlonkVerifier.verifyProof() for each hop and enforces the chain-state invariants. (2) SDK bridge — @bolyra/theseus adapter wraps the completed delegate() flow, translates Theseus agent identity format to AgentCredential, and submits proofs to the registry. Agent operators run proveHandshake-equivalent locally (Node or rapidsnark) and post proofs on-chain. (3) Off-chain verification — for Theseus dApps that want to gate API calls rather than on-chain actions, verifyHandshake() can run entirely off-chain against the agentTree root snapshot, with the Theseus registry serving as root-of-truth via a read RPC call.

deliverables.name: Theseus Integration Architecture Spec
deliverables.description: One-page technical brief mapping Bolyra primitives to Theseus's agent-rooted trust model. Covers: AgentPolicy as chain root (hop 0), Delegation circuit per hop, IdentityRegistry as delegation state anchor, 8-bit scope monotonicity guarantee. Written for Sunday call — frames the integration before a single line of code is written.
deliverables.reuses: existing Bolyra CLAUDE.md, fit analysis above

deliverables.name: Base Sepolia Live Demo Script
deliverables.description: Node script that proves a 3-hop A→B→C delegation chain using existing AgentPolicy + Delegation PLONK circuits on Base Sepolia. Agent A gets FINANCIAL_MEDIUM (bit 3), delegates to B with READ_DATA+WRITE_DATA only, B delegates to C with READ_DATA only. Script prints proof hashes + on-chain verifyProof results. This is what gets shown on Sunday — no new circuit work needed, uses deployed 0x285D2E315AdD9c82337d8a4BDF567AB3a9215B93.

deliverables.name: AgentRootedDelegation.circom
deliverables.description: Variant of Delegation.circom that replaces the humanNullifier anchor at trust root with an operator-signed EdDSA agent credential commitment. Input signals change: swap humanLeaf+merkleProof for agentCredentialHash+operatorSigR+operatorSigS. All scope-monotonicity constraints (delegateeScope ⊆ delegatorScope), expiry narrowing, max-3-hop counter, and identity-bound scope commitment chain linking are preserved verbatim. Output: agentRootedDelegationProof compatible with new TheseusRegistryAdapter.
deliverables.reuses: Delegation.circom (70% reuse — constraint logic unchanged, input wiring changes)

deliverables.name: TheseusRegistryAdapter.sol
deliverables.description: Stripped-down IdentityRegistry for Theseus L1. Stores: agentTree root (LeanIMT), 30-entry root history buffer, delegation nullifier set (replay protection), hop counter per chain ID. Exposes: registerAgent(agentCredentialHash), submitDelegation(proof, publicSignals), verifyChain(chainId, claimedScope) → bool. No human tree, no HumanUniqueness verifier. Designed for EVM-compatible Theseus — fallback path if Theseus is non-EVM is a JSON-RPC adapter stub.
deliverables.reuses: IdentityRegistry.sol (50% reuse — removes human tree, adapts delegation state logic)

deliverables.name: Theseus Verifier Deployment Bundle
deliverables.description: Hardhat deploy scripts for Theseus testnet: deploys PlonkVerifier (AgentPolicy), DelegationPlonkVerifier (existing .zkey, no recompile needed), and TheseusRegistryAdapter in one tx sequence. Includes verify step that calls verifyProof with a known-good test proof to confirm chain compatibility. Blocked on Theseus providing testnet RPC + faucet.

deliverables.name: bolyra-theseus TypeScript integration example
deliverables.description: Self-contained example under integrations/theseus/ that shows the full A→B→C chain against Theseus testnet: generateAgentCredential() → proveAgentPolicy() → proveDelegation(A→B) → proveDelegation(B→C) → TheseusRegistryAdapter.verifyChain(). Uses @bolyra/sdk subprocess bridge. Meant to be the 'copy-paste quickstart' Theseus devs hand to agent builders.

spec.demo_description: Run the Base Sepolia Live Demo Script live on the call: show Agent A (FINANCIAL_MEDIUM) delegating to B (READ_DATA+WRITE_DATA) delegating to C (READ_DATA), print on-chain verifyProof=true for each hop, then attempt to forge C claiming FINANCIAL_MEDIUM — verifyProof=false. No new circuit work needed. Pair with the one-page architecture spec showing how this maps to Theseus L1. The ask: Theseus testnet RPC access + 30-min technical session with Eric to confirm EVM compatibility.
risks: Theseus VM may not be EVM-compatible — if custom VM, TheseusRegistryAdapter needs a full rewrite; the AgentRootedDelegation circuit output is still usable but the on-chain verifier layer is unknown. Validate this on Sunday before writing circuit code.

AgentRootedDelegation circuit: replacing humanNullifier with agentCredentialHash changes the Merkle inclusion proof wiring in CIP-1 — may surface constraint-count surprises that push 5-day estimate to 8. Run constraint count check (snarkjs r1cs info) after first compile before committing.

Solo bandwidth: 12.5 days assumes no context-switching to GeniusComply or ZKProva. Realistic throughput is ~8 focused days in a 2-week sprint, so circuit work may slip to week 3 unless Theseus testnet access is confirmed fast.

Theseus testnet stability unknown — if RPC is flaky or faucet-gated, verifier deployment and integration testing block. Mitigate: run integration tests against Base Sepolia with a TheseusRegistryAdapter mock until Theseus testnet is stable.
spec.success_criteria: Agent C can submit a PLONK proof to TheseusRegistryAdapter.verifyChain() that returns true only when C's claimed scope is a strict subset of A's original credential scope, and the call reverts (or returns false) when C claims any bit A never held — confirmed by a passing Hardhat test and a live Theseus testnet transaction hash.
spec.theseus_dependencies: 1) Theseus testnet RPC endpoint + chain ID (needed before verifier deployment). 2) Confirmation that Theseus L1 is EVM-compatible and supports Solidity verifiers (pairing-library gas budget matters — BN254 precompiles at standard EIP-197 addresses). 3) Testnet faucet or funded deployer key. 4) 30-min async technical session with Eric to confirm execution environment before AgentRootedDelegation circuit work starts — prevents wasted circuit days if the VM requires a different proof system.

tier3_findings: AXIS 1 — CONCERN: A ZKP-capable engineer hired by Theseus could replicate scope-monotonicity enforcement in 2-3 months using circomlib primitives + snarkjs directly. The 8-bit cumulative-bit model is clever but not patented or trade-secret. The main moat is that Bolyra has already debugged trusted-setup, verifier regeneration, and circuit-contract integration — all of which are painful first-time costs but not insurmountable. Bolyra's lead is time-to-market, not technical impossibility.

AXIS 2 — CONCERN: On a transparent L1, scope monotonicity is achievable with on-chain ACLs without ZKP: Agent A posts permission bitmask on-chain, delegation records are smart-contract-enforced, and the chain contract validates Y ⊆ X ⊆ A's bitmask at each hop. The ZKP advantage (hiding intermediate permission sets from observers) only matters if agents have competitive reasons to conceal their authorization scope — a use-case that is speculative at seed stage. The card's structural argument ('no human auditor') is actually a win for on-chain ACLs, not against them: the L1 contract IS the auditor.

AXIS 3 — CONCERN: Theseus is $100K pre-mainnet. In the first 12 months, the realistic delegation topology is flat (one orchestrator → one executor), not 3-hop chains. The Fetch.ai privilege escalation analog cited in the rationale emerged at scale (multi-agent marketplaces with thousands of agents) — not at launch. No specific Theseus agent type or integration partner is named that requires multi-hop delegation at launch. This is a v2 problem being pitched as a launch blocker.

AXIS 4 — CONCERN (borderline FAIL): Theseus's execution environment is entirely unknown — the open questions section explicitly asks 'EVM-compatible? Custom VM?' If Theseus runs a non-EVM VM, deploying Solidity verifiers requires a full Groth16/PLONK verifier reimplementation in the Theseus native language, which could take months and may require Theseus-side engineering commitments they cannot make at seed stage. The new AgentRootedDelegation.circom circuit also triggers a new trusted setup ceremony — correctly flagged in the rationale but understated in the score. Proving-side integration (agents must run snarkjs or call a Bolyra proving endpoint) adds latency that may be unacceptable for real-time L1 transaction flows.

AXIS 5 — PASS: The generalizable value is real. Removing the human-root assumption from Bolyra's circuits is foundational to the agent identity thesis regardless of whether Theseus ships. AgentRootedDelegation.circom, if built, serves any agent-native chain (Fhenix, Ritual, AO, future agent L1s). The IdentityRegistry 'major modification' is the only Theseus-specific risk, and even that redesign (agentTree replacing humanTree at root) is protocol-level work Bolyra needs anyway.

CRITICAL GAP: The single largest kill signal is Axis 4's VM unknown combined with Axis 2's on-chain ACL alternative. Sunday's call must nail down: (a) EVM or custom VM, and (b) whether Theseus already has an identity/auth approach in flight. If they're building ACL-based delegation now, ZKP is an upgrade pitch, not a foundational requirement.
tier3_summary: Survives on generalizability alone — the core problem is real but premature for a $100K pre-mainnet chain, on-chain ACLs solve 80% of it without ZKP overhead, and unknown VM architecture makes integration complexity unknowable until Sunday's call.
concerns: AXIS 1 — CONCERN: A ZKP-capable engineer hired by Theseus could replicate scope-monotonicity enforcement in 2-3 months using circomlib primitives + snarkjs directly. If Theseus runs a non-EVM VM, deploying Solidity verifiers requires a full Groth16/PLONK verifier reimplementation in the Theseus native language, which could take months and may require Theseus-side engineering commitments they cannot make at seed stage. The new AgentRootedDelegation.circom circuit also triggers a new trusted setup ceremony — correctly flagged in the rationale but understated in the score. The IdentityRegistry 'major modification' is the only Theseus-specific risk, and even that redesign (agentTree replacing humanTree at root) is protocol-level work Bolyra needs anyway.

If they're building ACL-based delegation now, ZKP is an upgrade pitch, not a foundational requirement.

title: Authorization proof layer atop Theseus verifiable AI execution
description: Theseus advertises 'verifiable AI execution' — proving WHAT an agent computed. But verifiable execution without verifiable authorization is an incomplete trust model: you can prove an agent ran model M and produced output O, but not that it was authorized to act on behalf of entity E with permission scope S. Bolyra's AgentPolicy circuit closes this gap by binding the execution proof to a scope commitment: Poseidon3(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment, expiryTimestamp). The composition is: Theseus execution attestation anchors to a Bolyra scope commitment, so on-chain verifiers can check both claims atomically. No new circuit — the existing AgentPolicy selective disclosure output (requiredScopeMask) is the binding point. New work is a Solidity adapter (est. 2 days) that wraps IdentityRegistry.verifyHandshake with Theseus's execution attestation hash.

rationale.agent_need: The authorization gap is structurally real — verifiable execution without scope binding is an incomplete trust model — but evidence of Theseus specifically lacking this and agents on Theseus actively needing it comes from absence-of-documentation inference rather than direct citations from Theseus materials or community demand signals.
rationale.zkp_edge: The non-ZKP alternative (EdDSA signature + ACL contract) is named implicitly but not rigorously compared; the key ZKP advantage — selective disclosure of requiredScopeMask without revealing full bitmask — is genuine but the atomic on-chain composition benefit could be approximated with a trusted attestation registry, so the structural failure of alternatives is understated.
rationale.primitive_readiness: Exact primitives are named (AgentPolicy circuit, PlonkVerifier at specific address, IdentityRegistry.verifyHandshake), modification scope is minimal (thin adapter + one overload + SDK additive option), and the 3.5-day estimate is realistic and well-decomposed.
rationale.partnership_leverage: The argument that Theseus cannot build this in <3 months is asserted without evidence of their team's ZKP capability or whether they have an identity solution in progress; the competitor alternatives (Lit Protocol MPC, Turnkey TEE attestation) are identified but the case that they are meaningfully worse for Theseus's specific architecture is not closed.

evidence.summary: Theseus is an agent-native L1 with verifiable AI execution but no documented identity/auth layer beyond raw keypairs — the authorization proof gap is confirmed by the absence of any scope-binding primitive in public materials.

Lit offers MPC-based programmable key management for agents with condition-based access control, but relies on threshold signing rather than ZKP scope commitments — no zero-knowledge selective disclosure of permission scope.

Turnkey provides secure enclave key management for agents (AWS Nitro TEE), enabling hardware-attested signing but no on-chain authorization scope proof — attestation proves execution environment, not permission boundary.
Privy targets embedded wallets for human users with social recovery; minimal agent-native support and no ZKP delegation — not a direct competitor in the agent-rooted trust chain space.

evidence.source: W3C DID Core + VC Data Model 2.0
evidence.summary: W3C DIDs and Verifiable Credentials define agent identity descriptors but provide no native ZKP scope-commitment binding to on-chain execution proofs — the atomic composition Bolyra proposes is not covered by existing VC spec.
Emerging IETF work on AI agent authentication acknowledges the authorization gap but proposes OAuth-style token delegation rather than ZKP-based scope commitments — no on-chain verifiability.
Fetch.ai uses DID-based agent identity on its Cosmos-based chain but relies on centralized registration without ZKP privacy or cumulative-bit scope enforcement — permission model is coarse-grained.
SingularityNET focuses on AI service marketplace payments rather than agent authorization proofs — no delegation circuit or scope-binding primitive.

Semaphore v4 ceremony (depth-20 Groth16) is the closest deployed ZKP identity primitive on EVM chains, but it proves group membership for humans only — no agent policy or scope-commitment binding to execution attestations.

Aztec's privacy L2 provides ZKP-native smart contracts but is EVM-adjacent, not agent-native L1 — no off-the-shelf AgentPolicy circuit or Poseidon3 scope-commitment adapter for external chain integration.
Aleo builds a ZKP-native L1 with private state, but Leo language circuits are Aleo-specific and not portable as Solidity adapters to EVM-compatible agent chains like Theseus.

ERC-4337 account abstraction and ERC-7579 modular accounts allow permission modules on EVM chains, but no ZKP scope-commitment primitive exists — Bolyra's Solidity adapter would be the first such module.

mapped_primitives.usage: The scope commitment Poseidon3(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment, expiryTimestamp) is the exact binding point. The circuit's requiredScopeMask selective disclosure output proves the agent had permission S without revealing the full bitmask. Theseus's execution attestation hash gets anchored to this commitment — an on-chain verifier can check 'agent had scope S AND computed output O' atomically. The expiry enforcement and EdDSA operator signature verification already cover the credential lifecycle.

verifyHandshake is the on-chain anchor. The Theseus adapter wraps this call: it passes the existing (humanProof, agentProof, nonce) tuple plus the Theseus execution attestation hash, verifies the Bolyra handshake first, then checks attestation hash inclusion in the same transaction. The existing nonce equality enforcement and delegation nullifier replay protection carry over.
AgentPolicy uses PLONK. The deployed PlonkVerifier at 0x28b99c48f19F1680a7D88733ffeb9AFbcB3B95Db handles proof verification without modification. The adapter contract calls through to this verifier.

If Theseus execution involves a sub-agent acting under delegated authority (e.g., human → orchestrator → worker), the Delegation circuit's composable chain (max 3 hops) and scope-narrowing guarantee extend the authorization proof through the delegation chain. The delegateeScope ⊆ delegatorScope invariant ensures scope cannot escalate at any hop. Optional for v1 but directly applicable for multi-agent Theseus pipelines.

proveHandshake(human, agent, options) accepts an options object — the Theseus attestation hash slots in as an additional binding value mixed into the sessionNonce (or passed as a separate public input to the adapter). createAgentCredential covers credential issuance before the Theseus run. No new SDK functions needed; options extension is additive.

new_components_needed.description: TheseusAuthAdapter.sol — thin Solidity adapter that calls IdentityRegistry.verifyHandshake then validates that the Theseus execution attestation hash (keccak256 of model hash + output commitment) matches the scope commitment bound in the AgentPolicy proof's public inputs. Emits AuthorizedExecution(agentId, scopeMask, attestationHash, blockNumber). No new verifier logic — delegates to existing PlonkVerifier and Groth16Verifier.

Add proveHandshake options.attestationHash: Hex field. When set, the SDK mixes it into the sessionNonce derivation so the proof is bound to the specific Theseus execution. Also add verifyHandshakeWithAttestation(humanProof, agentProof, nonce, attestationHash) → HandshakeResult that calls TheseusAuthAdapter instead of IdentityRegistry directly.

Theseus composition spec — one-page document defining the canonical attestation hash format (what Theseus must commit to: modelHash, inputHash, outputHash, agentId, timestamp), the nonce binding scheme, and the on-chain verification flow. Needed to make the integration portable across Theseus versions and auditable.

theseus-bolyra integration package — minimal TypeScript wrapper that takes a Theseus execution receipt, extracts the attestation hash, and calls @bolyra/sdk's verifyHandshakeWithAttestation. Analogous to langchain-bolyra-tool pattern already in integrations/.

theseus_integration_surface: On-chain: TheseusAuthAdapter.sol deployed on Base Sepolia calls IdentityRegistry.verifyHandshake (existing) then asserts attestationHash matches the public scope commitment output from the AgentPolicy PLONK proof — single transaction, atomic authorization + execution verification. Off-chain SDK bridge: proveHandshake() accepts Theseus's execution receipt hash in options, mixes it into sessionNonce so the ZK proof binds to that specific execution. Theseus's own verifier (whatever chain they run on) can read the TheseusAuthAdapter's AuthorizedExecution event as a cross-chain attestation that authorization was valid at execution time. No oracle, no bridge — the proof is self-contained in the calldata.

deliverables.name: TheseusAuthAdapter.sol
deliverables.description: Thin Solidity adapter: calls IdentityRegistry.verifyHandshake, then checks that keccak256(modelHash, outputCommitment) matches the attestationHash public input bound in the AgentPolicy proof. Emits AuthorizedExecution(agentId, scopeMask, attestationHash, blockNumber). No new verifier logic — delegates to existing PlonkVerifier.

deliverables.name: IdentityRegistry.sol patch
deliverables.description: Add verifyHandshakeWithAttestation(humanProof, agentProof, nonce, attestationHash) overload. Existing verifyHandshake remains unchanged. The attestationHash is passed through to TheseusAuthAdapter — no new verification logic in the registry itself.

deliverables.name: @bolyra/sdk attestation binding
deliverables.description: Extend proveHandshake(human, agent, options) to accept options.attestationHash. When present, mix it into sessionNonce as keccak256(sessionNonce, attestationHash) before witness generation. Additive — no breaking change to existing callers.

deliverables.name: Integration test suite
deliverables.description: Hardhat test: (1) issue AgentPolicy credential with permissionBitmask, (2) simulate Theseus execution attestation as keccak256(modelHash, outputCommitment), (3) call TheseusAuthAdapter.verifyAuthorizedExecution, (4) assert AuthorizedExecution event with correct scopeMask and attestationHash. Uses mock Theseus attestation until Theseus testnet is available.
deliverables.reuses: existing Hardhat test scaffolding, AgentPolicy circuit artifacts

deliverables.name: Theseus integration spec
deliverables.description: One-page spec for the Theseus team: attestation hash format (keccak256 input schema), adapter ABI, required public inputs from their execution environment, and how to call TheseusAuthAdapter from a Theseus agent's on-chain action. Reference implementation pointer to the integration test.

deliverables.name: Sunday call demo artifact
deliverables.description: Single-page visual: scope commitment binding diagram (AgentPolicy scope commitment → Theseus attestation anchor → on-chain atomic check), plus terminal output of the integration test passing against a mock attestation. Pre-built; no live proving needed on the call.
deliverables.reuses: existing AgentPolicy circuit, existing test output

spec.demo_description: Show the scope commitment binding diagram (5 min), then run the Hardhat integration test live (mock Theseus attestation): one on-chain transaction that calls TheseusAuthAdapter.verifyAuthorizedExecution, emits AuthorizedExecution event with scopeMask and attestationHash, passes. Demonstrates the full trust model — agent had scope S AND computed output O — atomically verified. No live ZK proving needed; use pre-generated proof artifacts from CI.
risks: Theseus execution environment may not be EVM-compatible — if it uses a custom VM, the Solidity adapter is irrelevant and the integration point becomes off-chain attestation verification instead. Confirm EVM compatibility in the Sunday call before writing any Solidity.

Theseus attestation hash format is unknown — the adapter assumes keccak256(modelHash, outputCommitment) but Theseus may use a different schema (Pedersen hash, Poseidon, raw ECDSA sig). Get their attestation spec before finalizing the adapter.
Theseus testnet may not exist yet — if they are pre-testnet, integration tests stay mocked and the deliverable is a deployable contract + spec rather than a live deployment.
Solo-founder time: 4.5 days assumes no interruptions. TheseusAuthAdapter.sol is the highest-value artifact — prioritize it first and treat the SDK extension as optional for the Sunday call.
AgentPolicy PLONK proof generation takes ~8s per proof on a MacBook — if the Sunday demo runs live proving, it may feel slow. Use pre-generated proof artifacts instead.
spec.success_criteria: A single on-chain transaction on Base Sepolia (or Theseus testnet once available) that: (1) verifies a valid Bolyra AgentPolicy proof binding permissionBitmask to credentialCommitment and expiryTimestamp, (2) checks that the Theseus execution attestation hash matches the scope commitment public input, and (3) emits AuthorizedExecution(agentId, scopeMask, attestationHash, blockNumber) — all in one atomically-reverting call. TheseusAuthAdapter integration test must pass in CI with FULL_PROOF=1.
spec.theseus_dependencies: Need from Theseus before finalizing adapter: (1) EVM compatibility confirmation or custom VM ABI, (2) attestation hash schema — exact inputs to keccak256 or alternative hash function used for execution attestation, (3) testnet RPC endpoint and faucet access, (4) any existing agent identity/keypair format (EdDSA curve, key derivation path) so AgentPolicy EdDSA operator signature verification aligns. These are blocking only for the production adapter — the Sunday demo works with mocks.

tier3_findings: AXIS 2 FAIL — Selective disclosure of requiredScopeMask has weak value in an agent-native L1 context. Theseus agents hold their own keys and ARE the principals; permission bitmask visibility is generally desirable for on-chain composability, not a liability. The atomic composition claim ('agent had scope S AND computed output O') is achievable with EdDSA signature over (executionHash, scopeCommitment) plus an on-chain ACL registry — no ZKP overhead required. The proposal identifies this gap but does not close the argument that selective disclosure has economic or security value here; it simply asserts it. This is the proposal's structural weakness.

AXIS 4 CONCERN — Theseus is an L1 with a custom agent runtime. The proposal assumes EVM-compatible Solidity verifier deployment in a '2-day adapter' estimate. If Theseus runs a custom VM (highly probable for an 'agent-native L1'), this estimate is fiction — implementing a Groth16 verifier in a non-EVM environment is weeks of work minimum, potentially requiring a new proof system entirely. The call on June 29 must resolve this before any engineering begins.

AXIS 1 CONCERN — The non-ZKP counterfactual is real. Theseus engineers with standard Solidity + OpenZeppelin experience can build EdDSA-signed scope commitments anchored to execution attestations in under 3 weeks without touching Circom. The barrier to DIY is not ZKP expertise but the marginal value of the privacy layer — which is currently unproven for their use case. Bolyra's moat is the production-ready circuit and SDK, not the concept.

AXIS 3 CONCERN — No specific agent use case on Theseus is named that fails without ZKP scope binding at launch. The authorization gap is structurally real but the operational need is theoretical at Theseus's current scale ($100K funded, pre-mainnet). The need sharpens at delegation chain depth > 2 and multi-operator scenarios, which are 12+ months out for Theseus.

AXIS 5 PASS — The Solidity adapter pattern (wrapping verifyHandshake with an external attestation hash) is a reusable primitive for any agent chain with execution attestations. If Theseus pivots or fails, the adapter generalizes to Fetch.ai, any EVM-compatible agent platform, or Bolyra's own hosted proving service. Work is not wasted.

CONDITIONAL GATE — This proposal should not advance to sprint work until the June 29 call confirms: (1) Theseus VM is EVM-compatible or provides a Solidity execution environment; (2) Theseus can articulate a concrete privacy or regulatory requirement that forces scope hiding rather than on-chain transparency; (3) Theseus has at least one live agent use case requiring multi-operator delegation within 6 months.
tier3_summary: Structurally sound idea undermined by a weak ZKP value proposition in an agent-native context where permission transparency beats privacy, and a critical unknown (VM architecture) that could make the 2-day adapter estimate off by an order of magnitude — gate on June 29 call answers before committing any engineering.

The atomic composition claim ('agent had scope S AND computed output O') is achievable with EdDSA signature over (executionHash, scopeCommitment) plus an on-chain ACL registry — no ZKP overhead required. The proposal identifies this gap but does not close the argument that selective disclosure has economic or security value here; it simply asserts it. This is the proposal's structural weakness.

If Theseus runs a custom VM (highly probable for an 'agent-native L1'), this estimate is fiction — implementing a Groth16 verifier in a non-EVM environment is weeks of work minimum, potentially requiring a new proof system entirely.
