The Poseidon3 scope commitment, scope blinding salt, and constraint correctness (no under-constrained Merkle depth, no brute-forceable scope commitments) require circuit expertise that Theseus's 1-2 engineers almost certainly lack. The 'circomlib directly' escape hatch fails because the hard part is the circuit security properties, not the proving system wiring. The protocol-autoresearch experiments (scope_blinding_salt, scope_commitment_brute_force) document the non-obvious attack surface. DOES NOT kill.

AXIS 2 CONCERN — The proposal bundles two distinct value claims: (a) VERIFIABILITY (counterparty can confirm bit 2 is set) and (b) PRIVACY (bits 5-7 stay hidden). The verifiability claim is strong — self-attestation in a marketplace is trivially gameable. The privacy claim is weaker: at Theseus launch-scale (likely <100 agents), competitive intelligence value of hiding SUB_DELEGATE and ACCESS_PII from service counterparties is speculative. If the privacy motivation evaporates under scrutiny, the case narrows to 'verifiable capability claims' — which BBS+ could theoretically address without on-chain ZKP. Deduct on the weakest dimension but does not kill.

AXIS 3 CONCERN — No Theseus documentation confirms an agent marketplace exists or is on the roadmap. The Fetch.ai Agentverse analogy is valid structurally but Theseus is pre-mainnet with $100K in funding. The concrete first use case (Agent A advertises '$100 financial services', Agent B verifies before transacting) is plausible but invented, not observed. The need only becomes operationally real when agents are transacting with unknown counterparties at volume — unlikely in first 12 months given team size. Demand is speculative.

AXIS 4 CONCERN — This is the highest-stakes unknown. The PlonkVerifier at 0x28b99c... is deployed on Base Sepolia (EVM). If Theseus runs a non-EVM custom VM, none of the existing verifier infrastructure ports without implementing a native PLONK verifier from scratch — weeks of work, not 3-4 days. The 3-4 day estimate only covers the SDK wrapper and DID service endpoint spec; it assumes Theseus can call verifyProof() on an EVM contract. The call context explicitly flags 'possibly non-EVM' as open. This is a binary gate: EVM-compatible = CONDITIONAL proceed; non-EVM = integration cost overwhelms the value.

AXIS 5 PASS — The work is highly portable. The DID CapabilityAttestation service endpoint spec is useful for any agent marketplace (Fetch.ai, Autonolas, future L1s). The SDK wrapper around AgentPolicy selective disclosure is a general primitive. No new circuit work required (AgentPolicy PLONK already exists). The 3-4 day investment produces artifacts that serve Bolyra's protocol regardless of Theseus's fate. No single-partner dependency risk.

title: Chain-scoped nullifier binding for Theseus↔Base cross-chain agent auth
description: Bolyra's existing nullifier scheme hashes (credentialCommitment, sessionNonce) but is chain-blind — a Theseus agent credential proof replayed on Base Sepolia would pass verification today. Adding chain_id as a public input to AgentPolicy's nullifier hash (nullifier = H(credentialCommitment, sessionNonce, chain_id)) creates cryptographically-enforced domain separation at the circuit level. This means a Theseus agent can present the same underlying credential on multiple chains without the ZKP verifier accepting cross-chain replays. The fix is a single-line Circom change to AgentPolicy and a matching chain_id public signal in the Solidity verifier — but the protocol guarantee is fundamental to safe multi-chain operation. Without this, any cross-chain Bolyra deployment is vulnerable to replay across chains sharing the same BN254 curve.

rationale.agent_need: The Theseus 'no human key path' structural constraint is cited as motivation, but the cross-chain replay vulnerability is inherent to any multi-chain ZKP deployment — not specific to Theseus agents — and no Theseus documentation or community discussion directly evidences demand for chain-scoped nullifiers.
rationale.zkp_edge: The non-ZKP alternative (wallet-level key scoping as used by Coinbase AgentKit) is named and its structural failure mode identified — it cannot enforce domain separation at proof-verification time without trusting the wallet layer — making the ZKP circuit-level guarantee genuinely superior.
rationale.primitive_readiness: Exact circuits (AgentPolicy.circom, Delegation.circom), contract functions (PlonkVerifier, DelegationPlonkVerifier), and SDK surface are named with realistic per-deliverable day estimates; PLONK's universal SRS avoids ceremony overhead, making the 2-day total credible.
rationale.partnership_leverage: The argument that Theseus cannot build this in 3 months relies on assumed ZKP inexperience with no assessment of their team's crypto background, and the availability of Lit Protocol and Coinbase AgentKit as drop-in alternatives weakens the exclusivity claim.

evidence.source: theseuschain.com (fetch blocked — context provided by caller)
evidence.summary: Theseus is an agent-native L1 funded by Orange DAO; its 'no human key path' constraint means agents hold raw EdDSA keypairs with no human root — creating a structural identity gap Bolyra can fill.

Lit offers MPC-based programmable key pairs with cross-chain support for agents, but relies on threshold signatures rather than ZKP — no nullifier-level domain separation or permission-bit delegation circuits.

Coinbase AgentKit/CDP agentic wallets provide key custody for AI agents on Base, but offer no ZKP layer — cross-chain replay is addressed only by wallet-level key scoping, not cryptographic circuit enforcement.
Turnkey provides secure HSM-backed key management for agents and enterprises but is human-policy-centric with no ZKP delegation model or chain-scoped nullifier scheme.

Privy's server wallets support agent use cases with policy enforcement, but identity is wallet-address-scoped with no ZKP proof layer — agents can't prove bounded permissions without exposing credentials.

Emerging IETF draft for AI agent authentication covers delegation chains and scope attestation but is OAuth/JWT-based with no cryptographic nullifier or chain-ID binding — ZKP approach is complementary, not redundant.
did:key and DID methods are the de-facto agent identity layer but are chain-agnostic with no replay protection across EVM chains — nullifier domain separation is an open gap in DID specs.

EIP-712 includes chain_id in domain separator for signature replay protection — the exact same principle Bolyra would encode in its ZKP nullifier, giving Bolyra a well-understood analogue to reference in demos.

Fetch.ai has an agent-native Cosmos SDK chain with key-based identity; cross-chain agent auth is handled via IBC relayers, not ZKP — no nullifier scheme, replay protection is at the IBC packet level only.
Autonolas runs multi-agent services across multiple EVM chains using multi-sig safe accounts; no ZKP identity layer — cross-chain replay is not addressed at the identity primitive level.
Virtuals tokenizes AI agents on Base; identity is token-ownership-based with no cryptographic delegation or ZKP nullifier scheme — consumer-facing, not infrastructure-grade identity.

Semaphore uses nullifiers for double-spend prevention in anonymous signaling groups on Ethereum — does not bind nullifier to chain_id, meaning the same gap Bolyra's fix addresses exists in Semaphore deployments.

World ID uses ZKP nullifiers for Sybil-resistant human identity across chains, but the nullifier includes an action/app scope rather than chain_id — cross-chain replay is handled by app-scoped external nullifiers, not circuit-level chain binding.
Aztec implements privacy via ZKP at the L2 computation level; agent identity is not a first-class primitive — focused on private DeFi, not agent authorization.

evidence.source: BN254 curve cross-chain replay — known EVM vulnerability class
evidence.summary: All EVM-compatible chains share the BN254 precompiles, making ZKP proofs verified via ecPairing replayable across chains unless the verifying contract or circuit binds chain_id — this is a documented class of vulnerability with no off-the-shelf ZKP-agent solution.

mapped_primitives.usage: Primary circuit requiring modification. The nullifier currently computes Poseidon3(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment, expiryTimestamp) for the scope commitment, but the replay vulnerability is in the proof's public outputs — adding chain_id as a fourth Poseidon input to the identity-bound scope commitment (or as a standalone public signal committed into the nullifier hash) cryptographically domain-separates proofs per chain. The PLONK build is preferred here since it avoids a per-circuit Groth16 ceremony when public inputs expand.

Delegation chains inherit the AgentPolicy credential commitment as their root. If AgentPolicy adds chain_id binding, the Delegation circuit's delegator-credential-commitment link (UC3.1 fix) must also incorporate chain_id in its scope commitment chain, otherwise a cross-chain attacker can strip the delegation envelope and resubmit the base AgentPolicy proof on a different chain. The scope commitment chain linking already exists — chain_id just needs to thread through it.

Adding chain_id as a public input to AgentPolicy changes the number of public signals, which invalidates the existing vkey.json and thus the deployed PlonkVerifier at 0x28b99c48. A new verifier must be snarkjs-exported from the updated .zkey and redeployed. PLONK's universal SRS (pot16.ptau) means no new trusted setup ceremony — just re-export and redeploy.

Same regeneration requirement as PlonkVerifier: if Delegation circuit adds chain_id to its public signal set, the DelegationPlonkVerifier at 0x285D2E must be regenerated from the updated vkey and redeployed.

The atomic handshake verification function must pass chain_id as a public signal when calling PlonkVerifier.verifyProof(). The root history buffer and nonce equality logic are unaffected. The contract needs a chain_id immutable set at deploy time (or read from block.chainid) so the verifier call is self-consistent — each chain's IdentityRegistry only accepts proofs bound to its own chain_id.

proveHandshake(human, agent, options) and the internal AgentPolicy witness builder must accept and forward chain_id into the circuit's public input vector. verifyHandshake must validate that the chain_id in the proof matches the expected deployment chain. The options bag already exists on proveHandshake, so chain_id threads in without a breaking API change.

new_components_needed.description: AgentPolicy.circom patch: add chain_id as a public input signal and include it in the Poseidon hash that produces the identity-bound scope commitment (or as a direct input to the nullifier hash). Constraint delta is negligible (~1-2 constraints for the additional Poseidon input). Requires re-running the PLONK trusted setup (snarkjs plonk setup using existing pot16.ptau — no new ceremony) and exporting a new vkey.

Delegation.circom patch: thread chain_id through the delegator-scope-commitment chain so the scope commitment chain linking remains consistent end-to-end across hops. Requires re-running PLONK setup and exporting new DelegationPlonkVerifier.

Regenerated PlonkVerifier and DelegationPlonkVerifier Solidity contracts (snarkjs exportSolidityVerifier from updated vkeys). Deploy to Base Sepolia and Theseus. Update IdentityRegistry to read block.chainid as the chain_id immutable and pass it in the verifyProof public signals array.

Add chain_id parameter to proveHandshake options (defaults to current chain from provider, overridable for cross-chain pre-computation). Update AgentPolicy witness builder, verifyHandshake chain_id assertion, and TypeScript types. Bump to @bolyra/sdk v0.2.1 (non-breaking addition).

Update draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 section on nullifier computation to formally specify chain_id domain separation. Add conformance test vectors covering cross-chain replay rejection (extend test-vectors.json with ~4 new vectors: valid same-chain, rejected cross-chain Base→Theseus, rejected cross-chain Theseus→Base, valid independent proof on each chain). Update did:bolyra method spec to note chain_id scoping of agent DIDs.

theseus_integration_surface: Theseus deploys a fresh IdentityRegistry (with chain_id = Theseus chain_id immutable) plus the regenerated PlonkVerifier and DelegationPlonkVerifier to their chain. Bolyra agents targeting Theseus call proveHandshake(..., { chainId: theseusChainId }) via @bolyra/sdk — the circuit binds the proof to Theseus's chain_id at witness-generation time. The IdentityRegistry on Base Sepolia independently holds its own chain_id binding. An agent operating across both chains generates two independent proofs (same underlying credential, different chain_id inputs) — the ZKP verifier on each chain rejects the other chain's proof by construction. The integration surface for Theseus is therefore: (a) the IdentityRegistry deployment on Theseus's chain, (b) the SDK's chainId option in proveHandshake, and (c) an optional thin RPC bridge if Theseus agents need to present Base Sepolia root history proofs (cross-chain Merkle root sync, which is a separate tracked opportunity). No API layer or off-chain relay is required for the core domain separation fix.

deliverables.name: AgentPolicy.circom chain_id binding
deliverables.description: Add chain_id as a 4th public input to the Poseidon nullifier hash: nullifier = Poseidon4(credentialCommitment, sessionNonce, chain_id, expiryTimestamp). Single-line change + new public signal declaration. PLONK build preferred — no new trusted setup since pot16.ptau covers expanded public input vector.

deliverables.name: Delegation.circom chain_id threading
deliverables.description: Thread chain_id through the delegator-credential-commitment scope chain so a stripped delegation envelope can't be replayed on a different chain with the base AgentPolicy proof. chain_id added to the delegatorScopeCommitment Poseidon hash — mirrors the AgentPolicy change structurally.

deliverables.name: Circuit recompile + vkey regeneration
deliverables.description: Run npm run compile:circuits to rebuild .r1cs and .zkey for both AgentPolicy and Delegation (Groth16 + PLONK). Export updated vkey.json files via snarkjs. No new ceremony — pot16.ptau is the universal SRS. Validate witness generation with updated test vectors.

deliverables.name: PlonkVerifier + DelegationPlonkVerifier redeployment
deliverables.description: Export new Solidity verifier contracts from updated vkey.json files via snarkjs exportSolidityVerifier. Deploy both to Base Sepolia. Record new contract addresses. Groth16 verifiers get same treatment — two deploys total per circuit = 4 contract deployments.

deliverables.name: IdentityRegistry chain_id immutable
deliverables.description: Add `uint256 public immutable CHAIN_ID = block.chainid` set in constructor. Pass CHAIN_ID as public signal in the verifyProof call inside the atomic handshake function. Each chain's IdentityRegistry self-enforces its own chain_id — no cross-chain proof accepted. Root history buffer and nonce logic untouched.

deliverables.name: @bolyra/sdk chain_id option
deliverables.description: Thread chainId into proveHandshake(human, agent, { chainId }) options bag — no breaking API change. Internal AgentPolicy witness builder forwards chainId to the public input vector. verifyHandshake validates proof.publicSignals[chainId_index] matches expected chain. Update TypeScript types + JSDoc. Python SDK subprocess bridge picks this up automatically via the JS call.

deliverables.name: Cross-chain replay integration tests
deliverables.description: Test suite verifying: (1) proof generated with chainId=84532 (Base Sepolia) fails on a verifier expecting chainId=1; (2) same credential works on the correct chain; (3) delegation chain with chain_id threading rejects cross-chain stripped proof; (4) SDK throws descriptive error on chain_id mismatch. Uses existing Mocha + Hardhat test harness.

deliverables.name: Theseus integration spec (call prep)
deliverables.description: Two-page technical spec covering: Bolyra's chain-scoped nullifier model, how it fills Theseus's no-human-key-path identity gap, agent-rooted trust chain via AgentPolicy + Delegation circuits, proposed Theseus chain_id registration (they need to assign a canonical chain_id), and the 3-call integration API (proveHandshake, verifyHandshake, onchain IdentityRegistry.atomicVerify). Includes a sequence diagram. Shareable before the Sunday call.

spec.demo_description: For the Sunday June 29 call: show the Theseus integration spec doc + the 3-line Circom diff that adds chain_id binding + a live terminal demo of proveHandshake failing with the wrong chainId (witness generation error). Enough to establish technical credibility and drive the 'what chain_id will Theseus register?' conversation. A deployed end-to-end proof on Base Sepolia is 5 days out — not Sunday.
risks: Theseus VM compatibility unknown: if Theseus is not EVM-compatible the Solidity PlonkVerifier approach doesn't translate directly — need to know their execution environment before investing in contract deployment
Theseus mainnet timeline unclear: if they're 6+ months from launch the integration is premature and the sprint is better spent on Base Sepolia proof-of-concept that Theseus can observe

PLONK constraint count change: adding a public signal changes the constraint fingerprint — if existing .zkey files have hardcoded constraint counts, compile will fail silently and produce a mismatched verifier; must diff r1cs stats before/after
Solo capacity: 5-day estimate assumes no interruptions; circuit compile + vkey export alone can surface snarkjs version mismatches that eat a half-day
Theseus chain_id registration: without a canonical chain_id from Theseus the nullifier domain separation is unanchored — this is a blocker for production but not for the Sunday demo
spec.success_criteria: A proof generated by an agent on Theseus's chain_id is accepted by the Theseus IdentityRegistry and rejected by the Base Sepolia IdentityRegistry (and vice versa), verified by the integration test suite with zero false acceptances across 100 cross-chain replay attempts. SDK API remains non-breaking: existing proveHandshake callers without chainId option default to current chain via block.chainid.
spec.theseus_dependencies: Three asks before implementation is production-ready: (1) canonical chain_id they will register — needed to anchor nullifier domain separation; (2) EVM compatibility confirmation — determines whether Solidity PlonkVerifier deploys directly or needs a Theseus-native verifier adapter; (3) testnet RPC endpoint + faucet access so the IdentityRegistry can be deployed and end-to-end tested on Theseus before mainnet. For Sunday, only ask #2 is blocking.

tier3_findings: AXIS 1 (Theseus Builds It Themselves) — CONCERN: The proposal itself describes the fix as 'a single-line Circom change.' Any team building an agent-native L1 with ZKP ambitions can execute a one-line Circom edit and regenerate a PLONK proving key in a day. The complexity is not in the circuit change itself but in the trusted setup ceremony — and the card claims PLONK's universal SRS avoids that overhead entirely. If Theseus already uses snarkjs or circomlib for any proving, they replicate this in under a week. The defense that Theseus lacks ZKP expertise is unsubstantiated: a funded team building a ZKP-adjacent L1 likely has at least one engineer who can read Circom.

AXIS 2 (Standard Crypto Suffices) — FAIL: This is the card's fatal weakness. EIP-712 domain separation — explicitly cited as the 'well-understood analogue' — already solves chain-scoped replay for signed credentials at zero ZKP overhead. Theseus agents today use raw EdDSA keypairs. For that stack, you add chain_id to the signing domain and replay attacks are cryptographically prevented. The ZKP nullifier approach only adds value if the agent is already generating Bolyra ZKP proofs. That precondition is not established for Theseus. The proposal is solving a problem that exists only within the ZKP ecosystem, then arguing Theseus needs ZKP because the problem exists — circular reasoning.

AXIS 3 (No Agent Actually Needs This) — FAIL: The card names zero concrete Theseus agent use cases. The replay vulnerability requires: (a) Theseus agents adopt Bolyra ZKP credential proofs, AND (b) the same credential is presented on both Theseus and Base Sepolia, AND (c) an adversary attempts a cross-chain replay. None of these conditions are documented as present or imminent. The vulnerability is real for multi-chain ZKP deployments generally, but there is no evidence any Theseus agent is in or near that situation at launch or within 12 months. This is a solution to a hypothetical problem that requires a prior architectural commitment (adopting Bolyra) to even become relevant.

AXIS 4 (Integration Complexity vs Value) — CONCERN: The Bolyra-side work is genuinely trivial (1 Circom line, regenerate PLONK key, update Solidity verifier). But the value delivered to Theseus requires answering an open question the card avoids: what is Theseus's VM? If non-EVM, the Solidity verifier cannot be deployed. The card assumes EVM-compatible verification but the Theseus context explicitly lists VM type as unknown. If Theseus needs a custom Groth16/PLONK verifier in their native execution environment, the integration cost explodes regardless of how small the Bolyra change is.

AXIS 5 (Single-Partner Dependency) — PASS: This is the card's one genuinely strong axis. The nullifier chain_id binding is a protocol-level correctness fix that applies to every multi-chain Bolyra deployment. The work produces a reusable circuit artifact and updated verifier contracts. Even if Theseus disappears before the call ends, Bolyra should make this fix. The Theseus angle is essentially a framing device for a protocol improvement that stands on its own merit.

STRUCTURAL DIAGNOSIS: This proposal is a legitimate Bolyra protocol fix (the chain-blind nullifier is a real vulnerability class, BN254 cross-chain replay is documented) wrapped in Theseus partnership framing that cannot survive scrutiny. Two axes fail outright because the ZKP value proposition is only relevant after Theseus has already adopted Bolyra — the card cannot argue Theseus needs the fix without first establishing the adoption. The fix should be shipped as a Bolyra v0.3 protocol change, not positioned as a Theseus-specific integration deliverable.
tier3_summary: Ship the nullifier fix unconditionally as a Bolyra protocol correctness patch, but do not lead with it as a Theseus partnership value proposition — the ZKP advantage is only relevant after Theseus adopts Bolyra, making the argument circular, and EIP-712 domain separation already solves the same problem for their current EdDSA-keypair stack.

title: Authorization proof layer completing Theseus's verifiable execution story
description: Theseus advertises 'verifiable AI execution on-chain' — but verifiable execution without verifiable authorization is an incomplete security story. You can prove WHAT a model computed; you cannot prove WHETHER that agent was authorized to act. Bolyra provides the missing half: a Groth16/PLONK proof that this agent held permission bits 0b00000111, delegated by entity X, not expired, not replayed. The partnership pitch to Theseus's investors (Orange DAO / future Series A) is: 'Theseus proves the computation; Bolyra proves the mandate.' This is a 3-month roadmap item requiring a joint spec that defines how Theseus's execution attestation format (unknown — TEE? ZKP? optimistic?) composes with Bolyra's authorization proof in a single on-chain handshake. It's worth raising on Sunday to plant the narrative seed, but the concrete deliverable is a joint whitepaper / IETF draft amendment (extending draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 with an agent-execution attestation binding) that both teams co-author. This differentiates Theseus from every other agent L1 in the market.

rationale.agent_need: The 'no human key path' architectural constraint creates a structural need for authorization proofs, but the evidence is largely inferred from Theseus's design rather than citing documented pain points or community discussion.
rationale.zkp_edge: Lit Protocol (MPC, no scope proof) and Turnkey (TEE attestation, no self-contained proof) are named and their structural failures articulated, but the gap analysis assumes Theseus needs a verifiable on-chain proof artifact rather than just trusting their own execution layer.
rationale.primitive_readiness: AgentPolicy and Delegation circuits map cleanly to the need with only minor modifications (Poseidon3→Poseidon4), but the IdentityRegistry requires major work for cross-chain deployment on Theseus's L1, and Theseus's attestation format is explicitly unknown, making the integration spec speculative.
rationale.partnership_leverage: The complementary narrative ('Theseus proves computation; Bolyra proves mandate') is credible and differentiated, and Bolyra's ZKP circuit expertise (Circom, trusted setup) represents a genuine build-vs-buy moat, but Theseus's identity roadmap and whether they've already engaged Lit/Turnkey is unknown.

evidence.summary: Theseus is an agent-native L1 with no human key path — agents hold their own keys and persist on-chain, making raw EdDSA keypairs the presumed identity primitive with no delegation or permission layer documented.

x402 is an HTTP-native micropayment protocol for AI agents; its presence in Theseus's orbit suggests Theseus is building toward agent economic autonomy, where authorization scope (spending limits, permission bits) is a natural adjacent need.

Lit offers Programmable Key Pairs (PKPs) for agent signing with condition-based access control, but uses threshold MPC not ZKP — no cryptographic proof of permission scope is emitted, making it opaque to on-chain verifiers.

Turnkey provides secure key management via TEE-backed signing with policy rules, but produces no standardized proof artifact — verification requires trusting Turnkey's attestation infrastructure rather than a self-contained ZK proof.
Privy is human-centric embedded wallet auth; no agent-to-agent delegation primitive and no ZKP layer — not a direct competitor for the Theseus use case.

evidence.source: IETF draft-ietf-oauth-agentic-auth (in-progress as of training cutoff)
evidence.summary: IETF OAuth WG has active work on agentic authorization patterns extending OAuth 2.0; the draft addresses delegated agent identity but relies on bearer tokens, not ZK proofs — Bolyra's IETF draft amendment to add ZKP binding would be genuinely novel.

evidence.source: W3C DID Core + DID-linked Verifiable Credentials
evidence.summary: W3C VC/DID infrastructure is the nearest standards-track analog to what Bolyra proposes, but VC presentation requires revealing the credential — ZK selective disclosure is a known gap explicitly called out in the VC-DI spec.
Fetch.ai agents use DID-rooted identity within their Agentverse but rely on centralized ACL-style registration, not cryptographic delegation proofs; no on-chain ZK authorization primitive exists.
Autonolas defines autonomous services with on-chain service NFTs encoding operator multi-sig, but no ZKP scope-narrowing delegation — closest architectural analog to Theseus among live agent L1s.
World ID is the most widely deployed ZK identity proof on L1s (Ethereum, OP), but is human-uniqueness only — no agent credential primitive and no permission-bit delegation circuit.

Semaphore v4 (the ceremony Bolyra reuses for HumanUniqueness) is deployed on Ethereum mainnet for anonymous group membership proofs; Bolyra's AgentPolicy + Delegation circuits extend this pattern into machine-credential space with no prior art.

Polygon ID ships ZK-native verifiable credentials on EVM chains, but targets human identity with a centralized issuer model — no agent-to-agent delegation circuit and no 8-bit cumulative permission encoding.
Aztec's privacy L2 uses ZK for transaction confidentiality but not for identity delegation — different problem space with no agent authorization primitive.

mapped_primitives.usage: Core authorization proof primitive. The selective-disclosure requiredScopeMask already proves 'this agent held permission bits 0b00000111 at time T, not expired.' For Theseus, the binding point is adding Theseus's execution attestation hash as an additional public input committed into the identity-bound scope commitment (Poseidon3 → Poseidon4). This transforms AgentPolicy from a standalone authorization proof into a statement: 'this agent was authorized AND this specific execution was covered by that authorization.' The PLONK build avoids a new trusted setup.

Proves the mandate chain behind the executing agent — human operator → agent → sub-agent up to 3 hops, with scope narrowing and expiry narrowing enforced in-circuit. For Theseus this answers 'WHO delegated authority to this agent for this action,' which is the missing provenance story Theseus's execution proof cannot provide. The delegatee scope commitment chain can be extended to include the execution context hash, linking the delegation token to the specific Theseus execution attestation.

Atomic handshake verification (Groth16 + PLONK in single tx) and root-history buffers are the on-chain settlement layer. For Theseus, the registry needs to either be deployed on Theseus's L1 or a cross-chain proof relay must be built. The existing delegation nullifier replay protection and hop-count enforcement are directly reusable as-is — they do not depend on which chain hosts the verifier.

Optional but valuable for Theseus's investor narrative: proves the human operator who issued the agent credential is unique (Semaphore v4 Merkle membership + scope-bound nullifier). Addresses sybil resistance at the operator layer — one human, one root of authorization. No modification needed; integrates via the existing proveHandshake path.

The IETF draft is the coordination artifact for the joint whitepaper deliverable. Needs a new section — 'Agent Execution Attestation Binding' — defining (a) the attestation_hash wire format, (b) how it enters the AgentPolicy public inputs, (c) the composed verification predicate. This section is the concrete output that differentiates the Theseus partnership from a generic integration and provides the narrative anchor for Orange DAO / Series A positioning.

PLONK verifier contract must be redeployed on Theseus's L1 (or a bridge must relay proof verification calls back to Base Sepolia). The verifier itself is stateless and chain-agnostic — same Solidity artifact, new deployment. This is the minimal on-chain footprint Bolyra needs on the Theseus side.

new_components_needed.description: ExecutionAttestationBinding: extend AgentPolicy to accept executionAttestationHash (Poseidon hash of Theseus's execution output) as a 5th public input and fold it into the scope commitment (Poseidon4 instead of Poseidon3). Keeps the existing requiredScopeMask selective disclosure intact. PLONK build only — no new trusted setup needed. Blocked on Theseus disclosing their attestation format (TEE report hash? ZKP public outputs? optimistic receipt hash?).

TheseusAuthGateway: contract deployed on Theseus's L1 that (1) accepts a Theseus execution receipt, (2) accepts a Bolyra AgentPolicy PLONK proof with executionAttestationHash matching the receipt hash, and (3) emits an AuthorizedExecution event only when both verify. Wraps the redeployed PlonkVerifier. Complexity is high because Theseus's execution receipt format is unknown — interface is a stub until Theseus shares their attestation spec.

IETF draft amendment: new Section 6 'Agent Execution Attestation Binding' in draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01. Defines the attestation_hash wire type, public input layout for ExecutionAttestationBinding, the composed verification predicate (authorization_proof ∧ execution_attestation → authorized_execution), and security considerations for TEE vs ZKP vs optimistic attestation sources. Co-authored with Theseus team.

proveAuthorizedExecution(agentCredential, executionAttestation, options): new SDK function that wraps AgentPolicy proof generation with the execution attestation hash bound as public input. Returns {authProof, executionAttestationHash, nonce} suitable for submission to TheseusAuthGateway. Requires ExecutionAttestationBinding circuit artifacts to be available.

@bolyra/theseus: adapter package that bridges Bolyra SDK with Theseus's chain SDK — handles receipt ingestion, attestation hash derivation, proveAuthorizedExecution call, and TheseusAuthGateway submission. Stub-able until Theseus's SDK is public.

theseus_integration_surface: Three integration surfaces, in dependency order. (1) Spec layer first: extend draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 jointly with Theseus — this defines the executionAttestationHash wire type and is the prerequisite for all circuit and contract work; no code needed, just a co-authored IETF section. (2) Verifier contract on Theseus's L1: deploy PlonkVerifier (stateless, same artifact) plus TheseusAuthGateway (new contract) on Theseus's chain; this is the on-chain settlement point where Theseus execution receipts and Bolyra authorization proofs meet in a single atomic transaction. (3) SDK bridge: @bolyra/theseus adapter that agents call at runtime — ingests the Theseus execution receipt, derives the attestation hash, calls proveAuthorizedExecution, and submits the composed proof to TheseusAuthGateway. The Sunday narrative seed should anchor on surface (1) only: 'Theseus proves the computation; Bolyra proves the mandate — let's co-author the spec that composes them.' Surfaces (2) and (3) follow once Theseus's attestation format is disclosed.

deliverables.name: Sunday narrative deck (1-pager)
deliverables.description: Single-page pitch artifact: 'Theseus proves the computation; Bolyra proves the mandate.' Diagram showing AgentPolicy proof + Theseus execution attestation composing into a single on-chain predicate. No code. Plant narrative seed, extract open questions (VM type, attestation format, testnet ETA).

deliverables.name: AgentPolicy v2 circuit — attestation_hash binding
deliverables.description: Extend AgentPolicy.circom: add `attestation_hash` as a 5th public input. Swap Poseidon3 scope commitment for Poseidon4(operatorPubKey, permissionBits, expiry, attestation_hash). PLONK build avoids new trusted setup. Proves 'agent held permission bits AND this specific Theseus execution was covered by that authorization.'

deliverables.name: Delegation circuit — execution context binding
deliverables.description: Extend Delegation.circom: thread attestation_hash as an additional public input through the delegatee scope commitment chain. Enables proof of 'WHO delegated authority AND for THIS execution.' Scope-narrowing and expiry-narrowing logic unchanged — one-way constraint still holds.

deliverables.name: IETF draft amendment — section 7: Agent Execution Attestation Binding
deliverables.description: New section in draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 defining: (a) attestation_hash wire format (TEE quote hash | ZKP output hash | optimistic commitment hash — one field regardless of Theseus's proving system), (b) how it enters AgentPolicy public inputs, (c) the composed 2-proof verification predicate, (d) security properties (replay protection via sessionNonce already in handshake). This is the coordination artifact for Orange DAO / Series A narrative.

deliverables.name: PoC demo harness
deliverables.description: Node.js script: generate a mock Theseus execution attestation hash, run AgentPolicy v2 PLONK proof with it as public input, verify on-chain against the existing IdentityRegistry (local Hardhat). Outputs a JSON showing both proofs composed. Readable by non-engineers. Target: 2-week deliverable to share with Eric Wang post-call.

deliverables.name: Theseus VM adapter (conditional)
deliverables.description: IF Theseus is EVM-compatible: redeploy existing PlonkVerifier.sol + IdentityRegistry.sol on Theseus testnet, add attestation_hash to verifyHandshake() calldata. IF non-EVM: build a cross-chain proof relay that posts a Merkle root of verified handshake proofs to Theseus via a light client. This is the major unknown — do not start until Theseus confirms VM architecture.
deliverables.reuses: PlonkVerifier, IdentityRegistry (if EVM); new if non-EVM

deliverables.name: SDK attestation helper (TypeScript)
deliverables.description: Add `bindExecutionAttestation(agentCredential, attestationHash)` to @bolyra/sdk. Returns an augmented credential ready for AgentPolicy v2 proving. 10 lines of glue code over existing createAgentCredential path.

spec.demo_description: Sunday: show the existing Bolyra handshake demo (AgentPolicy PLONK proof + delegation chain) running live, walk through the 1-pager narrative, and pose the three gating questions: (1) What is Theseus's execution attestation format — TEE quote, ZKP output, or optimistic hash? (2) Is the Theseus VM EVM-compatible? (3) Can we get testnet access + an engineering contact for joint spec authoring? No new code for Sunday — the narrative artifact is the entire Sunday deliverable.
risks: Theseus VM is non-EVM: cross-chain relay adds 10-15 days and introduces a liveness dependency — scope to EVM-only for v1

Theseus 'no human key path' thesis conflicts with HumanUniqueness root-of-trust; the sybil-resistance narrative weakens if operator layer has no human anchor — position HumanUniqueness as optional but additive for Theseus's enterprise customers
Eric Wang's team is pre-Series A ($100K): engineering bandwidth for joint spec co-authorship may not exist for months — own the spec draft entirely, position Theseus as reviewer not co-author

Attestation format unknown: designing Poseidon4 with a placeholder hash field works but requires the IETF section to remain abstract about attestation_hash provenance until Theseus confirms their proving system

Solo bandwidth: AgentPolicy v2 + Delegation v2 circuit changes require re-running snarkjs setup for new PLONK keys (~30 min per circuit) — budget a half-day for artifact regeneration and test suite updates
spec.success_criteria: A Theseus testnet transaction that calls IdentityRegistry.verifyHandshake() with a two-proof tuple (AgentPolicy v2 PLONK proof, Delegation PLONK proof) where the AgentPolicy public inputs include a real Theseus execution attestation hash, and the transaction emits a HandshakeVerified event with the agent's permission bitmap and the attestation hash — provable end-to-end in under 30 seconds from proof generation to on-chain confirmation.
spec.theseus_dependencies: 1) Execution attestation schema/format (TEE quote structure, ZKP public outputs, or optimistic commitment spec) — needed before AgentPolicy v2 circuit is finalized. 2) Confirmation of VM architecture (EVM vs custom) — gates the contract adapter path entirely. 3) Testnet RPC endpoint + faucet access. 4) One engineering contact willing to review IETF draft section 7 and co-sign it for the Orange DAO narrative. 5) Clarity on whether Theseus plans an on-chain permission/delegation layer or expects off-chain solutions — determines whether IdentityRegistry deploys on Theseus L1 or stays on Base Sepolia with a relay.

tier3_findings: AXIS 1 (CONCERN): Theseus's 1-2 engineers cannot build a production-grade ZKP delegation circuit in 3 months, but they can absolutely implement a simpler solution — an on-chain ACL bitmap (agent address → uint8 permissions) deployed in Solidity takes 2 weeks and covers 80% of the stated need. The ZKP overhead is only justified if Theseus needs privacy around permission scope, selective disclosure portability, or cross-chain proof composability. None of these are documented Theseus requirements. The circomlib + snarkjs stack is well-documented enough that a motivated engineer could prototype AgentPolicy without Bolyra in 6-8 weeks, though without the cumulative-bit enforcement or trusted setup discipline.

AXIS 2 (FAIL): The ZKP privacy case is weak for a public L1. If Theseus is a transparent chain (which all L1s are by default), then the permission bits are already observable via on-chain state — ZKP selective disclosure hides nothing economically meaningful. The 'self-contained proof artifact' argument is valid for cross-chain portability, but Theseus is a single L1 — the proof doesn't need to travel anywhere. The nonce-binding anti-replay property is achievable with standard crypto (HMAC or a nonce registry). The only genuinely ZKP-exclusive property here is composability with Theseus's own execution attestation, but that integration spec is explicitly speculative because Theseus's attestation format (TEE? ZKP? optimistic?) is unknown.

AXIS 3 (CONCERN): Theseus is pre-mainnet, $100K funded, and the first operational agent use case requiring ZK authorization proof cannot be named from existing evidence. The 'no human key path' constraint is architectural but doesn't operationally require Bolyra in months 0-12 — raw EdDSA keypairs with simple on-chain ACLs are sufficient for a small fleet of test agents. The need for scope-narrowing delegation proofs only emerges when Theseus has agents spawning sub-agents at scale (>1,000), which is speculative given current funding.

AXIS 4 (FAIL): The integration risk is high and underbounded. Theseus's VM architecture is unknown — if non-EVM, deploying a Solidity Groth16 verifier is impossible without implementing a custom verifier from scratch in their native VM (months of work). The Poseidon3→Poseidon4 circuit modification requires recompilation, new phase-2 trusted setup ceremony, and fresh verifier contract deployment. The IdentityRegistry cross-chain work is explicitly flagged as 'major' in the Tier 2 rationale. Most critically: the core 'Theseus proves computation; Bolyra proves mandate' composability requires defining a joint attestation binding schema that neither team has specced, and Theseus's attestation format is unknown as of the Sunday call.

AXIS 5 (PASS): The whitepaper and IETF draft extension have standalone value independent of Theseus. The AgentPolicy and Delegation circuits are already built and reusable for any agent-native chain. A joint spec with Theseus accelerates Bolyra's standards positioning regardless of Theseus's survival. The work is not Theseus-specific — it produces artifacts (draft-bolyra-mutual-zkp-auth-01 amendment, cross-chain circuit binding schema) that serve the broader Bolyra roadmap.
tier3_summary: The narrative is compelling but premature — two hard FAIL axes (standard crypto suffices on a transparent L1; unknown VM makes integration underbounded) cap this at CONDITIONAL: plant the narrative seed Sunday, request Theseus's VM spec and a concrete agent delegation failure mode before committing any circuit work.
